title: Comprehensive Psychology Note
description: This note template covers many aspects of client presentation, using the following titles: Current Presentation, Past Medical & Psychiatric History,
prompt:
[presentation] Current Presentation
• Presenting complaint: primary reason client sought therapy this visit, how long issues have been occurring, key symptoms reported
• Severity/Impact: impact on work, daily activities, relationships, mood, motivation, coping capacity
Only include what is explicitly mentioned. Leave blank if not discussed.

[history] Past Medical & Psychiatric History
• Psychiatric Diagnoses: current or past diagnoses
• Medical History: relevant medical conditions
• Treatments/Hospitalisations: recent interventions or hospitalisations
• Current Medications: name, dose, frequency if mentioned
• Recent Substance Use: type, frequency, duration if mentioned
Omit any sub-item not evidenced in the transcript.

[mse] Mental Status Examination
Behaviour: [engagement, activity level, notable behaviours observed]
Speech: [pace only if notably fast or slow; omit if within normal range]
Mood: [client's self-described emotional state, use their own words where possible]
Affect: [range, appropriateness, congruence with stated mood]
Thoughts: [thought content and process; preoccupations, rumination, distortions, insight into thoughts]
Perceptions: [flashbacks, hallucinations, sensory re-experiencing, misinterpretations]
Cognition: [memory, orientation, concentration, comprehension]
Insight: [client's understanding of their own condition and symptoms]
Judgment: [decision-making, awareness of consequences of actions]
Omit sub-items not evidenced in the transcript.

[content] Session Content, Goals, Obstacles/Progress, and Interventions
Write as four clearly labelled sub-sections in this exact order:

Session Content:
• Significant topics discussed during the session
• Life events, milestones, or circumstances mentioned
• Key relationships: names, roles, and relevant interactions

Goals:
• Therapy goals or objectives discussed or reviewed this session
• New goals raised or existing goals updated
• Homework or tasks assigned by the therapist for between sessions

Obstacles, Setbacks and Progress:
• Progress the client has made toward therapy goals
• Setbacks, barriers, or obstacles to progress

Interventions:
• Specific therapeutic techniques used during the session (e.g. validation, psychoeducation, CBT strategies, grounding)
• Concepts, frameworks, or psychoeducation introduced; handouts or whiteboard content

[risk] Risk Assessment and Management
• Suicidal Ideation: [current or past ideation, plan, intent — or state "Denied" if explicitly denied]
• Homicidal Ideation: [include only if mentioned in transcript]
• Self-harm: [include only if mentioned in transcript]
• Violence & Aggression: [recent or past incidents if mentioned]
• Addictive Behaviours: [substance use, gambling, or other addictive behaviours if mentioned]
• Risk-taking/Impulsivity: [include only if mentioned in transcript]
• Management Plan: [steps taken to address any identified risks]
Omit any item not evidenced in the transcript.

[summary] Session Summary
Write one concise narrative paragraph summarising: the client's current clinical status, key content discussed this session, therapeutic interventions provided, risk assessment outcome, and progress toward therapy goals.

[nextsteps] Next Steps / Plan
• Next Session: date and time of next appointment if mentioned
• Assigned Homework: tasks or activities assigned to the client for before the next session

sections.label: Session Content, Goals, Obstacles/Progress, and Interventions

title: Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT)
description: This template focuses on the six core processes of Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT). For sessions conducted using the ACT framework, this
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by therapists working within an Acceptance and Commitment Therapy, or ACT, framework with clients who present with a variety of mental health issues. Using the transcript from the session, you will generate a detailed summary of the session from an Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) perspective. Ensure the summary is concise and adheres to ACT terminology. Ensure the language used reflects the compassionate, non-judgmental stance central to ACT. Organise the summary under the following headings.)

[client_presentation_and_context] Client Presentation and Context

* \[Briefly summarise the client’s presenting concerns or difficulties discussed in the session. Highlight any relevant changes to symptoms, emotional state, and any notable context or changes since the last session. Note the client's expressed values, goals, or current struggles relevant to their therapeutic journey. Describe the client’s progress since last session, including any notable changes in behaviour, mood, or functioning. Detail any client feedback or reflections on homework or between-session practice.]

[act_conceptualisation_of_problems] Act Conceptualisation of Problems

(For each circumstance discussed, use the following three sub-headings to provide a brief case conceptualisation using the ACT model: Cognitive Fusion, Experiential Avoidance, Unworkable Action:)

* \[**Cognitive Fusion:** Describe the thoughts the client is entangled with or perceives as truths rather than mental events. (Output the sub-heading only if relevant content was mentioned in the transcript, otherwise leave blank.)]
* \[**Experiential Avoidance:** Describe any attempts to suppress, escape, or avoid unwanted internal experiences, such as thoughts, emotions and sensations. (Output the sub-heading only if relevant content was mentioned in the transcript, otherwise leave blank.)]
* \[**Unworkable Action:** Describe behaviour driven by avoidance or fusion that moves the client away from their values or leads to increased suffering. (Output the sub-heading only if relevant content was mentioned in the transcript, otherwise leave blank.)]

[focus_of_the_session] Focus of the Session

* \[Identify the key areas of focus during the session (e.g., fostering acceptance, enhancing defusion, clarifying values, strengthening commitment to actions, identifying avoidance behaviours. summarise the techniques, metaphors, or experiential exercises used during the session (e.g., mindfulness exercises, defusion practices, values clarification activities). Describe the specific psychological flexibility processes targeted, such as acceptance, defusion, present-moment awareness, self-as-context, values clarification, or committed action. Use the following sub-headings which describe the six core therapeutic processes of ACT:]
* **Acceptance**: \[Describe the exploration of the client’s willingness to experience difficult thoughts and emotions.]
* **Cognitive Defusion**: \[Describe the techniques used to help the client observe thoughts as they are (e.g., as words or images) rather than as truths. If the client used language that suggested fusion with a thought, such as a declaration that a thought is a fact, quote it. If the client used language that suggests cognitive defusion from a thought, such as language like “I had the thought that”, which demonstrates distancing from the thought, quote that and underline the distancing language]
* **Contact With The Present Moment:** \[Describe the use of any mindfulness exercises, or discussions to help the client stay grounded in the here and now.]
* **Self-as-Context:** \[Describe any work that focused on fostering a sense of self that transcends thoughts, feelings, and experiences.]
* **Values:** \[Describe any clarification of the client’s core values and exploration of how to live in alignment with these values.]
  Committed Action: \[Describe steps the client is taking toward meaningful, values-based action, including problem-solving barriers.]

[therapeutic_interventions_summary] Therapeutic Interventions Summary

* \[In paragraph form and in no more than four sentences,, summarise the focus of the intervention, the goals of those strategies. Note how these interventions were tailored to the client’s needs and progress. Describe any discussions around acceptance or committed action.]

[client_s_responses_engagement_and_insigh] Client’s Responses, Engagement, and Insights

* \[Record the client’s reactions, reflections, and insights gained during the session, including the client’s emotional response to therapeutic interventions. Note evidence of increased psychological flexibility or movement toward valued living. Describe the level of rapport between therapist and client, including any resistance or challenges observed during the session.]

[progress_and_challenges] Progress and Challenges

* \[Describe progress made toward therapeutic goals, referencing any observable shifts in the client’s behaviour, thinking, or emotional responses. Highlight any barriers or challenges discussed and how these were approached collaboratively.]

[commitment_and_action_steps] Commitment and Action Steps

* \[Outline the specific actions or experiments the client committed to taking before the next session, and how these will align with their values. Include any agreed-upon homework or mindfulness practices. Detail any identified potential barriers to completing these tasks.  (only include if mentioned in the transcript otherwise leave blank)]

[reflection_and_plan] Reflection and Plan

* \[Summarise the clinician’s reflections on the session, including any modifications to the treatment plan or goals based on the client’s progress. Note the planned focus for the next session and any considerations for the therapeutic approach. Note the time, day and date of the next session if mentioned.] (only include if mentioned in the transcript, otherwise leave blank)

title: ADHD / Autism Diagnostic Report
description: A report that details the findings of a comprehensive ADHD and/or autism assessment. This report template is the perfect accompaniment for clinicians
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by clinicians who have conducted a comprehensive attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, also known as ADHD, and/or autism assessment. It will take the format of a detailed report aimed at a variety of health professionals and other key stakeholders. For example, it could be used to provide diagnostic advice to a general practitioner or medical specialist, or could be given to a government or academic institution as proof of an individual’s diagnosis. Therefore, the tone and language of the report must be formal and use language consistent with evidence-based psychiatric and psychological assessments. However, it is very important that your language and tone should also reflect a neurodiversity-affirming approach. The report will adhere to the following structure:)

\[Print the name of the recipient of the report.] (If the recipient’s name is not stated, just write "Recipient Name".)

\[Print the address of the recipient of the report.] (If the recipient’s address is not stated, just write "Recipient Address".)

Re: \[Print the client's first and last name.]

DOB: \[Print the client's date of birth in DD/MM/YYYY format.]

Address: \[Print the client's address. If unknown, leave blank.]

Dear \[Recipient name (using appropriate title if applicable).] (If the recipient’s name is not stated, just write "Recipient Name".),

The following report refers to a comprehensive ADHD / autism assessment of \[client's first and last name]. \[If the report is only focused on ADHD, then only print “ADHD” in the preceding sentence. If the report is only focused on autism, then only print “autism” in the preceding sentence. If the report is focused on both ADHD and autism, then print “ADHD / autism” as noted already in the preceding sentence.]

\[Provide a summary of the purpose of the assessment and the client’s associated presenting concerns. Describe any relevant personal information e.g., age and gender of client; cultural background; and any modifications or assistive measures required for the evaluation (such as the need for an interpreter) or other context that is relevant to the assessment. This information may come from people present in the assessment session other than the client.]

[information_sources] Information Sources

* \[List all the sources of information, including dates, clinical interviews, psychometric assessments.]
* \[List the documents and other sources of information by third parties provided to the clinician to inform the contents of the report (only include if explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, or transcripts, otherwise leave blank).]

[history] Personal and Developmental History

\[Provide a detailed summary of the client’s personal and developmental history. Include information such as early development, family history and structure, education and employment history, occupation, current living arrangements, and social support. Describe any history of trauma, including family or intergenerational trauma, or significant adversity, or any type of traumatic event. Describe any family history of any illness or medical condition, including psychiatric illness and neurodevelopmental conditions. Include any information about family suicides or family addiction behaviours (e.g., alcoholism, other drug abuse).] (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, or transcripts, otherwise leave blank.)

[medical_status_and_medical_history] Medical Status and Medical History

\[Describe previous non-psychiatric diagnoses and medical conditions, including information about when the diagnosis was made and by whom. Provide information given by the client that describes responses to treatment, such as effective outcomes, side effects, and adverse reactions.] (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, or transcripts, otherwise leave blank.)

[mental_health_history] Mental Health History

\[Describe previous mental health and psychiatric diagnoses and conditions, including information about when the diagnosis was made and by whom. Provide information given by the client that describes responses to treatment, such as effective outcomes, side effects, and adverse reactions. Describe any previous psychological interventions, including with therapists, online programs, or group programs. Describe any past hospitalisations for mental health reasons, including locations, names of key medical personnel involved, and dates of these hospitalisations.] (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, or transcripts, otherwise leave blank.)

[medications] Medication History

\[Describe all medications that the client is currently taking, including psychotropic and non-psychotropic. Include information about doses and frequencies. Describe any medication history that details information such as response to any past medication, side effects, and adverse reactions. Assess adherence to previous medication regimens.] (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, or transcripts, otherwise leave blank.)

[drug_and_alcohol_use_history] Drug and Alcohol Use History

\[Provide a detailed summary of the client’s drug and alcohol use history. Describe the client’s current level of alcohol consumption, cigarette or vape use, and describe if the client consumes any other recreational or illicit substance. Describe any past or current misuse of prescription medications.] (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, or transcripts, otherwise leave blank.)

[other_addiction_history] Other Addiction History

\[Provide a detailed summary of the client’s other addiction history. Describe any other potentially problematic addictive behaviours mentioned by the client (e.g., gambling, video gaming, Internet, social media, food, pornography). Describe this information even if the client does not view the behaviour as problematic or as an addiction.] (Only include if addictive behaviours were explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, or transcripts, otherwise leave blank.)

[legal_and_forensic_history] Legal and Forensic History

\[Provide a detailed summary of the client’s legal and forensic history. Describe any previous history mentioned by the client that involves illegal activity, interactions with the justice system, imprisonment, interactions with the family law system, or domestic violence behaviour.] (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, or transcripts, otherwise leave blank.)

[current_presentation_functional_assessme] Current Presentation / Functional Assessment

\[Provide a detailed summary of the client’s current presentation and current functioning. Include information related to the purpose of the ADHD and/or autism assessment, and the client’s presenting concerns e.g., type and nature of current symptoms; length of time with current symptoms; external stressors that are relevant to the client’s current presentation; and interpersonal or relationship factors that are relevant to the client’s current presentation. Include other information related to the impact on current functioning for the client (e.g., occupational, family, social, academic). Describe key lifestyle indicators that are potentially relevant to the purpose of this assessment and the client’s presenting concerns. Describe the client’s current sleep pattern and sleep hygiene. Describe the client’s current diet, including binge behaviours, caffeine intake, and consumption of junk food. Describe the client’s current level of physical exercise or general activity levels. Describe the client’s level of social functioning, including information about quality and quantity of social and family relationships, the client’s efforts at connecting socially with others, and the extent of any social isolation. In your descriptions, be sure that you have covered the client’s level of current functioning across multiple domains: daily living skills; social and interpersonal functioning; occupational or academic functioning; and self-care and independence.] (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, or transcripts, otherwise leave blank.)

[mse] Mental Status Examination (MSE)

\[Provide a narrative summary in less than four sentences of the client's mental status, and, where applicable, taking into account: Appearance (the client's general physical appearance, hygiene, grooming, and attire); behaviour (the client's interaction with their surrounds, activity level, psychomotor activity, eye contact, and rapport); speech (the rate, volume, tone, coherence, and articulation of the client's speech); mood and affect (the client's reported mood and emotional state, using their own words if possible, and the range and appropriateness of the client's emotional response during the appointment, noting any discrepancies with the client's stated mood; and any observations about emotional expression made by the clinician); thought process (the client's coherence, organisation, and flow of thoughts); thought content (the content of the client's thoughts, noting the presence of delusions, distortions, obsessions, or intrusive thoughts, if any); perception (hallucinations or other perceptual disturbances of the client, if any); cognition (the client's orientation to time / place / person, attention, memory, concentration, and comprehension); and insight and judgment (the client's understanding of their mental health presentation and their decision-making capacity, noting any lack of awareness or denial).] (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, or transcripts, otherwise leave blank.)

[strengths_coping_skills_recreational_int] Strengths, Coping Skills, Recreational Interests, and Hobbies

\[Provide a detailed summary of the client’s strengths, coping skills, recreational interests, and hobbies. Describe the client’s strengths, positive coping skills, recreational interests, hobbies, and other protective factors. Only highlight things that are accepted as helpful and useful in promoting a healthy lifestyle, and that are likely to assist in managing mental health. Do not list anything that is considered harmful to mental health (e.g., consuming alcohol, other addictive behaviour).] (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, or transcripts, otherwise leave blank.)

[scales] Psychometric Assessment

\[Provide a summary of any psychometric assessment results. For each test, start a new paragraph, and include: the name of the assessment, a brief description of the psychometric assessment, the date of test administration, and relevant purpose to the current ADHD and/or autism evaluation. Provide relevant quantitative results (e.g., scores, percentiles). Provide an interpretation of these results. Provide any implications for these results in the clinical context related to the current evaluation. (Repeat this process for as many assessments that were administered.) If no psychometric assessments were administered, leave this section blank.]

[formulation_impression] Formulation / Impression

\[Referring to ADHD-specific and/or autism-specific issues and factors, provide a detailed bio-psycho-social psychiatric formulation for the client's presenting concerns, including predisposing, precipitating, perpetuating and protective factors, and the impact of these on the client's overall presentation. For ADHD and/or autism presentations, it is important to note the genetic predisposition for these conditions. Include evidence from the session notes, contextual notes, psychometric assessments; and transcripts to enhance the details of your formulation.]

[diagnosis] Diagnosis

\[Describe the clinician's ADHD and/or autism diagnostic impressions and observations, based on DSM-5-TR and ICD-11 criteria. Provide the rationale given by the clinician for this diagnosis (or diagnoses), including details of relevant criterion that the client meets for such a diagnosis, and the results of psychometric testing which may provide evidence for or against a diagnosis being applied. Include the diagnostic code of any such diagnoses. Describe if the clinician has stated that these diagnoses are new or confirmation of diagnoses previously given. Detail any comorbidities or differential diagnosis considerations provided by the clinician. Outline any relevant cultural, context, or trauma-informed considerations.]

[risk] Risk

\[Based on information in the session notes, contextual notes, and transcripts, make a risk assessment about the client in a statement (e.g., suicidal or homicidal ideation, self-harm or harm to others, and risk of relapse, if any). Include any information related to past suicide attempts or past self-harming behaviour. Include any information about psychosocial stressors contributing to the level of risk. Include any information about protective factors that mitigate the risk.]

[nextsteps] Recommendations

* \[Describe any recommendations made by the clinician. Such information may include the following:]
* \[Specify if the clinician has made any evidence-based recommendations for medication reviews or changes. Provide the clinician's rationale for these recommendations. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* \[Specify if the clinician has made any suggested non-pharmacological interventions, such as psychotherapy or lifestyle modifications. Provide the clinician's rationale for these recommendations. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* \[Specify if the clinician has made or suggested any referrals to other specialists or services. Provide the clinician's rationale for these recommendations. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* \[Specify any diagnostic-specific based recommendations that were discussed, including ways to manage the diagnosis or general recommendations that were discussed around the diagnosis. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* \[Specify any lifestyle or personal decision-based recommendations that were discussed. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* \[Specify if the clinician has suggested any workplace or academic modifications. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* \[Specify if the clinician has suggested any plans for follow-up and monitoring (e.g., next appointment details). If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* \[Detail if any Safety Plan has been made by the clinician in the event that the client poses a risk to safety, including steps to take in a crisis. If the clinician has not made any Safety Plan, leave blank.]

[summary] Summary

\[Provide a paragraph summary of the ADHD and/or autism evaluation in less than six sentences, including a summary of the clinician’s formulation and diagnosis. If applicable, describe the clinician’s clinically informed opinion about the likely course of the presenting condition(s), including: factors influencing the prognosis; likelihood of improvement with recommended interventions; and any limitations to prognostic certainty.]

If you have any questions about the contents of this report, please feel free to contact me.

Yours sincerely,

\[Clinician Name.] (If the clinician's name is not stated, just write "Clinician Name".)

sections.label: Strengths, Coping Skills, Recreational Interests, and Hobbies

title: ADHD Clinical Interview for Adult Diagnosis
description: For clinicians who interview an adult to assess for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), this template provides a concise summary of
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by a clinician who is interviewing an adult client as part of a clinical investigation into whether the adult client meets criteria for a diagnosis of attention deficit hyperactivity disorder according to the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 5th Edition, Text Revision, also known as DSM-5-TR, and the International Classification for Diseases, 11th Edition, also known as ICD-11. Analyse the transcript and provide responses according to the instructions in the section headings listed below.)

[referrals] Referral Reason

[Describe the client’s reasons for seeking the attention deficit hyperactivity disorder assessment. Describe the symptoms, behaviours, events, and situations that have led the client to be assessed for attention deficit hyperactivity disorder. Describe when the onset of specific issues occurred, and how the specific issues have changed over time. Describe the impact on the client’s daily functioning, such as at home, with family, at work, in education environments, with social relationships, with hobbies, and in any other area of the client’s life. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate any of the client’s specific issues.]

[people_present] People Present

* [List the person or people who were in attendance during the interview, other than the clinician. Identify who the client is. State what the relationship is of the client to any other person in attendance (e.g., partner, carer, other relative, case worker).]

[mse] Mental Status Examination (MSE)

[Provide a short paragraph using language consistent with accepted psychiatric and medical terms of no more than four sentences that summarises the client’s presentation using the following standard MSE domains (only include each domain if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual note, otherwise leave blank): Appearance (the client’s general physical appearance, hygiene, grooming, and attire); Behaviour (the client’s interaction with their surrounds, activity level, psychomotor activity, eye contact, and rapport; including any hyperactive behaviour or behaviour that is related to the client’s concentration abilities); Speech (the rate, volume, tone, coherence, and articulation of the client’s speech; including if the client communicates in any other non-verbal way, such as with gestures, noises, or eye contact); Mood and Affect (including the client’s reported mood and emotional state; including the range and appropriateness of the client’s emotional response during the appointment, and note any discrepancies with the client’s stated mood; including any observations about emotional expression made by the clinician; including any behaviours that provide insight into mood and affect, such as anger outbursts, withdrawal, and laughing; including any other factors that may be providing insight into the client’s mood or affect, such as expressions of illness, or other interactions with the clinician; including the response of the client to the use of any visual aids regarding mood or affect, such as mood scales); Thought Process (the client’s coherence, organisation, and flow of thoughts; including any magical / fantasy thinking; including any “flight of ideas” type thinking that may indicate that the client’s speech is unable to keep up with their thought processes); Thought Content (the content of the client’s thoughts, and note the presence of delusions, distortions, obsessions, or intrusive thoughts, if any; including any tangential thinking that the client is having); Perception (hallucinations or other perceptual disturbances of the client, if any; including if the client indicates that these experiences are distressing or impairing); Cognition (the client’s orientation to time / place / person, attention, memory, concentration, and comprehension; including the results of any stated cognitive assessment that has previously been carried out on the client; including the client’s attention and concentration abilities); and Insight and Judgment (the client's understanding of their own difficulties and challenges, noting any lack of awareness or denial).]

[history] Background

[personal_and_developmental_history] Personal and Developmental History

[Describe as much detail as possible related to the client’s personal and developmental history. Include information related to the client’s early development, such as milestones and delays, such as crawling, walking, speech, and toileting; and emotional development. Describe the client’s education history, such as academic performance, school behaviour, being the victim or perpetrator of past bullying, and willingness to attend school. Describe the client’s gross and fine motor skills (e.g., handwriting, bike riding, ball skills).]

[Describe the client’s family history and structure, current living arrangements (including the names of all people known to live at home with the client and their relationship to the client), and social support. If relevant, include any collateral information about siblings or other family members of the client.]

[Describe any history of trauma, including family or intergenerational trauma, or significant adversity or any type of traumatic event.]

[Describe any family history of any illness, medical condition, psychiatric illness, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, autism, and other neurodevelopmental conditions. Include any information about family suicides or family addiction behaviours, such as alcoholism, and other drug abuse.]

[Describe the current circumstances of the client. Include information such as type of employment or education currently engaged in; current marital status; current friendships and other relationships; quality and satisfaction with relationships; current daily routine; and the current level of insight that the client has about the impact of symptoms on their everyday functioning. Describe any support that the client currently receives from any government or non-government source.]

[previous_assessments] Previous Assessments

[Describe the results of any previous psychological, cognitive, or medical testing. Detail when any such assessments occurred, the name of the practitioner that administered the assessment, and the profession of the practitioner who administered the assessment. If no information about previous assessments is mentioned in the transcript, leave this section out.]

[medical_history] Medical History

[Describe any previous medical, psychiatric, or neurodevelopmental diagnoses. Include information such as who made the diagnosis, their profession, and the approximate date that the diagnosis was given. If no information about medical history is mentioned in the transcript, leave this section out.]

[medications] Current Medications

[Describe all the current medications that the client is taking, both psychotropic and non-psychotropic. Include information on doses and frequencies, and provide the reason why the client is on each medication. If no information about current medications is mentioned in the transcript, leave this section out.]

[other_previous_treatment] Other Previous Treatment

[Describe any previous non-medication interventions that the client has participated in. Include things such as psychological therapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, and any other treatments that have been conducted by a registered health practitioner. If no information about other previous treatment is mentioned in the transcript, leave this section out.]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria A1

[inattention_five_or_more_of_the_followin] Inattention – five or more of the following are required

#### A

[Describe if the client often fails to give close attention to details or makes careless mistakes in academic work, at work, or during other activities (e.g., overlooks or misses details, work is inaccurate). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### B

[Describe if the client often has difficulty sustaining attention in tasks or leisure activities (e.g., has difficulty remaining focused during lectures, conversations, or lengthy reading). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### C

[Describe if the client often does not seem to listen when spoken to directly (e.g., mind seems elsewhere, even in the absence of any obvious distraction). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### D

[Describe if the client often does not follow through on instructions and fails to finish academic work, chores, or duties in the workplace (e.g., starts tasks but quickly loses focus and is easily sidetracked). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### E

[Describe if the client often has difficulty organising tasks and activities (e.g., difficulty managing sequential tasks; difficulty keeping materials and belongings in order; messy, disorganised work; has poor time management; fails to meet deadlines). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### F

[Describe if the client often avoids, dislikes, or is reluctant to engage in tasks that require sustained mental effort (e.g., academic work, preparing reports, completing forms, reviewing lengthy papers). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### G

[Describe if the client often loses things necessary for tasks or activities (e.g., learning materials, pencils, books, tools, wallets, keys, paperwork, eyeglasses, mobile telephones). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### H

[Describe if the client is often easily distracted by extraneous stimuli (including having unrelated thoughts). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### I

[Describe if the client is often forgetful in daily activities (e.g., doing chores, running errands, returning calls, paying bills, keeping appointments). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria A2

#### Hyperactivity and Impulsivity – five or more of the following are required

#### A

[Describe if the client often fidgets with, or taps hands or feet, or squirms in seat. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### B

[Describe if the client often leaves seat in situations when remaining seated is expected (e.g., leaves his or her place in the classroom, in the office, or other workplace, or in other situations that require remaining in place). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### C

[Describe if the client often runs about or climbs in situations where it is inappropriate. Describe if the client says they often feel restless. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### D

[Describe if the client is often unable to play or engage in leisure activities quietly. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### E

[Describe if the client is often “on the go”, acting as if “driven by a motor” (e.g., is unable to be or uncomfortable being still for extended time, as in restaurants, meetings; may be experienced by others as being restless or difficult to keep up with. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### F

[Describe if the client often talks excessively. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### G

[Describe if the client often blurts out an answer before a question has been completed (e.g., completes people’s sentences; cannot wait for their turn in conversation). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### H

[Describe if the client often has difficulty waiting his or her turn (e.g., while waiting in line). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### I

[Describe if the client often interrupts or intrudes on others (e.g., butts into conversations, games, or activities; may start using other people’s things without asking or receiving permission; may intrude into or take over what others are doing.) Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria B

[Describe in one or two sentences if several of the inattentive or hyperactive-impulsive symptoms were present prior to the age of 12. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria C

[Describe in one or two sentences if several of the inattentive or hyperactive-impulsive symptoms are present in two or more settings (e.g., at home, academic setting, or work; with friends or relatives; in other activities). If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria D

[Describe in two or three sentences if there is clear evidence that the inattentive or hyperactive-impulsive symptoms interfere with, or reduce the quality of, social, academic, or occupational functioning. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria E

[Print this statement:] Before applying a diagnosis of ADHD, the clinician must ensure that the symptoms do not occur exclusively during the course of schizophrenia or another psychotic disorder and are not better explained by another mental disorder such as mood disorder, anxiety disorder, dissociative disorder, personality disorder, substance intoxication or withdrawal.

[strengths_interests_and_hobbies] Strengths, Interests, and Hobbies

[Describe the client’s strengths, interests, and hobbies. Detail any unique skills that the client has, or any characteristics highlighted by the clinician or anyone else as being above average, desirable, or beneficial. Detail any of the client’s abilities described as gifted or advanced in comparison to others. Describe any of the client’s personality traits that the clinician or anyone else has highlighted as being desirable, advantageous or in some way positive.]

[client_goals] Client Goals

[Describe all the ways that the client would like to improve. According to the client, describe what it would take for them to flourish. This may include improved ability to form friendships; improved employment or academic outcomes; improvements to the client’s mood; improved ability to regulate other emotions such as anxiety and anger; etc.]

[clinician_observations_notes_and_diagnos] Clinician Observations, Notes, and Diagnostic Impressions

[Describe any other observations or notes made by the clinician during the interview. This may include information related to their assessment, comments on anything that the client has said, comments in response to anything the client has done, or any other clinically relevant information provided by the clinician in the transcript. If provided, describe the clinician’s diagnosis, explanation of the client’s cognitive or neurodivergent profile, and observations about comorbidities, differential diagnosis, and co-occurring traits such as anxiety, trauma, executive dysfunction.]

[risk] Risk

[Based on clinician comments in the transcript, make a risk assessment in a statement (e.g., suicidal or homicidal ideation, self-harm, or harm to others, if any). Include any information related to past suicide attempts or past self-harming behaviour. Include any information about psychosocial stressors contributing to the level of risk. Describe any risk for social isolation. Describe any risk for being the victim or perpetrator of bullying, and include any stated history of bullying. Describe any protective factors that mitigate the risk.]

[nextsteps] Next Steps

* [Specify if any evidence-based recommendations have been made by the clinician for further referral to a psychiatrist or other specialist regarding a medication review for the client. If the clinician is a qualified prescribing doctor, specify any evidence-based recommendations for new or existing medication, and specify if any existing medications should be continued or discontinued. Provide the clinician’s rationale for these recommendations. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* [Specify any suggested non-pharmacological interventions made by the clinician, such as psychotherapy or lifestyle modifications. Provide the clinician’s rationale for these recommendations. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* [Specify if any referrals to other specialist services have been or will be made by the clinician (e.g., lab tests). Provide the clinician’s rationale for these recommendations. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* [Detail what the plan is for ongoing assessment, follow-up, and monitoring, as detailed by the clinician. If the clinician has not made any plans for ongoing assessment, follow-up, or monitoring, leave blank.]
* [Detail if any Safety Plan has been made by the clinician in the event that the client poses a risk to safety, including steps to take in a crisis. Document the client’s understanding of the Safety Plan. If the clinician has not made any Safety Plan, leave blank.]
* [Summarise any education provided by the clinician to the client about the client’s ongoing assessment, diagnosis, and treatment options. Document the client’s understanding and agreement with this education. If the clinician has not provided any education to the client about the ongoing assessment, diagnosis, and treatment options, leave blank.]
* [List the details of any future appointment between the clinician and the client, including the time, day, and date.]

[summary] Summary

[Provide a brief paragraph summary of the appointment with the client in no more than four sentences.]

[END TEMPLATE.]

sections.label: Inattention – five or more of the following are required
Clinician Observations, Notes, and Diagnostic Impressions

title: ADHD Clinical Interview With Parent / Carer
description: For clinicians who interview a parent or carer of a child suspected of having attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), this template provides
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by a clinician who is interviewing a parent or carer as part of a clinical investigation into whether their child meets criteria for a diagnosis of attention deficit hyperactivity disorder according to the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 5th Edition, Text Revision, also known as DSM-5-TR, and the International Classification for Diseases, 11th Edition, also known as ICD-11. Analyse the transcript and provide responses according to the instructions in the section headings below. Use professional, clinically appropriate language that is congruent with the language used in the DSM-5-TR and ICD-11.)

[referrals] Referral Reason

[Describe the parent or carer’s reasons for seeking the attention deficit hyperactivity disorder assessment. Describe the symptoms, behaviours, events, and situations that have led the parent or carer to have the child assessed for attention deficit hyperactivity disorder. Describe when the onset of specific issues occurred, and how the specific issues have changed over time. Describe the impact on the child’s daily functioning, such as at home, with family, at school, with social relationships, with hobbies, and in any other area of the child’s life. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate any of the child’s specific issues.]

[people_present] People Present

* [List the person or people who were in attendance during the interview, other than the clinician. State what the relationship of the person or people are to the child (e.g., parent, carer, other relative, case worker). Use bullet points.]

[State whether the child being assessed was in attendance or not.]

[mse] Mental Status Examination (MSE)

[If the child being assessed is not in attendance during the interview, do not include this section.]

[If the child being assessed is in attendance during the interview, do include this section.]

[Provide a short paragraph of no more than four sentences using language consistent with accepted psychiatric and medical terms, and taking into account the child's developmental stage, that summarises the client’s presentation using the following standard MSE domains (only include if each domain is explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual note, otherwise leave blank): Appearance (the child’s general physical appearance, hygiene, grooming, and attire); Behaviour (the child’s interaction with their surrounds, activity level, psychomotor activity, eye contact, and rapport; including information about the child’s interactions with any toys, creative behaviours such as drawing, and how the parent or carer has influenced the child’s behaviour in the session; including any hyperactive behaviour or behaviour that is related to the child’s concentration abilities); Speech (the rate, volume, tone, coherence, and articulation of the child’s speech; including if the child communicates in any other non-verbal way, such as with gestures, noises, or eye contact); Mood and Affect (the child’s reported mood and emotional state; including the range and appropriateness of the child’s emotional response during the appointment, and note any discrepancies with the child’s stated mood; including any observations about emotional expression made by the clinician; including any behaviours that provide insight into mood and affect, such as tantrums, withdrawal, and laughing; including any other factors that may be providing insight into the child’s mood or affect, such as expressions of illness, contents of drawings and other creative play, and interactions with the clinician, or parent or carer; including the response of the child to the use of any visual aids regarding mood or affect, such as mood scales); Thought Process (the child’s coherence, organisation, and flow of thoughts; including any magical / fantasy thinking, the child referring to any imaginary friends, or the child’s belief in any supernatural beings such as Santa Claus, which does not have to indicate the presence of psychotic thinking, but may match the child’s developmental stage and / or how the child is responding to any external life events; including any “flight of ideas” type thinking that may indicate that the child’s speech is unable to keep up with their thought processes); Thought Content (the content of the child’s thoughts, and note the presence of delusions, distortions, obsessions, or intrusive thoughts, if any; including any magical / fantasy thinking, the child referring to any imaginary friends, or the child’s belief in any supernatural beings such as Santa Claus, which does not have to indicate the presence of psychotic thinking, but may match the child’s developmental stage and / or how the child is responding to any external life events; including any tangential thinking that the child is having); Perception (hallucinations or other perceptual disturbances of the child, if any; including if the child indicates that these experiences are distressing, impairing, or inconsistent with typical play themes for a child of similar age; including the presence of any stated nightmares or night terrors, and distinguish these from any mistaken belief that they are considered to be perceptual disturbances); Cognition (the child’s orientation to time / place / person, attention, memory, concentration, and comprehension; including the results of any stated cognitive assessment that has previously been carried out on the child; including the child’s attention and concentration abilities); and Insight and Judgment (the child's understanding of their own difficulties and challenges, noting any age-appropriate lack of awareness or denial).]

[history] Background

[personal_and_developmental_history] Personal and Developmental History

[Describe as much detail as possible related to the child’s personal and developmental history. Include information related to the child’s early development, such as issues with pregnancy and birth; milestones and delays, such as crawling, walking, speech and toileting; and emotional development. Describe the child’s education history, such as academic performance, school behaviour, being the victim or perpetrator of past bullying, and willingness to attend school. Describe the child’s gross and fine motor skills (e.g., handwriting, bike riding, ball skills).]

[Describe the child’s family history and structure, current living arrangements (including the names of all people known to live at home with the child and their relationship to the child), and social support. If relevant, include any collateral information about siblings or other family members living at home with the child.]

[Describe any history of trauma, including family or intergenerational trauma, or significant adversity or any type of traumatic event.]

[Describe any family history of any illness, medical condition, psychiatric illness, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, autism, and other neurodevelopmental conditions. Include any information about family suicides or family addiction behaviours, such as alcoholism, and other drug abuse.]

[Describe the current circumstances of the child. Include information such as name and place of current school; current school grade; current status of friendships; current daily routine; and the current level of insight that the child has about the impact of symptoms on their everyday functioning.]

[previous_assessments] Previous Assessments

[Describe the results of any previous psychological, cognitive, or medical testing. Detail when any such assessments occurred, the name of the practitioner that administered the assessment, and the profession of the practitioner who administered the assessment. If no information about previous assessments is mentioned in the transcript, leave this section out.]

[medical_history] Medical History

[Describe any previous medical, psychiatric, or neurodevelopmental diagnoses. Include information such as who made the diagnosis, their profession, and the approximate date that the diagnosis was given. If no information about medical history is mentioned in the transcript, leave this section out.]

[medications] Current Medications

[Describe all the current medications that the child is taking, both psychotropic and non-psychotropic. Include information on doses and frequencies, and provide the reason why the child is on each medication. If no information about current medications is mentioned in the transcript, leave this section out.]

[other_previous_treatment] Other Previous Treatment

[Describe any previous non-medication interventions that the child has participated in. Include things such as psychological therapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, and any other treatments that have been conducted by a registered health practitioner. If no information about other previous treatment is mentioned in the transcript, leave this section out.]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria A1

[inattention_six_or_more_of_the_following] Inattention – six or more of the following are required

#### A

[Describe if the child often fails to give close attention to details or makes careless mistakes in schoolwork, at work, or during other activities (e.g., overlooks or misses details, work is inaccurate). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### B

[Describe if the child often has difficulty sustaining attention in tasks or play activities (e.g., has difficulty remaining focused during lectures, conversations, or lengthy reading). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### C

[Describe if the child often does not seem to listen when spoken to directly (e.g., mind seems elsewhere, even in the absence of any obvious distraction). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### D

[Describe if the child often does not follow through on instructions and fails to finish schoolwork, chores, or duties in the workplace (e.g., starts tasks but quickly loses focus and is easily sidetracked). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### E

[Describe if the child often has difficulty organising tasks and activities (e.g., difficulty managing sequential tasks; difficulty keeping materials and belongings in order; messy, disorganised work; has poor time management; fails to meet deadlines). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### F

[Describe if the child often avoids, dislikes, or is reluctant to engage in tasks that require sustained mental effort (e.g., school work or homework; for older adolescents and adults, preparing reports, completing forms, reviewing lengthy papers). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### G

[Describe if the child often loses things necessary for tasks or activities (e.g., school materials, pencils, books, tools, wallets, keys, paperwork, eyeglasses, mobile telephones). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### H

[Describe if the child is often easily distracted by extraneous stimuli (for older adolescents and adults, this may include unrelated thoughts). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### I

Describe if the child is often forgetful in daily activities (e.g., doing chores, running errands; for older adolescents and adults, returning calls, paying bills, keeping appointments). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria A2

#### Hyperactivity and Impulsivity – six or more of the following are required

#### A

[Describe if the child often fidgets with, or taps hands or feet, or squirms in seat. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### B

[Describe if the child often leaves seat in situations when remaining seated is expected (e.g., leaves his or her place in the classroom, in the office, or other workplace, or in other situations that require remaining in place). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### C

[Describe if the child often runs about or climbs in situations where it is inappropriate. Describe if the child says they often feel restless. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### D

[Describe if the child is often unable to play or engage in leisure activities quietly. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### E

[Describe if the child is often “on the go”, acting as if “driven by a motor” (e.g., is unable to be or uncomfortable being still for extended time, as in restaurants, meetings; may be experienced by others as being restless or difficult to keep up with. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### F

[Describe if the child often talks excessively. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### G

[Describe if the child often blurts out an answer before a question has been completed (e.g., completes people’s sentences; cannot wait for their turn in conversation). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### H

[Describe if the child often has difficulty waiting his or her turn (e.g., while waiting in line). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### I

[Describe if the child often interrupts or intrudes on others (e.g., butts into conversations, games, or activities; may start using other people’s things without asking or receiving permission; for adolescents and adults, may intrude into or take over what others are doing). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria B

[Describe in one or two sentences if several of the inattentive or hyperactive-impulsive symptoms were present prior to the age of 12. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria C

[Describe in one or two sentences if several of the inattentive or hyperactive-impulsive symptoms are present in two or more settings (e.g., at home, school, or work; with friends or relatives; in other activities). If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria D

[Describe in two or three sentences if there is clear evidence that the inattentive or hyperactive-impulsive symptoms interfere with, or reduce the quality of, social, academic, or occupational functioning. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria E

[Print this statement:] Before applying a diagnosis of ADHD, the clinician must ensure that the symptoms do not occur exclusively during the course of schizophrenia or another psychotic disorder and are not better explained by another mental disorder such as mood disorder, anxiety disorder, dissociative disorder, personality disorder, substance intoxication or withdrawal.

[strengths_interests_and_hobbies] Strengths, Interests, and Hobbies

[Describe the child’s strengths, interests, and hobbies. Detail any unique skills that the child has, or any characteristics highlighted by the clinician or parent or carer as being above average, desirable, or beneficial. Detail any of the child’s abilities described as gifted or advanced in comparison to same-aged peers. Describe any of the child’s personality traits that the clinician or parent or carer state as being desirable, advantageous or in some way positive.]

[parent_carer_goals] Parent / Carer Goals

[Describe all the ways that the parent or carer would like to see the child improve. According to the parent or carer, what would it take for the child to flourish. This may include improved ability to form friendships; improved academic engagement and performance; improvements to the child’s mood; improved ability to regulate other emotions such as anxiety and anger.]

[clinician_observations_notes_and_diagnos] Clinician Observations, Notes, and Diagnostic Impressions

[Describe any other observations or notes made by the clinician during the interview. This may include information related to their assessment, comments on anything that the parent or carer has said, comments in response to anything the child has done, or any other clinically relevant information provided by the clinician in the transcript. If provided, describe the clinician’s diagnosis, explanation of the child’s cognitive or neurodivergent profile, and observations about comorbidities, differential diagnosis, and co-occurring traits such as anxiety, trauma, executive dysfunction.]

[risk] Risk

[Based on clinician comments in the transcript, make a risk assessment in a statement (e.g., suicidal or homicidal ideation, self-harm, or harm to others, if any). Include any information related to past suicide attempts or past self-harming behaviour. Include any information about psychosocial stressors contributing to the level of risk. Describe any risk for social isolation. Describe any risk for being the victim or perpetrator of bullying, and include any stated history of bullying. Describe any protective factors that mitigate the risk.]

[nextsteps] Next Steps

* [Specify if any evidence-based recommendations have been made by the clinician for further referral to a paediatrician, psychiatrist, or other specialist regarding a medication review for the child. If the clinician is a qualified prescribing doctor, specify any evidence-based recommendations for new or existing medication, and specify if any existing medications should be continued or discontinued. Provide the clinician’s rationale for these recommendations. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* [Specify any suggested non-pharmacological interventions made by the clinician, such as psychotherapy or lifestyle modifications. Provide the clinician’s rationale for these recommendations. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* [Specify if any referrals to other specialist services have been or will be made by the clinician (e.g., lab tests). Provide the clinician’s rationale for these recommendations. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* [Detail what the plan is for ongoing assessment, follow-up, and monitoring, as detailed by the clinician. If the clinician has not made any plans for ongoing assessment, follow-up, or monitoring, leave blank.]
* [Detail if any Safety Plan has been made by the clinician in the event that the child poses a risk to safety, including steps to take in a crisis. Document the parent or carer’s understanding of the Safety Plan. If the clinician has not made any Safety Plan, leave blank.]
* [Summarise any education provided by the clinician to the parent or carer about the child’s ongoing assessment, diagnosis, and treatment options. Document the parent or carer’s understanding and agreement with this education. If the clinician has not provided any education to the parent or carer about the ongoing assessment, diagnosis, and treatment options, leave blank.]
* [List the details of any future appointment between the clinician and the parent or carer, or the child, including the time, day, and date.]

[summary] Summary

[Provide a brief paragraph summary of the appointment with the parent or carer in no more than four sentences.]

[END TEMPLATE.]
