(If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **Comments on Others' Emotions (B6):** [Describe the individual's communication of their recognition, understanding, and response to the feelings of other people or characters, e.g., spontaneously communicated a clear understanding or labelling of, or appropriate response to, several different emotions in others or characters / communicated some understanding, labelling, or response to an emotion in at least one other person or character / little or no understanding of emotion in others. Note that shared enjoyment with the clinician is not coded here as it is rated separately. Note relevant examples from activities such as Emotions, Cartoons, or Creating a Story. (If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **Insight into Typical Social Situations and Relationships (B7):** [Describe the individual's understanding of the nature of social relationships and their own role within them, e.g., showed examples of insight into several typical social relationships including their role in at least one, with no more than one example of inaccurate understanding / showed insight into several typical social relationships but not into their own role, or showed insight into only one relationship including their role / showed some insight into one typical social relationship, though not necessarily about their own role / showed no or limited insight into typical social relationships. Note relevant responses from activities such as Friends, Relationships, and Marriage; Loneliness; Social Difficulties and Annoyance; and Plans and Hopes. (If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **Responsibility (B8):** [Describe the degree to which the individual presented as responsible for their own actions in typical daily living situations, responding actively and independently to normal social expectations, e.g., described themselves as responsible for their own actions across several contexts including dealing with minor problems in daily living / provided at least one clear indication of personal responsibility but inconsistent across contexts, with no more than one clear example of a lack of responsibility / showed limited indication of a sense of responsibility for their own actions, or showed more than one clear example of lack of responsibility appropriate for their chronological age taking into account developmental level. Note relevant responses from activities such as Current Work or School and Daily Living. (If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **Quality of Social Overtures (B9):** [Describe the quality of the individual's attempts to initiate social interaction with the clinician, e.g., effectively used non-verbal and verbal means to make clear, contextually appropriate social overtures / slightly unusual quality of some social overtures, or overtures restricted to personal demands or related to the individual's interests but with some attempt to involve the clinician / inappropriate overtures lacking integration into context or social quality, including bringing up preoccupations with little attempt to involve the clinician / no social overtures of any kind. Note that this item focuses on the quality of social overtures, not their frequency, and that the rating should reflect the majority of social overtures rather than merely the best ones. (If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **Amount of Social Overtures and Attention Attempts (B10):** [Describe the frequency of the individual's attempts to get, maintain, or direct the clinician's attention, including to objects, actions, or topics of interest to the individual, e.g., frequent attempts to get or maintain the clinician's attention or to direct the clinician's attention to objects, actions, or topics of interest / some such attempts but reduced in frequency or range of activities / occasional attempts to get, maintain, or direct the clinician's attention, including overtures solely related to preoccupations / showed relatively little concern about whether the clinician was paying attention unless needing help. Note that requests are not included in this rating except at the lowest level. (If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **Quality of Social Response (B11):** [Describe how the individual responded to the clinician's social bids, e.g., showed a range of appropriate responses varied according to immediate social situations and pressures / showed responsiveness to most social contexts but somewhat limited, socially awkward, inappropriate, inconsistent, or consistently negative / odd or stereotyped responses, or responses restricted in range or inappropriate to context / minimal or no response to the clinician's attempts to engage the individual. (If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **Amount of Reciprocal Social Communication (B12):** [Describe the overall level of back-and-forth social engagement across the session, e.g., extensive use of verbal or non-verbal behaviours for social interchange including chat, comments, remarks, or non-verbal behaviours with reciprocal intent / some reciprocal social communication but reduced in frequency, amount, or range of contexts / most communication was object-oriented, responsive to questions only, echolalic, or focused on preoccupations with little or no social give-and-take / little or no communication with the clinician. (If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **Overall Quality of Rapport (B13):** [Describe the nature of the relationship established between the individual and the clinician across the session, e.g., comfortable interaction appropriate to the context of the assessment / interaction sometimes comfortable but not sustained, feeling awkward or stilted at times, or the individual's behaviour seeming mechanical or slightly off / one-sided or unusual interaction resulting in a consistently mildly uncomfortable session that would have been difficult without the clinician continuously modifying the structure beyond standard activities / the individual showed minimal regard for the clinician, or the session was markedly uncomfortable for a significant proportion of the time. (If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **IMAGINATION AND CREATIVITY** **Imagination and Creativity (C1):** [Describe evidence of creative and inventive thinking observed across the session, either in the individual's use of objects or through verbal descriptions, e.g., demonstrated several spontaneous, inventive, and creative activities or comments in conversation / showed some creative or make-believe actions but limited in range or occurring only in response to one structured situation (e.g., Creating a Story) / little spontaneous creative or make-believe contribution, or only actions that were repetitive or stereotyped in quality / no creative or inventive actions. Note relevant observations from activities such as Telling a Story From a Book, Cartoons, and Creating a Story. (If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **STEREOTYPED BEHAVIOURS AND RESTRICTED INTERESTS** **Unusual Sensory Interest in Play, Materials, or People (D1):** [Describe any atypical sensory exploration by the individual, e.g., no unusual sensory interests or sensory-seeking behaviours / several possible sensory interests, or one clear occurrence of an unusual sensory interest or sensory-seeking behaviour / definite interest in sensory elements of objects or materials, or sensory examination of themselves or others, with two or more clear occurrences / unusual sensory-seeking behaviours occurring frequently across at least two different tasks or activities, potentially interfering with the assessment. Note that sensory aversions are not coded here, and that looking into a mirror is not considered an unusual sensory interest. (If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **Hand and Finger and Other Complex Mannerisms (D2):** [Describe any unusual or repetitive movements or posturing of the hands, fingers, arms, or body, e.g., no unusual mannerisms observed / possible or occasional unusual repetitive hand, finger, or complex mannerisms / definite finger flicking or twisting, or hand, finger, or complex mannerisms, stereotypies, or posturing that, while possibly brief or infrequent, were clear / mannerisms occurring frequently across at least two different tasks or activities. Note that finger tapping, nail-biting, hair twisting, and thumb sucking are not coded here, and body rocking is only coded if it involves more than the torso. (If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **Self-Injurious Behaviour (D3):** [Describe any behaviours involving any kind of aggressive act against the individual themselves, even if not harmful, e.g., no attempts to harm self / dubious or possible self-injury, or one rare but clear example (e.g., biting one's hand, pulling one's hair, slapping one's face, or banging one's head) / more than one clear example of self-injury such as head banging, face slapping, hair pulling, or self-biting. (If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **Excessive Interest in or References to Unusual or Highly Specific Topics or Objects, or Repetitive Behaviours (D4):** [Describe any intense, narrow, or unusual interests and repetitive behaviours, e.g., no excessive interest in or references to unusual, specific, or restricted topics, objects, or repetitive behaviours / occasional references to unusual or highly specific topics or patterns of interest occurring to a remarkable degree, or occasional repetitive behaviours / definite or stereotyped patterns of interest that may intrude on or interfere with social communication, or definite repetitive behaviours / definite preoccupation(s) or repetitive behaviours that interfered with the assessment. Note the frequency and nature of any such references and whether they were well integrated into conversation. Note that unusual use of language or lack of conversational flexibility is coded separately under A4, and that repetitive behaviours involving objects with a clear sequence are coded separately under D5. (If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **Compulsions or Rituals (D5):** [Describe any ritualistic or compulsive behaviours, e.g., no obvious activities or verbal routines that had to be completed in full or in a particular sequence not required by the task / unusually routinised in speech or activities (e.g., insistence on finishing a task or providing lists not relevant to the conversation) but without clearly compulsive quality / one or more activities or verbal routines that the individual felt compelled to perform or complete in a specific way, with apparent pressure or anxiety if disrupted. Note that the provision of lists should be rated here. (If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **OTHER ABNORMAL BEHAVIOURS** **Overactivity and Agitation (E1):** [Describe the individual's overall activity level and any signs of agitation, e.g., sat still appropriately throughout the assessment / sat but often fidgeted or moved about in the chair without principal disruption to the assessment / difficulty sitting, moving either in or out of the chair or handling objects in a mildly disruptive way / overactive behaviours that were challenging to interrupt and that disrupted the assessment. (If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **Tantrums, Aggression, Negative or Disruptive Behaviour (E2):** [Note any anger or disruption beyond mild frustration during the session, including context and how managed, e.g., not disruptive, destructive, negative, or aggressive during the assessment / one example of mild disruption, anger, aggression, or negative behaviour (including verbal threats, swearing, or deliberately loud voice) / more than one intentionally disruptive or negative incident / marked or repeated temper tantrums or significant aggression (e.g., throwing things, hitting, or biting). If none present, state: "No significant behavioural concerns noted during assessment." (If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **Anxiety (E3):** [Describe any signs of anxiety observed during the session, e.g., no obvious anxiety / mild signs of anxiety or self-consciousness, especially at the beginning of the session or in response to specific activities / marked anxiety throughout the assessment, whether intermittent or continuous. Include triggers and any observable behavioural or physical signs if relevant. (If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **SUPPORT PERSON INTERACTION AND REPORT** **Support Person's Interaction Style:** [Describe how the support person engaged with the individual during the session, e.g., warm and supportive / highly directive / anxious or worried / observant and allowed the individual autonomy / required guidance to step back at times. If no support person was present, state: "The individual attended the session independently." (If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **Support Person Reports During Session:** [Briefly note any relevant information shared by the support person about communication, social interaction, repetitive behaviours, sensory responses, daily functioning, or developmental or life history concerns. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] **CLINICAL IMPRESSIONS AND SUMMARY** **Summary of Observed Strengths:** [Describe positive observations and capacities of the individual, e.g., demonstrated fluent verbal language and broad vocabulary; engaged in reciprocal conversation on topics of interest; showed understanding of others' emotions; demonstrated creativity in storytelling activities; showed warmth and humour; communicated own affect effectively; rapport improved across the session, etc. (If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **Summary of Observed Differences and Areas of Support Need:** [Describe patterns consistent with autism characteristics, e.g., differences in social reciprocity and social communication including atypical or inconsistent eye contact, limited integration of verbal and non-verbal communication, reduced spontaneous social initiation, limited gesture use, restricted conversational reciprocity, stereotyped or idiosyncratic use of language, restricted range of interests or repetitive references to specific topics, limited communication of own affect, limited insight into social relationships, presence of compulsive or ritualistic behaviours, and/or unusual sensory-seeking behaviours. (If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **Overall Clinical Impressions:** [Provide an integrated observational summary using neurodiversity-affirming language balanced with clinical terminology. DO NOT PROVIDE DEFINITIVE DIAGNOSES. This section is to provide a summary of characteristics that assists the clinician to provide a diagnosis. Examples:] [Option 1 — ASD characteristics observed: "Based on today's structured observational assessment using ADOS-2 Module 4, [INDIVIDUAL NAME] demonstrated a pattern of differences in social communication and reciprocal social interaction, along with restricted/repetitive behaviours and interests, that are consistent with an autistic neurotype / autism spectrum presentation. Specifically, [he/she/they] showed [list key observations across domains]. These observations should be integrated with developmental history, support person report, and other assessment data to inform the clinician's diagnostic formulation."] [Option 2 — Mixed presentation: "While [INDIVIDUAL NAME] demonstrated some features associated with autism (e.g., [list specific observations]), other areas appeared more consistent with typical functioning for [his/her/their] age and developmental level (e.g., [list areas]). Further integration with comprehensive developmental history, support person interview, and additional assessment measures is required by the clinician to determine if the pattern of differences meets criteria for autism spectrum disorder."] [Option 3 — Below threshold: "Although [INDIVIDUAL NAME] showed some individual features that can be associated with autism (e.g., [list]), the overall pattern of behaviours observed during today's assessment did not reach the threshold typically associated with autism spectrum disorder on the ADOS-2 Module 4. [His/Her/Their] social communication skills, reciprocal social interaction, and imaginative and creative contributions appeared broadly consistent with expectations for [his/her/their] age and language level, with [describe notable strengths]. These findings will be considered alongside other assessment data to help inform the clinician's diagnosis."] **ADOS-2 Module 4 Algorithm Scores (if available):** [If scores have been calculated, include here:] Communication (A) Total: [X] (items A4, A8, A9, A10) Social Interaction (B) Total: [X] (items B1, B2, B6, B8, B9, B11, B12) Communication + Social Interaction Total: [X] Stereotyped Behaviours and Restricted Interests Total: [X] (items C1, D1, D2, D4, D5) ADOS-2 Classification: [autism / autism spectrum / non-spectrum] Autism: Communication Total ≥ 3, AND Social Interaction Total ≥ 6, AND Communication + Social Interaction Total ≥ 10 Autism Spectrum: Communication Total ≥ 2, AND Social Interaction Total ≥ 4, AND Communication + Social Interaction Total ≥ 7, with at least one total below the autism cutoff Non-Spectrum: Any total below autism spectrum cutoffs [Note: Unlike Modules 1–3, the ADOS-2 Module 4 does not currently have a Calibrated Severity Score (Comparison Score). Algorithm scores are used directly to determine classification.] [If scores not yet calculated, print: "Formal scoring and classification will be completed following the session using the ADOS-2 Module 4 algorithm and will be integrated into the comprehensive diagnostic report."] **RISK, SAFETY, AND CONSENT** **Risk and Safety:** [If no concerns, print: "No immediate safety concerns were identified during today's assessment."] [If concerns present: Briefly describe any risk behaviours, safety concerns, aggression, or self-injurious behaviours, and note actions taken or still to be taken. (If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **Individual's Engagement:** [Describe the individual's apparent willingness to participate, response to transitions between activities, and overall tolerance of the assessment process, e.g., tolerated assessment well overall / showed some distress but was able to continue / required shortened session due to distress / engaged fully but with some hesitancy around specific topics or tasks. (If unable to make a judgment based on the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Unable to determine from available information.")] **PLAN AND NEXT STEPS** **Further Assessment:** [Describe what additional assessment components are needed or planned, as stated by the clinician or support person. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)] **Feedback Session:** [Note plan for sharing results with the individual and/or family in a Feedback Session. Include time and date for that session, if known. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)] **Additional Clinical Notes:** [Note any factors that may have influenced performance, e.g., the individual's mental health presentation, fatigue, unfamiliar environment, support person anxiety, need for additional observation, co-occurring conditions that may affect ADOS-2 presentation (e.g., anxiety, ADHD, mood disorder, personality disorder), or any modifications to standard administration that were necessary. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

title: Adult Cognitive Assessment Report
description: Provides a draft adult cognitive assessment report. It is recommended that clinicians add files of any relevant assessment results (e.g., WAIS,
prompt:
(Provide a detailed cognitive assessment report for a mental health clinician assessing an adult. Ensure that all information is attributed to its source, such as third party report, clinician observations, standardised assessments, etc. Provide your responses according to the instructions in the section headings below:) Confidential [Start on new line:] Psychological Assessment Report [Start on new line:] Name: [Print the client's first and last name.] [Start on new line:] Date Of Birth: [Print the client's date of birth in Day Month, Year format. If unknown, print: "Unknown."] [Start on new line:] Age at Assessment: [Print the client’s age on the day of the assessment in Years, Months format. If unknown, print: "Unknown."] [Start on new line:] Client's Address: [If known, print the client's address. If unknown, print: "Unknown."] [Start on new line:] Assessor: [Print the first and last name of the clinician assessing the client, and their profession.] [Start on new line:] Measures Used: * [Provide a list of the psychometric assessments used and quoted in this report. The list should be in order from oldest administered assessment to most recent administered assessment.] [Start on new line:] Assessment Date: [Print the date of the assessment in Day Month, Year format. The date printed here should reflect the date of the most recent assessment administered.] [Start on new line:] Report Date: [Print the date of this report in Day Month, Year format.] [Start on new line:] Documents Reviewed: * [Provide a list of all the documents quoted in this report other than the assessments administered. This might include reports from other health professionals, case worker reports, etc. Include the date of these documents, if known, and the author and author’s title of these documents. The list should be in order from oldest document to most recent document. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
***
[referrals] Referral Information [In a single paragraph, describe the reasons why a cognitive assessment has been requested for the client e.g., NDIS assessment, assessment of capacity for legal reasons, access to government resources, suitability for employment, etc. If known, indicate who has sought the cognitive assessment, who has referred the client, and any other information about the referral itself. When referring to someone other than the client for the first time, be sure to explain who that person is and what their relationship is to the client. Summarise the symptoms, behaviours, events, and situations that have led to referring the client for a cognitive assessment. Summarise when the onset of specific issues occurred, and how the specific issues have changed over time. Describe the impact on other aspects of the client's life, such as daily functioning, home / family life, social relationships, hobbies, and in any other area of the client’s life. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)] [history] Background Information / Personal History [In a single paragraph, describe background information and any personal history that is relevant to the client’s current circumstances. Consider including information such as: legal issues; housing; ability to manage finances; ability to live independently; pregnancy issues or complications; other developmental issues (e.g., delays in reaching milestones, etc.); historical and current family circumstances (e.g., family relationship break-ups, etc.); historical academic difficulties; historical social issues; and historical adaptive functioning difficulties. Finish the paragraph with a sentence about the client’s strengths and/or special interests and hobbies. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)] [previous_assessments] Previous Assessments [Describe the results of any previous psychological, developmental, educational, speech-language, occupational therapy, physical therapy, or medical assessments and evaluations. Detail when any such assessments occurred, the name of the practitioner that administered the assessment, the profession of the practitioner who administered the assessment, and the key findings and recommendations. If no previous assessments are explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "No previous assessments have been completed.")] [medical_and_mental_health_history] Medical and Mental Health History [In a single paragraph, describe the client’s medical and mental health history. Consider including such information as: previous diagnostic information (including when the diagnosis was made and by whom); current and previous medications; current and previous interventions (e.g., psychological therapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, etc.); impact of diagnoses on the client’s daily functioning and the client’s ability to learn; and family medical and mental health history. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)] [presentation] Current Concerns [In a single paragraph, summarise the current concerns for the client in relation to the cognitive assessment. Consider including information such as: current adaptive functioning difficulties; current neurological difficulties; current social difficulties; current employment issues, etc. Include both the client’s perspective, if applicable, and the perspective of any third party (e.g., case worker, parent / caregiver, guardian, etc.) if applicable. Describe the ways that the client or third party would like to see the client improve and specific goals they have for the client's development and functioning. This may include goals related to independent living, communication, social skills, behaviour, independence, employment, emotional regulation, or other areas. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)] [assessment] ASSESSMENT [behavioural_observations] Behavioural Observations [In a single paragraph, describe the client’s appearance and presentation; engagement and rapport; communication and comprehension; attention and concentration; motivation and effort; problem-solving style and working approach; emotional state and affect; and any other factors that may have influenced performance on any of the assessments. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)] [assessments_administered] Assessments Administered (In this section, you will provide information on each assessment administered. You will start with assessments measuring cognitive functioning and intelligence, then assessments measuring academic achievement, then assessments measuring adaptive functioning, then assessments measuring mental health, and then any other assessments used that fall into any other category. Note that different clinicians will use different types of measures, and not all reports will contain measures from all these domains.) [For each assessment administered, create a separate sub-section with a sub-heading naming the assessment used e.g., Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale – Fourth Edition Australian and New Zealand Version (WISC-V); Wechsler Individual Achievement Test – Third Edition Australian and New Zealand Version (WIAT-III); Adaptive Behavior Assessment System – Third Edition (ABAS-3); Depression Anxiety Stress Scales – Long Form (DASS-42); etc.] [For each assessment administered, below the respective sub-heading, provide an introductory paragraph describing the assessment. Consider including such information as: what the assessment is designed to measure; how the measure presents its results; summary of scales contained within the measure; and any other relevant information that helps orient the reader to why the measure was used.] [For each assessment administered, below the introductory paragraph, provide as many paragraphs as needed to explain the client’s results on the assessment and what these results mean for the client’s presenting concerns. You must decide what results are most relevant to the client’s presenting concerns, and only include results that expand on the reader’s understanding of the client’s cognitive profile. Therefore, you do not necessarily have to include every numerical result on every scale of every measure used. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)] [diagnosis] Diagnostic Impression [In a single paragraph, if known, describe the clinician’s diagnostic impressions and hypotheses, including the clinician’s differential diagnoses, and any other ideas expressed by the clinician around the possible reasons for the client’s presentation. If the clinician has not indicated any diagnostic impressions in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Clinician still to complete.")] [summary] Summary [Provide a concise summary in short paragraphs of the client’s reason for referral, the client’s assessment results, and a synthesis of all information. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.) [nextsteps] Recommendations * [Provide a list of evidence-based recommendations in response to the results of this assessment. Consider including recommendations related to: the client’s employment and/or academic potential and performance; the client’s adaptive functioning; the client’s ability to live independently (either now or into the future); the client’s mental health, etc. Recommendations can be directed towards any of the following: family members; guardians; caregivers; case managers; employers; disability support services; referrals to other health specialists for further assessment; referrals to any other services; etc.] * [List details of any future actions agreed on by the client or any other third party (e.g., family member, guardian, caregiver, case worker, etc.). This may include future appointment times.] [conclusion] Conclusion [In a single paragraph of less than four sentences, provide a summary of this cognitive assessment and any important ramifications for the client.] [Print the following:] While this report identifies areas where [client's first name] may benefit from additional support, it is important to recognise that [he/she] possesses strengths in areas not measured by these particular assessments. Building on these strengths may enhance [his/her]’s self-confidence, engagement, and overall well-being. Additionally, performance on cognitive assessments can be influenced by various factors including motivation, attention, fatigue, anxiety, and circumstances on the day of testing. The results should therefore be interpreted as one component of a comprehensive understanding of [client's first name]'s abilities. If you have any questions or would like to discuss any aspect of this report, please do not hesitate to contact me. Yours sincerely, [Clinician’s first and last name.]

title: Applied Behaviour Analysis (ABA) Session Note
description: For therapists using Applied Behaviour Analysis (ABA) with clients who have autism spectrum disorder, intellectual disabilities, or other
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by Applied Behaviour Analysis (ABA) therapists, behaviour analysts, and behaviour technicians working with clients across the lifespan using Applied Behaviour Analysis. Using the transcript from the session, you will generate a detailed summary from an ABA perspective.) (CRITICAL INSTRUCTION: First, determine the session type. If the session involved direct work with the client (with or without caregiver present), use SECTION A: DIRECT THERAPY SESSION. If the session was primarily focused on training parents/caregivers without the client present or with minimal client involvement, use SECTION B: PARENT/CAREGIVER TRAINING SESSION. Only complete the section that matches the session type. Do not complete both sections for a single session.) **SECTION A: DIRECT THERAPY SESSION** (Complete this section only if the session involved direct therapeutic work with the client.) **Session Information:** [Document the following: Date and time of session (start and end time), total duration, location of session (e.g., home, clinic, school, community), service type (e.g., 1:1 direct ABA therapy, group session), therapist/technician name and credentials, client name and age. Note if caregiver was present during session.] **Client Presentation:** [Describe the client's presentation at the start of the session, including affect, mood, engagement level, and any notable physical or emotional state. Note any changes from previous sessions. Document any relevant contextual factors (e.g., changes in medication, sleep, recent life events) that may impact the session. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] **Programs and Targets Addressed:** [Describe the specific skill acquisition programs and/or behaviour reduction plans that were targeted during this session. For each program, briefly note the specific targets worked on. Include program names as they appear in the treatment plan (e.g., "Receptive Language: Following 1-step directions," "Social Skills: Initiating greetings," "Manding: Requesting preferred items using AAC device", etc.) (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] **Session Activities and Teaching Procedures:** [Describe what occurred during the session and the teaching procedures used. Detail the ABA teaching methods employed (e.g., Discrete Trial Training/DTT, Natural Environment Teaching/NET, incidental teaching, task analysis, chaining, shaping, etc.). Note the structure of the session (e.g., structured table work, naturalistic play-based instruction, community-based instruction). Describe specific activities conducted and how they related to target programs. Include any modifications made to planned activities and the rationale for changes. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] **Data Collection Summary:** (Create a list to present quantitative data collected during the session. Organise by program/target and include relevant metrics.)
* **Program/Target:** [Name of skill or behaviour] * **Data Type:** [Specify: trial-by-trial, frequency, duration, percentage, interval recording, etc.] * **Performance Summary:** [e.g., "8/10 correct responses (80% accuracy)", "Requested independently 5 times", "Tantrum duration: 3 minutes total across 2 occurrences"] * **Prompt Level:** [If applicable, note prompting required: e.g., "Responded independently on 3/10 trials, required verbal prompt on 5/10 trials, required physical prompt on 2/10 trials"] * **Comparison to Previous Session:** [Note trend: improving, maintaining, declining, variable] [Repeat the above format for each program/target addressed in session. In the event that there is more than one program/target, be sure to leave a blank line space before each new program/target.] [For behaviour reduction: Include ABC (Antecedent-Behaviour-Consequence) data if challenging behaviours occurred, documenting what happened before, the behaviour itself, and the consequence provided.] **Prompting and Prompt Fading:** [Describe the prompting strategies used during the session (e.g., verbal prompts, gestural prompts, model prompts, physical prompts, visual prompts, etc.). Note any progress with prompt fading or changes to the prompting hierarchy. Document the client's response to different prompt levels. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] **Reinforcement Strategies:** [Describe the reinforcement strategies employed during the session. Note the reinforcement schedule used (e.g., continuous reinforcement, variable ratio, fixed ratio, token economy, etc.). Describe what reinforcers were effective (e.g., tangibles, edibles, praise, preferred activities, sensory items, tokens, etc.). Note any changes to reinforcement strategies and their effectiveness. Document any preference assessments conducted. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] **Challenging Behaviours:** [If challenging behaviours occurred, document them objectively. Include frequency, duration, and/or intensity. Describe the antecedents (what happened immediately before), the behaviour itself (in observable, measurable terms), and consequences provided. Note any patterns observed. Document the effectiveness of behaviour intervention strategies implemented. If no challenging behaviours occurred, state this explicitly.] **Barriers and Challenges:** [Describe any barriers encountered during the session (e.g., environmental distractions, equipment issues, client illness, competing motivating operations, session interruptions, etc.). Note how barriers were addressed and their impact on session productivity. Include any environmental modifications made.] **Client Response and Progress:** [Describe how the client responded to interventions and teaching throughout the session. Note engagement level, motivation, attention span, and cooperation. Highlight any notable successes, breakthroughs, or "aha moments." Document any difficulties or barriers to learning observed. Describe the client's overall responsiveness to prompts, reinforcement, and error correction procedures.] **Skill Generalisation and Maintenance:** [Describe any evidence of skill generalisation (e.g., using skills in new settings, with different materials, or with different people). Document maintenance of previously mastered skills if assessed. Describe any strategies used to promote generalisation (e.g., varying materials, changing instructors, practicing in natural environments, etc.).] **Caregiver Involvement:** [If caregiver was present during the session, describe their involvement. Note any observations they shared, questions they asked, or concerns they raised. Document any informal coaching or guidance provided to the caregiver during the session. Note caregiver's understanding and implementation of strategies if they participated in activities with the client. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] **Clinical Decisions and Modifications:** [Describe any clinical decisions made during the session. Document modifications to teaching procedures, prompting levels, reinforcement schedules, or mastery criteria. Explain the rationale for any changes made to programming. Note if any targets were mastered, put on maintenance, or discontinued. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] **Risk Assessment:** [Describe any risk factors discussed or observed, such as self-harm, aggression toward others, elopement risk, or suicidal ideation. If current suicidal thoughts or behaviours are present, highlight this information. List protective factors that mitigate the risk. Document any safety plans in place or action plans implemented if risk is elevated. If no risk factors are present, state this explicitly.] **Plan and Next Steps:** [Describe any recommendations from the therapist made in today’s session. Note any programming changes stated by the therapist or caregiver (e.g., increasing difficulty, fading prompts, adjusting reinforcement, modifying behaviour intervention strategies, etc.). Identify areas requiring additional focus or senior clinician consultation, as stated by the therapist or caregiver. Describe any environmental modifications or material needs for upcoming sessions, as stated by the therapist or caregiver. Outline the preliminary plan for the next session, including which programs and targets will be prioritised, as stated by the therapist or caregiver. Note the planned date/time of the next session if established. Include any preparations needed for the next session (e.g., materials to gather, activities to plan, caregiver coordination, etc.), as stated by the therapist or the caregiver.] **Session Summary:** [Provide a concise summary of the session in less than four sentences. Include overall productivity of the session, key highlights of client progress or challenges, and primary focus moving forward.] **SECTION B: PARENT/CAREGIVER TRAINING SESSION** (Complete this section only if the session was primarily focused on training the parent/caregiver.) **Session Information:** [Document the following: Date and time of session (start and end time), total duration of session, location of session, service type (Parent/Caregiver Training), clinician name conducting the training, parent/caregiver name(s) and relationship to client, client name and age. Note if client was present during training or if training was conducted without client present.] **Caregiver Presentation and Context:** [Describe the caregiver's engagement level, receptiveness to training, and any relevant contextual information. Note any concerns, questions, or specific challenges the caregiver raised at the beginning of the session. Document the caregiver's emotional state and readiness to learn.] **Training Goals and Objectives:** [Describe the specific parent/caregiver training goals being addressed in this session as outlined in the treatment plan. Examples: "Caregiver will implement differential reinforcement of alternative behaviour (DRA) with 80% fidelity," "Parent will identify relevant antecedents for target behaviours with 80% accuracy," "Caregiver will implement prompting hierarchy for self-help skills with correct sequence." Note which goals were the primary focus of today's session. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] **Topics Covered and Content Delivered:** [Describe the ABA principles, strategies, and techniques taught during the session. Include specific topics covered (e.g., understanding function of behaviour, implementing token economy system, using visual schedules, applying prompting strategies, conducting preference assessments, using specific behaviour management techniques, etc.). Note any educational materials provided (handouts, videos, visual aids, etc.). Describe how concepts were explained and whether real-life examples relevant to the family were used.] **Training Methods and Demonstrations:** [Describe how the training was delivered. Note if the clinician used explanation, modeling/demonstration, role-play, in-vivo coaching (coaching while caregiver interacts with client), video examples, or written materials. Describe any demonstrations provided by the therapist and what specific techniques were demonstrated. Document whether the training was didactic, interactive, or experiential.] **Caregiver Practice and Implementation:** [Describe what the caregiver practiced during the session. Document the caregiver's actual implementation of strategies with observable details. For example: "Parent practiced the 3-step prompting sequence (verbal cue → gestural prompt → physical guidance) during 5 opportunities for toothbrushing. Achieved 60% fidelity, requiring one verbal cue from clinician for correct prompt timing." Note the caregiver's fidelity to procedures using measurable data where possible (e.g., percentage of steps completed correctly, number of prompts required from therapist, etc.).] **Caregiver Performance Data:** (If applicable, present data on caregiver's implementation of strategies.)
* **Strategy/Skill Practiced:** [Name the specific ABA technique.] * **Implementation Fidelity:** [e.g., "Implemented 7/10 steps correctly (70% fidelity)", "Provided reinforcement within 2 seconds on 4/5 opportunities".] * **Areas of Strength:** [What the caregiver did well.] * **Areas for Improvement:** [What needs additional practice or clarification.] * **Prompts/Supports Provided by Clinician:** [Note coaching provided.] [Repeat for each skill/strategy practiced. In the event that there is more than one strategy/skill, be sure to leave a blank line space before each new strategy/skill.] **Connection to Client Treatment Plan:** [Explicitly connect the caregiver training to the client's goals and current programming. Explain how the caregiver's implementation of these strategies will support the client's progress. Reference specific client behaviours or skills that will be impacted. Include brief data or observation about how improved caregiver implementation is affecting client outcomes if applicable (e.g., "Caregiver's improved use of reinforcement correlates with client's 15% increase in compliance over the past two weeks per session data"). (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] **Caregiver Understanding and Engagement:** [Describe the caregiver's level of understanding of concepts taught. Note questions asked by the caregiver and answers provided. Describe the caregiver's engagement level and receptiveness to feedback. Document any concerns, resistance, or barriers to implementation raised by the caregiver. Note the caregiver's confidence level in implementing strategies independently.] **Caregiver Goals Progress:** [Review progress toward the caregiver's training goals outlined in the treatment plan. Note if goals were met, progressing, or require additional support. Document any updates needed to caregiver goals based on current performance and needs. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] **Barriers to Implementation:** [Describe any barriers the caregiver identified or that were observed during the session. These may include: time constraints, other family members not implementing consistently, difficulty managing multiple children, emotional challenges, physical limitations, lack of materials/resources, competing demands. Document how these barriers were addressed during the session.] **Problem-Solving and Collaboration:** [Describe any collaborative problem-solving that occurred. Note how the therapist and caregiver worked together to address challenges or adapt strategies to fit the family's routine and lifestyle. Document any modifications made to strategies to increase feasibility for the family. Highlight examples of the caregiver contributing ideas or insights about their child.] **Home Practice Plan and Assignments:** [Detail the specific homework or practice assignments given to the caregiver by the therapist. Be explicit about what the therapist has advised the caregiver to do, when, and how often. Include:]
* **Strategy to Practice:** [Name the specific technique.] * **Frequency/Schedule:** [e.g., "Practice during 3 mealtimes daily", "Use token board for 2 target behaviours throughout the day".] * **Data Collection Required:** [e.g., "Record frequency of independently completed steps on provided data sheet", "Note times when strategy was used in caregiver journal".] * **Materials Provided:** [List any materials given to support home implementation.] [Include the rationale provided by the therapist for assignments and expected outcomes. Note any concerns the caregiver expressed about completing assignments.] **Educational Materials and Resources Provided:** [List all handouts, worksheets, written instructions, video links, apps, or other resources provided to the caregiver by the therapist. Include titles/descriptions of materials. Note if materials are in the caregiver's preferred language and at an appropriate comprehension level. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] **Clinical Observations and Reflections:** [Describe the clinician's professional observations about the training session. Note the quality of the therapeutic relationship with the caregiver. Describe the therapist’s reflections on the effectiveness of teaching methods used. Identify any adjustments needed for future training sessions to better meet the caregiver's learning style or needs. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] **Risk Assessment:** [Describe any risk factors discussed or observed, such as self-harm, aggression toward others, elopement risk, or suicidal ideation. If current suicidal thoughts or behaviours are present, highlight this information. List protective factors that mitigate the risk. Document any safety plans in place or action plans implemented if risk is elevated. If no risk factors are present, state this explicitly.] **Next Steps:** [Outline the preliminary agenda for the next parent/caregiver training session as agreed upon by the therapist and the caregiver. Note which goals will be targeted next. Specify the planned date/time of next training session. Include any preparations needed (materials to create, videos to review, etc.). Document the plan for following up on home practice. Note how progress will be monitored (e.g., caregiver will bring completed data sheets to next session, clinician will review video of caregiver implementation, follow-up phone call scheduled, etc.). Include any scheduled check-ins between now and the next training session. If applicable, note any discussion about coordination with other professionals involved in the client's care (e.g., speech therapist, occupational therapist, school team). Document any information shared about the client's progress or strategies that should be communicated to other team members. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] **Session Summary:** [Provide a concise summary of the training session in less than four sentences. Include what was taught, how the caregiver responded, key outcomes, and the focus for continued caregiver development.]

title: Autism Clinical Interview for Adult Diagnosis
description: For clinicians who interview an adult to determine a diagnosis of autism spectrum disorder (ASD), this template provides a concise summary of
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by a clinician who is interviewing an adult client as part of a clinical investigation into whether they meet criteria for a diagnosis of autism spectrum disorder according to the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 5th Edition, Text Revision, also known as DSM-5-TR, and the International Classification for Diseases, 11th Edition, also known as ICD-11. After analysing the transcript, provide a concise summary of information provided by the client according to the section headings listed below. These section headings summarise specific criteria related to the diagnosis of autism spectrum disorder according to the DSM-5-TR and ICD-11. Your generated summary will assist to synthesise all the information provided by the client which will ultimately lead the clinician to make a diagnostic decision about whether the client meets criteria for autism spectrum disorder. Use professional, clinically appropriate language that is congruent with the language used in the DSM-5-TR and ICD-11. Avoid including direct quotes from the transcript unless the quotes are pivotal to the clinical assessment process. The template will be organised according to the following format:)

[referrals] Referral Reason

\[Describe the client’s reasons for seeking the autism assessment. Describe the symptoms, behaviours, events, and situations that have led the client to seek out an assessment for autism. Describe when the onset of specific issues occurred, and how the specific issues have changed over time. Describe the impact on the client’s daily functioning, such as at home, with family, at work or university, with social relationships, with hobbies, and in any other area of the client’s life. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate any of the client’s specific issues.]

[people_present] People Present

* \[Other than the client and the clinician, list the other people in attendance during the interview. State what the relationship of the person or people are to the client (e.g., partner, carer, other relative, case worker). If there are no other people in attendance, do not type the section title, and do leave this section blank.]

[mse] Mental Status Examination (MSE)

\[Provide a short paragraph using language consistent with accepted psychiatric and medical terms of no more than four sentences that summarises the client’s presentation using the following standard MSE domains (only include each domain if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual note, otherwise leave blank): Appearance (the client’s general physical appearance, hygiene, grooming, and attire); Behaviour (the client’s interaction with their surrounds, activity level, psychomotor activity, eye contact, and rapport); Speech (the rate, volume, tone, coherence, and articulation of the client’s speech; including if the client communicates in any other non-verbal way, such as with gestures, noises, or eye contact); Mood and Affect (the client’s reported mood and emotional state using their own words if possible; including the range and appropriateness of the client’s emotional response during the appointment, and note any discrepancies with the client’s stated mood; including any observations about emotional expression made by the clinician; including any behaviours that provide insight into mood and affect, such as anger outbursts, withdrawal, and laughing; including any other factors that may be providing insight into the client’s mood or affect, such as expressions of illness, or other interactions with the clinician; including the response of the client to the use of any visual aids regarding mood or affect, such as mood scales); Thought Process (the client’s coherence, organisation, and flow of thoughts); Thought Content (the content of the client’s thoughts, and note the presence of delusions, distortions, obsessions, or intrusive thoughts, if any); Perception (hallucinations or other perceptual disturbances of the client, if any); Cognition (the client’s orientation to time / place / person, attention, memory, concentration, and comprehension; including the results of any stated cognitive assessment that has previously been carried out on the client; including the client’s attention and concentration abilities); and Insight and Judgment (the client's understanding of their mental health and their decision-making capacity, noting any lack of awareness or denial).]

[history] Background

[personal_and_developmental_history] Personal and Developmental History

\[Describe as much detail as possible related to the client’s personal and developmental history. Include information related to the client’s early development, such as milestones and delays, including crawling, walking, speech, and toileting; and emotional development. Describe the client’s education history, such as academic performance, school behaviour, being the victim or perpetrator of past bullying, and willingness to attend school. Describe the client’s gross and fine motor skills (e.g., handwriting, bike riding, ball skills).]

\[Describe the client’s family history and structure, current living arrangements (including the names of all people known to live at home with the client and their relationship to the client), and social support. If relevant, include any collateral information about siblings or other family members of the client.]

\[Describe any history of trauma, including family or intergenerational trauma, or significant adversity or any type of traumatic event.]

\[Describe any family history of any illness, medical condition, psychiatric illness, autism, and other neurodevelopmental conditions. Include any information about family suicides or family addiction behaviours, such as alcoholism, and other drug abuse (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual note, otherwise leave blank).]

\[Describe the current circumstances of the client. Include information such as type of employment or education currently engaged in; current marital status; current status and extent of friendships and other relationships; quality and satisfaction with relationships; current daily routine; and the current level of insight that the client has about the impact of symptoms on their everyday functioning. Describe any support that the client currently receives from any government or non-government source.]

[previous_assessments] Previous Assessments

* \[List the results of any previous psychological, cognitive, or medical testing. Detail when any such assessments occurred, the name of the practitioner that administered the assessment, and the profession of the practitioner who administered the assessment. If there are no previous assessments noted in the transcript, leave this section out.]

[medical_history] Medical History

* \[List any previous medical, psychiatric, or neurodevelopmental diagnoses. Include information such as who made the diagnosis, their profession, and the approximate date that the diagnosis was given.]

[medications] Current Medications

* \[List all the current medications that the client is taking, both psychotropic and non-psychotropic. Include information on doses and frequencies, and provide the reason why the client is on each medication. If there are no current medications noted in the transcript, leave this section out.]

[other_previous_treatment] Other Previous Treatment

\[Describe any previous non-medication interventions that the client has participated in. Include things such as psychological therapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, and any other treatments that have been conducted by a registered health practitioner. If there is no other previous treatment noted in the transcript, leave this section out.]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria A

[social_communication_and_interaction] Social Communication and Interaction

#### A1

\[Describe any deficits in social-emotional reciprocity. This can range from abnormal social approach and failure of normal back-and-forth conversation; to reduced sharing of interests, emotions, or affect; to failure to initiate or respond to social interactions. Describe the client’s ability to initiate and maintain conversations. Describe the client’s methods of interaction with others (e.g., inclusive, dominant, solitary), the client’s greatest social challenges (e.g., speaking in front of others, working collaboratively, enjoying social gatherings), and any type of social situation where the client thrives or struggles. Describe any evidence that the client struggles to identify and understand sarcasm or any other type of social language convention, or any type of social convention generally. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### A2

\[Describe any deficits in nonverbal communicative behaviours used for social interaction. This can range from poorly integrated verbal and non-verbal communication; to abnormalities in eye contact and body language or deficits in understanding and use of gestures; to a total lack of facial expressions and non-verbal communication. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### A3

\[Describe any deficits in developing, maintaining, and understanding friendships and other relationships. This can range from having difficulties adjusting behaviour to suit various social contexts; to difficulties in sharing joyful moments with others or in making friends; to absence of interest in other people. Describe the quantity and quality of the client’s friendships. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria B

[restricted_patterns_of_behaviour] Restricted Patterns of Behaviour

#### B1

\[Describe any stereotyped or repetitive motor movements, use of objects, or speech (e.g., simple motor stereotypies, lining up or flipping objects, hand-flapping, echolalia, idiosyncratic phrases, superficially formal language, unusual tone of voice such as a strange accent). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### B2

\[Describe the client’s level of insisting on sameness, having inflexible adherence to routines, or ritualised patterns of verbal or non-verbal behaviour (e.g., extreme distress at small changes to routine, difficulties with transitions, rigid thinking patterns, greeting rituals, needing to take the same route, or needing to eat the same food every day). Describe any anxiety or worries that may be related to perfectionism or compulsion for completion. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### B3

\[Describe any highly restricted, fixated interests or hobbies that are abnormal in intensity or focus (e.g., strong attachment to or preoccupation with unusual objects, excessively circumscribed or perseverative interests). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### B4

\[Describe any hyper- or hyporeactivity to sensory input or unusual interest in sensory aspects of the environment (e.g., apparent indifference to pain / temperature, adverse response to specific sounds or textures, excessive smelling or touching of objects, visual fascination with lights or movement). Describe any sensory sensitivities. Describe any behaviours related to sensory processing, such as seeking or avoiding certain stimuli. Describe how the client’s sensory sensitivities impact on their daily functioning. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria C

\[Describe in one paragraph how relevant autism symptoms of this client presented in the early developmental period according to information provided in the transcript. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria D

\[Make a statement in one paragraph about how relevant autism symptoms have caused clinically significant impairment in social, family, educational, or other important areas of current functioning for the client. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria E

\[Describe in one paragraph whether the client has previously been diagnosed with intellectual disability or global developmental delay, or whether the clinician believes such an assessment would be beneficial if it has not previously been completed. Make a statement about the clinician’s observations of the client’s social functioning in comparison with expected social functioning in the context of accepted social norms.]

[strengths_interests_and_hobbies] Strengths, Interests, and Hobbies

\[Describe the client’s strengths, interests, and hobbies. Detail any unique skills that the client has, or any characteristics highlighted by the clinician as being above average, desirable, or beneficial. Detail any of the client’s abilities described as gifted or advanced in comparison to accepted norms. Describe any of the client’s personality traits that the clinician or other attendees state as being desirable, advantageous or in some way positive.]

[client_goals] Client Goals

\[Describe all the ways that the client would like to improve. According to the client, what would it take for them to flourish. This may include improved ability to form friendships; improved employment or academic outcomes; improvements to the client’s mood; improved ability to regulate other emotions such as anxiety and anger.]

[clinician_observations_notes_and_diagnos] Clinician Observations, Notes, and Diagnostic Impressions

\[Describe any other observations or notes made by the clinician during the interview. This may include information related to their assessment, comments on anything that the client has said, comments in response to anything the client has done, or any other clinically relevant information provided by the clinician in the transcript. Describe the outcome of any autism-specific assessments administered prior to or during this clinical interview. If provided, describe the clinician’s diagnosis, explanation of the client’s neurodivergent profile, and observations about comorbidities, differential diagnosis, and co-occurring traits such as anxiety, trauma, executive dysfunction, etc.]

[risk] Risk

\[Based on information in the transcript, make a risk assessment in a statement (e.g., suicidal or homicidal ideation, self-harm or harm to others, if any). Include any information related to past suicide attempts or past self-harming behaviour. Include any information about psychosocial stressors contributing to the level of risk. Describe any risk for social isolation. Describe any risk for being the victim or perpetrator of bullying, and include any stated history of bullying. Describe any protective factors that mitigate the risk.]

[nextsteps] Next Steps

* \[Specify if any evidence-based recommendations have been made by the clinician for further referral to a psychiatrist, or other specialist regarding a medication review for the client. If the clinician is a qualified prescribing doctor, specify any evidence-based recommendations for new or existing medication, and specify if any existing medications should be continued or discontinued. Provide the clinician’s rationale for these recommendations. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* \[Specify any suggested non-pharmacological interventions made by the clinician, such as psychotherapy or lifestyle modifications. Provide the clinician’s rationale for these recommendations. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* \[Specify if any referrals to other specialist services have been or will be made by the clinician (e.g., lab tests). Provide the clinician’s rationale for these recommendations. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* \[Detail what the plan is for ongoing assessment, follow-up, and monitoring, as detailed by the clinician. If the clinician has not made any plans for ongoing assessment, follow-up, or monitoring, leave blank.]
* \[Detail if any Safety Plan has been made by the clinician in the event that the client poses a risk to safety, including steps to take in a crisis. Document the client’s understanding of the Safety Plan. If the clinician has not made any Safety Plan, leave blank.]
* \[Summarise any education provided by the clinician to the client about the client’s ongoing assessment, diagnosis, and treatment options. Document the client’s understanding of and agreement with this education. If the clinician has not provided any education to the client about the ongoing assessment, diagnosis, and treatment options, leave blank.]
* \[List the details of any future appointment between the clinician and the client, including the time, day, and date.]

[summary] Summary

\[Provide a paragraph summary of the appointment with the client in no more than four sentences.]

sections.label: Clinician Observations, Notes, and Diagnostic Impressions

title: Autism Clinical Interview With Parent / Carer
description: For clinicians who interview a parent or carer of a child suspected of having autism spectrum disorder (ASD), this template provides a concise
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by a clinician who is interviewing a parent or carer as part of a clinical investigation into whether their child meets criteria for a diagnosis of autism spectrum disorder according to the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 5th Edition, Text Revision, also known as DSM-5-TR, and the International Classification for Diseases, 11th Edition, also known as ICD-11. After analysing the transcript, provide a concise summary of information provided by the parent or carer according to the section headings listed below. These section headings summarise specific criteria related to the diagnosis of autism spectrum disorder according to the DSM-5-TR and ICD-11. Your generated summary will assist to synthesise all the information provided by the parent or carer which will ultimately lead the clinician to make a diagnostic decision about whether the child meets criteria for autism spectrum disorder. Use professional, clinically appropriate language that is congruent with the language used in the DSM-5-TR and ICD-11. Avoid including direct quotes from the transcript unless the quotes are pivotal to the clinical assessment process. The template will be organised according to the following format:)

[referrals] Referral Reason

\[Describe the parent or carer’s reasons for seeking the autism assessment. Describe the symptoms, behaviours, events, and situations that have led the parent or carer to have the child assessed for autism. Describe when the onset of specific issues occurred, and how the specific issues have changed over time. Describe the impact on the child’s daily functioning, such as at home, with family, at school, with social relationships, with hobbies, and in any other area of the child’s life. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate any of the child’s specific issues.]

[people_present] People Present

* \[List the person or people who were in attendance during the interview, other than the clinician. State what the relationship of the person or people are to the child (e.g., parent, carer, other relative, case worker).]

\[State whether the child being assessed was in attendance or not.]

[mse] Mental Status Examination (MSE)

\[If the child being assessed is not in attendance during the interview, do not include this section.]

\[If the child being assessed is in attendance during the interview, do include this section.]

\[Provide a short paragraph using language consistent with accepted psychiatric and medical terms of no more than four sentences that summarises the child’s presentation, taking into account their developmental stage, using the following standard MSE domains (only include each domain if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual note, otherwise leave blank): Appearance (the child’s general physical appearance, hygiene, grooming, and attire); Behaviour (the child’s interaction with their surrounds, activity level, psychomotor activity, eye contact, and rapport; including information about the child’s interactions with any toys, creative behaviours such as drawing, and how the parent or carer has influenced the child’s behaviour in the session); Speech (the rate, volume, tone, coherence, and articulation of the child’s speech; including if the child communicates in any other non-verbal way, such as with gestures, noises, or eye contact); Mood and Affect (the child’s reported mood and emotional state; including the range and appropriateness of the child’s emotional response during the appointment, and note any discrepancies with the child’s stated mood; including any observations about emotional expression made by the clinician; including any behaviours that provide insight into mood and affect, such as tantrums, withdrawal, and laughing; including any other factors that may be providing insight into the child’s mood or affect, such as expressions of illness, contents of drawings and other creative play, and interactions with the clinician, or parent or carer; including the response of the child to the use of any visual aids regarding mood or affect, such as mood scales); Thought Process (the child’s coherence, organisation, and flow of thoughts; including any magical / fantasy thinking, referring to any imaginary friends, or the child’s belief in any supernatural beings such as Santa Claus; which does not have to indicate the presence of psychotic thinking, but may match the child’s developmental stage and / or how the child is responding to any external life events); Thought Content (the content of the child’s thoughts; including the presence of delusions, distortions, obsessions, or intrusive thoughts, if any); Perception (hallucinations or other perceptual disturbances of the child, if any; including if the child indicates that these experiences are distressing, impairing, or inconsistent with typical play themes for a child of similar age; including the presence of any stated nightmares or night terrors, and distinguish these from any mistaken belief that they are considered to be perceptual disturbances); Cognition (the child’s orientation to time / place / person, attention, memory, concentration, and comprehension; including the results of any stated cognitive assessment that has previously been carried out on the child; including the child’s attention and concentration abilities); Insight and Judgment (the child's understanding of their own difficulties and challenges, noting any age-appropriate lack of awareness or denial).]

[history] Background

[personal_and_developmental_history] Personal and Developmental History

\[Describe as much detail as possible related to the child’s personal and developmental history. Include information related to the child’s early development, such as issues with pregnancy and birth; milestones and delays, such as crawling, walking, speech and toileting; and emotional development. Describe the child’s education history, such as academic performance, school behaviour, being the victim or perpetrator of past bullying, and willingness to attend school. Describe the child’s gross and fine motor skills (e.g., handwriting, bike riding, ball skills).]

\[Describe the child’s family history and structure, current living arrangements (including the names of all people known to live at home with the child and their relationship to the child), and social support. If relevant, include any collateral information about siblings or other family members living at home with the child.]

\[Describe any history of trauma, including family or intergenerational trauma, or significant adversity or any type of traumatic event.]

\[Describe any family history of any illness, medical condition, psychiatric illness, autism and other neurodevelopmental conditions. Include any information about family suicides or family addiction behaviours, such as alcoholism, and other drug abuse (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual note, otherwise leave blank).]

\[Describe the current circumstances of the child. Include information such as name and place of current school; current school grade; current status of friendships; current daily routine; and the current level of insight that the child has about the impact of symptoms on their everyday functioning.]

[previous_assessments] Previous Assessments

* \[Describe the results of any previous psychological, cognitive, or medical testing. Detail when any such assessments occurred, the name of the practitioner that administered the assessment, and the profession of the practitioner who administered the assessment. If there are no previous assessments noted in the transcript, leave this section out.]

[medical_history] Medical History

* \[Describe any previous medical, psychiatric, or neurodevelopmental diagnoses. Include information such as who made the diagnosis, their profession, and the approximate date that the diagnosis was given.]

[medications] Current Medications
