* \[Describe all the current medications that the child is taking, both psychotropic and non-psychotropic. Include information on doses and frequencies, and provide the reason why the child is on each medication. If there are no current medications noted in the transcript, leave this section out.]

[other_previous_treatment] Other Previous Treatment

\[Describe any previous non-medication interventions that the child has participated in. Include things such as psychological therapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, and any other treatments that have been conducted by a registered health practitioner. If there are no other previous treatments noted in the transcript, leave this section out.]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria A

[social_communication_and_interaction] Social Communication and Interaction

#### A1

\[Describe any deficits in social-emotional reciprocity. This can range from abnormal social approach and failure of normal back-and-forth conversation; to reduced sharing of interests, emotions, or affect; to failure to initiate or respond to social interactions. Describe the child’s ability to initiate and maintain conversations. Describe the child’s play style (e.g., inclusive, dominant, solitary), the child’s greatest social challenges (e.g., sharing, taking turns, following rules), and any type of social situation where the child thrives or struggles. Describe any evidence that the child struggles to identify and understand sarcasm. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### A2

\[Describe any deficits in nonverbal communicative behaviours used for social interaction. This can range from poorly integrated verbal and non-verbal communication; to abnormalities in eye contact and body language or deficits in understanding and use of gestures; to a total lack of facial expressions and non-verbal communication. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### A3

\[Describe any deficits in developing, maintaining, and understanding friendships and other relationships. This can range from having difficulties adjusting behaviour to suit various social contexts; to difficulties in sharing imaginative play or in making friends; to absence of interest in peers. Describe the quantity and quality of the child’s friendships. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria B

[restricted_patterns_of_behaviour] Restricted Patterns of Behaviour

#### B1

\[Describe any stereotyped or repetitive motor movements, use of objects, or speech (e.g., simple motor stereotypies, lining up of toys or flipping objects, hand-flapping, echolalia, idiosyncratic phrases, superficially formal language, unusual tone of voice such as a strange accent). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### B2

\[Describe the child’s level of insisting on sameness, having inflexible adherence to routines, or ritualised patterns of verbal or non-verbal behaviour (e.g., extreme distress at small changes to routine, difficulties with transitions, rigid thinking patterns, greeting rituals, needing to take the same route, or needing to eat the same food every day). Describe any anxiety or worries that may be related to perfectionism or compulsion for completion. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### B3

\[Describe any highly restricted, fixated interests or hobbies that are abnormal in intensity or focus (e.g., strong attachment to or preoccupation with unusual objects, excessively circumscribed or perseverative interests). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### B4

\[Describe any hyper- or hyporeactivity to sensory input or unusual interest in sensory aspects of the environment (e.g., apparent indifference to pain / temperature, adverse response to specific sounds or textures, excessive smelling or touching of objects, visual fascination with lights or movement). Describe any sensory sensitivities. Describe any behaviours related to sensory processing, such as seeking or avoiding certain stimuli. Describe how the child’s sensory sensitivities impact on their daily functioning. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria C

\[Describe in one paragraph how relevant autism symptoms of this child presented in the early developmental period according to information provided in the transcript. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria D

\[Make a statement in one paragraph about how relevant autism symptoms have caused clinically significant impairment in social, family, educational, or other important areas of current functioning for the child. If no such symptoms are noted in the transcript, type “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria E

\[Describe in one paragraph whether the child has previously been diagnosed with intellectual disability or global developmental delay, or whether the clinician believes such an assessment would be beneficial if it has not previously been completed. Make a statement about the clinician’s observations of the child’s social functioning in comparison with expected social functioning for the child’s age.]

[strengths_interests_and_hobbies] Strengths, Interests, and Hobbies

\[Describe the child’s strengths, interests, and hobbies. Detail any unique skills that the child has, or any characteristics highlighted by the clinician or parent or carer as being above average, desirable, or beneficial. Detail any of the child’s abilities described as gifted or advanced in comparison to same-aged peers. Describe any of the child’s personality traits that the clinician or parent or carer state as being desirable, advantageous or in some way positive.]

[parent_carer_goals] Parent / Carer Goals

\[Describe all the ways that the parent or carer would like to see the child improve. According to the parent or carer, what would it take for the child to flourish. This may include improved ability to form friendships; improved academic engagement; improvements to the child’s mood; improved ability to regulate other emotions such as anxiety and anger.]

[clinician_observations_notes_and_diagnos] Clinician Observations, Notes, and Diagnostic Impressions

\[Describe any other observations or notes made by the clinician during the interview. This may include information related to their assessment, comments on anything that the parent or carer has said, comments in response to anything the child has done, or any other clinically relevant information provided by the clinician in the transcript. If provided, describe the clinician’s diagnosis, explanation of the child’s neurodivergent profile, and observations about comorbidities, differential diagnosis, and co-occurring traits such as anxiety, trauma, executive dysfunction.]

[risk] Risk

\[Based on clinician comments in the transcript, make a risk assessment in a statement (e.g., suicidal or homicidal ideation, self-harm or harm to others, if any). Include any information related to past suicide attempts or past self-harming behaviour. Include any information about psychosocial stressors contributing to the level of risk. Describe any risk for social isolation. Describe any risk of being the victim or perpetrator of bullying, and include any stated history of bullying. Describe any protective factors that mitigate the risk.]

[nextsteps] Next Steps

* \[Specify if any evidence-based recommendations have been made by the clinician for further referral to a paediatrician, psychiatrist, or other specialist regarding a medication review for the child. If the clinician is a qualified prescribing doctor, specify any evidence-based recommendations for new or existing medication, and specify if any existing medications should be continued or discontinued. Provide the clinician’s rationale for these recommendations. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* \[Specify any suggested non-pharmacological interventions made by the clinician, such as psychotherapy or lifestyle modifications. Provide the clinician’s rationale for these recommendations. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* \[Specify if any referrals to other specialist services have been or will be made by the clinician (e.g., lab tests). Provide the clinician’s rationale for these recommendations. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* \[Detail what the plan is for ongoing assessment, follow-up and monitoring, as detailed by the clinician. If the clinician has not made any plans for ongoing assessment, follow-up or monitoring, leave blank.]
* \[Detail if any Safety Plan has been made by the clinician in the event that the child poses a risk to safety, including steps to take in a crisis. Document the parent or carer’s understanding of the Safety Plan. If the clinician has not made any Safety Plan, leave blank.]
* \[Summarise any education provided by the clinician to the parent or carer about the child’s ongoing assessment, diagnosis and treatment options. Document the parent or carer’s understanding and agreement with this education. If the clinician has not provided any education to the parent or carer about the ongoing assessment, diagnosis and treatment options, leave blank.]
* \[List the details of any future appointment between the clinician and the parent or carer, or the child, including the time, day and date.]

[summary] Summary

\[Provide a paragraph summary of the appointment with the parent or carer in no more than four sentences.]

sections.label: Clinician Observations, Notes, and Diagnostic Impressions

title: BIRP (Behaviour, Intervention, Response, and Plan)
description: Designed to summarise a session between a mental health clinician and a client using the BIRP (Behaviour, Intervention, Response, and Plan) note
prompt:
(Provide a summary of a session between a mental health clinician and a client using the BIRP note format. BIRP stands for Behaviour, Intervention, Response, and Plan. Consistent with typical BIRP notes, each dot point in the following sections must only print key information, and must be as short and sharp as possible. You must adhere to the following layout:)
 

[behaviour] Behaviour


* [Describe the client’s observable behaviour, mood, and presentation during this session. Include relevant self-reported experiences and contextual factors. Examples of content include: appearance, affect, and demeanour (e.g., well-groomed, withdrawn, restless); speech, eye contact, or posture; emotional state or mood as observed and/or reported (e.g., anxious, frustrated, hopeful); situational stressors or triggers discussed; and risk-related indicators (e.g., suicidal ideation, agitation, impulsivity). (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

 

[intervention] Intervention


* [Describe the clinician’s therapeutic interventions, strategies, or techniques used during this session. If known, include the therapeutic framework used by the clinician (e.g., CBT, ACT, DBT, etc.). Describe the specific tools or exercises used (e.g., thought diary, meditation, role-play, etc.). Describe the clinician’s personal style and rationale (e.g., validation, reflection, or summarising). Describe any collaboration or problem-solving steps taken during the session. Describe any adjustments to the clinician’s approach based on the client’s state or feedback. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

 

[response] Response


* [Describe the client’s level of engagement, insight, and emotional response during this session. Describe any evidence of progress (e.g., emotional shifts, self-awareness, or cognitive breakthroughs), change, or resistance. Describe the client’s feedback on techniques or discussion topics. Describe any observations of affect, motivation, or ambivalence. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

 

[nextsteps] Plan


* [Describe the agreed-upon goals or homework tasks for the client. Describe the plan for upcoming sessions or interventions. Describe any referrals, consultations, or coordination with other providers. Note any risk management issues, or details of safety plans. Describe any updates to treatment focus or diagnosis. Specify the next appointment details and any ongoing monitoring plan. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

title: Child Assessment Summary for Parent/Teacher
description: For educational and developmental psychologists administering cognitive, academic, or developmental assessments to children. This template generates
prompt:
(Generate a plain language summary report for parents, caregivers, and teachers following a cognitive, academic, or developmental assessment of a child. This template is designed for educational, developmental, and clinical psychologists working in paediatric or educational settings. All content must be derived exclusively from information explicitly mentioned in transcripts, contextual notes, clinical notes, psychometric results, or other documents provided. Do not generate recommendations, observations, or clinical interpretations beyond what has been stated by the assessing psychologist or contained in supporting materials. Write as if you are a knowledgeable professional speaking directly and warmly to a parent who wants to understand and help their child. The language should feel personal, human, and clear — like a conversation, not a report. Do not use psychological or clinical terminology without immediately explaining it in everyday terms. Do not report numerical scores or index labels. Use descriptive language only — for example, "Liam finds this easy" or "this is harder for him than for most children his age." Every finding must be connected to the child's real life. Do not describe a cognitive ability in abstract terms — always explain what it means for how the child actually experiences school, homework, friendships, or daily life. For example, rather than "working memory falls in the low average range," write something like: "Holding information in mind while doing something with it — like remembering the start of a sentence by the time he gets to the end — is harder for Liam than it is for most children his age. This is likely why homework feels so effortful, and why he loses track of where he's up to." Where relevant, link findings directly back to the concerns that brought the family to assessment. Help parents and teachers understand not just what was found, but why it matters for their child specifically. Use warm, non-stigmatising, strengths-based language throughout. Explain difficulties in a way that removes blame from the child — for example, "this isn't about effort or attitude, his brain finds this harder" or "she's not being difficult — this is more demanding for her than it looks." Acknowledge the child's strengths specifically. Structure the report according to the section headings below. Use indented bullet points within sections as specified.)
**CONFIDENTIAL** [assessment_summary] Assessment Summary Child's Name: [Print the child's first and last name.] Date of Birth: [Print the child's date of birth in Day, Month, Year format] Age at Time of Assessment: [Print the child's age at the time of assessment in years and months.] School: [Print the name of the school the child currently attends. If unknown, leave blank.] Year / Grade Level: [Print the child's current year level e.g., Year 3. If unknown, leave blank.] Assessor: [Print the first and last name of the clinician and their professional title.] Assessment Date: [Print the date of assessment in Day, Month, Year format.] Report Date: [Print the date of this report in Day, Month, Year format.] Assessments Used: [List each assessment tool administered, including the full name and abbreviation, and the date it was administered. Use a bullet point for each.] Documents Reviewed: [List all documents reviewed, including the author, title, and date of each. Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical notes, otherwise do not print this subheading.] [why_we_did_this_assessment] Why We Did This Assessment [In a single paragraph, explain in plain, direct language why the assessment was requested and what it was hoping to answer. Name the specific concerns that were raised — by parents, teachers, or others — and write about them as a parent or teacher would recognise them. For example: "Liam's teacher and parents had noticed he was finding reading and writing much harder than expected for a child his age, and wanted to understand why." This section sets up the thread that all subsequent findings and recommendations should connect back to. Write in a way that immediately signals to the parent: this report is about your child and your specific concerns.] [about_child_s_first_name] About [Child's First Name] [In a single paragraph, provide a brief, warm summary of who the child is — their age, year level, family context, and any relevant background. Include only information explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical notes. This section should read like a brief, human introduction to the child as a person, not a clinical history. Mention strengths, interests, and personality where noted.] [what_the_assessment_involved] What the Assessment Involved [In a single paragraph, explain in plain everyday language what the assessment involved and what it was designed to find out. Describe what cognitive and academic testing actually is — what the child did, what it measures, and what it can and cannot tell us. Avoid all jargon. Help the parent/teacher understand why this kind of assessment is useful and what it adds to what teachers and parents already know. Only include assessments explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical notes.] [what_we_found] What We Found [Organise findings under subheadings that reflect the areas assessed in plain language terms (e.g., Thinking and Reasoning, Learning and Memory, Reading and Writing, Maths, How Quickly [Child's First Name] Processes Information). Use bullet points under each subheading. For each finding: * Write about the child directly and personally, not about test results. Never say "the WISC-V indicates" or "index scores suggest." Say "[Child's first name] is strong at..." or "[Child's first name] finds it harder than most children their age to..." * Do not report any numerical scores or range labels such as "low average" or "75th percentile." Describe only what the result means in human terms. * Immediately connect each finding to how it shows up in the child's everyday life — at school, at home, doing homework, with friends. Use specific, relatable examples drawn from the information provided. * Where a difficulty is described, explain it in a way that removes blame from the child. Make clear that the difficulty is real, not a matter of effort, attitude, or character. * Where relevant, explicitly link the finding back to a concern raised at referral. Help the parent see the connection between what was found and what they came in worried about. * Acknowledge strengths warmly and specifically. Only include findings explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, clinical notes, or psychometric results.] [what_this_means_for_child_s_first_name] What This Means for [Child's First Name] [Open with one or two sentences that draw the findings together into a coherent, human picture of the child. Then use short bullet points to explain — in plain, direct language — what the overall profile means for the child's day-to-day experience. This section should help the parent/teacher feel that they now understand their child better, not just that they have received information. Explain difficulties in a way that builds empathy and removes blame — for example: "The gap between what Liam knows and what he can show on paper isn't a reflection of how hard he tries. It's a reflection of how much harder the process of writing is for him than it is for most children." Explicitly connect findings back to the original concerns raised at referral. Help the parent understand what was going on for their child. Where relevant, name the diagnostic conclusion in plain terms and explain what it means practically. End this section on a forward-looking, strengths-based note that feels honest rather than formulaic.] [nextsteps] Recommendations [Open with one sentence that connects the recommendations directly to the child's profile. Then organise specific, actionable recommendations under plain language subheadings (e.g., “At School," "In the Classroom," "At Home," "Getting Extra Help," "What to Do Next"). Use bullet points under each subheading. Write every recommendation in plain language. Where a recommendation might be unfamiliar to a parent, briefly explain what it means and why it will help this child specifically. For example, rather than "implement structured literacy intervention," write: "Look for a tutor who uses a structured literacy approach — this means they teach reading and spelling in a very systematic, step-by-step way that works particularly well for children who find phonics hard. General tutoring without this specific approach is unlikely to be enough." Only include subheadings with content to report. Do not generate recommendations independently — only include what the assessing psychologist has explicitly stated. Attribute each recommendation clearly enough that a parent knows who is responsible for acting on it — the school, the parent, or an outside specialist.] [summary] Summary [In no more than three sentences, summarise the key findings and most important next steps in plain, direct language. This paragraph should be immediately meaningful to a parent who has just read the whole report and wants to know: what does this mean, and what do we do? Where possible, end with something affirming about the child that feels earned and specific, not generic.] This report has been prepared to help [child's first name]'s parents, caregivers, and teachers understand the assessment findings and support [him/her/them] effectively. The recommendations are based on current information and should be reviewed as [child's first name]'s needs change over time. For any questions regarding this report or to discuss the implementation of recommendations, please feel free to contact me.
Yours sincerely, [Clinician's first and last name] [Professional title]

sections.label: What This Means for [Child's First Name]

title: Child Cognitive Assessment Report
description: Provides a draft report of a cognitive assessment for a child. This template is useful for clinicians who have used the "Developmental History
prompt:
(Provide a detailed cognitive assessment report for a mental health clinician assessing a child. Ensure that all information is attributed to its source, such as parental report, clinician observations, standardised assessments, etc. Provide your responses according to the instructions in the section headings below:) Confidential [Start on new line:] Psychological Assessment Report [Start on new line:] Name: [Print the child client's first and last name.] [Start on new line:] Date Of Birth: [Print the child client's date of birth in Day Month, Year format. If unknown, print: "Unknown."] [Start on new line:] Age at Assessment: [Print the child client’s age on the day of the assessment in Years, Months format. If unknown, print: "Unknown."] [Start on new line:] Client's Address: [If known, print the child client's address. If unknown, print: "Unknown."] [Start on new line:] Assessor: [Print the first and last name of the clinician assessing the child client, and their profession.] [Start on new line:] Measures Used: [Provide a list of the psychometric assessments used and quoted in this report. The list should be in order from oldest administered assessment to most recent administered assessment.] [Start on new line:] Assessment Date: [Print the date of the assessment in Day Month, Year format. The date printed here should reflect the date of the most recent assessment administered.] [Start on new line:] Report Date: [Print the date of this report in Day Month, Year format.] [Start on new line:] Documents Reviewed: * [Provide a list of all the documents quoted in this report other than the assessments administered. This might include reports from other health professionals, school correspondence, etc. Include the date of these documents, if known, and the author and author’s title of these documents. The list should be in order from oldest document to most recent document. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
***

[referrals] Referral Information [In a single paragraph, describe the reasons why the parent, caregiver, or teacher has sought a cognitive assessment for the child client, emphasising the academic aspects of this referral. If known, indicate who has sought the cognitive assessment, who has referred the parent, caregiver, or teacher, and any other information about the referral itself. When referring to someone other than the child client for the first time, be sure to explain who that person is and what their relationship is to the child client. Summarise the symptoms, behaviours, events, and situations that have led to referring the child for a cognitive assessment, emphasising the impact on the child client's academic learning. Summarise when the onset of specific issues occurred, and how the specific issues have changed over time. Describe the impact on other aspects of the child client's life, such as daily functioning, home / family life, social relationships, hobbies, and in any other area of the child client’s life. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)] [history] Background Information / Personal History [In a single paragraph, describe background information and any personal history that is relevant to the child client’s current circumstances. Consider including information such as: pregnancy issues or complications; other developmental issues (e.g., delays in reaching milestones, etc.); historical and current family circumstances (e.g., family relationship break-ups, etc.); historical academic difficulties; historical social issues; historical adaptive functioning difficulties; name and place of current school; current school grade. Finish the paragraph with a sentence about the child client’s strengths and/or special interests and hobbies. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)] [previous_assessments] Previous Assessments [Describe the results of any previous psychological, developmental, educational, speech-language, occupational therapy, physical therapy, or medical assessments and evaluations. Detail when any such assessments occurred, the name of the practitioner that administered the assessment, the profession of the practitioner who administered the assessment, and the key findings and recommendations. If no previous assessments are explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "No previous assessments have been completed.")] [medical_and_mental_health_history] Medical and Mental Health History [In a single paragraph, describe the child client’s medical and mental health history. Consider including such information as: previous diagnostic information (including when the diagnosis was made and by whom); current and previous medications; current and previous interventions (e.g., psychological therapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, etc.); impact of diagnoses on the child client’s functioning and academic performance; and family medical and mental health history. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)] [presentation] Current Concerns [In a single paragraph, summarise the current concerns for the child client in relation to the cognitive assessment. Consider including information such as: current academic difficulties; current adaptive functioning difficulties; current neurological difficulties; and current social difficulties. Include both the child client’s perspective, if applicable, and the perspective of the parent, caregiver, or teacher. Describe the ways that the parent, caregiver, or teacher would like to see the child client improve and specific goals they have for the child client's development and functioning. This may include goals related to communication, social skills, behaviour, independence, academic performance, emotional regulation, or other areas. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)] [assessment] ASSESSMENT [behavioural_observations] Behavioural Observations [In a single paragraph, describe the client’s appearance and presentation; engagement and rapport; communication and comprehension; attention and concentration; motivation and effort; problem-solving style and working approach; emotional state and affect; and any other factors that may have influenced performance on any of the assessments. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)] [assessments_administered] Assessments Administered (In this section, you will provide information on each assessment administered. You will start with assessments measuring cognitive functioning and intelligence, then assessments measuring academic achievement, then assessments measuring adaptive functioning, then assessments measuring mental health, and then any other assessments used that fall into any other category. Note that different clinicians will use different types of measures, and not all reports will contain measures from all these domains.) [For each assessment administered, create a separate sub-section with a sub-heading naming the assessment used e.g., Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children – Fifth Edition Australian and New Zealand Version (WISC-V); Wechsler Individual Achievement Test – Third Edition Australian and New Zealand Version (WIAT-III); Adaptive Behavior Assessment System – Third Edition (ABAS-3); Depression Anxiety Stress Scales – Youth Version (DASS-Y); etc.] [For each assessment administered, below the respective sub-heading, provide an introductory paragraph describing the assessment. Consider including such information as: what the assessment is designed to measure; how the measure presents its results; summary of scales contained within the measure; and any other relevant information that helps orient the reader to why the measure was used.] [For each assessment administered, below the introductory paragraph, provide as many paragraphs as needed to explain the child client’s results on the assessment and what these results mean for the child client’s presenting concerns. You must decide what results are most relevant to the child client’s presenting concerns, and only include results that expand on the reader’s understanding of the child client’s cognitive profile. Therefore, you do not necessarily have to include every numerical result on every scale of every measure used. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)] [diagnosis] Diagnostic Impression [In a single paragraph, if known, describe the clinician’s diagnostic impressions and hypotheses, including the clinician’s differential diagnoses, and any other ideas expressed by the clinician around the possible reasons for the child client’s presentation. If the clinician has not indicated any diagnostic impressions in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, print: "Clinician still to complete.")] [summary] Summary [Provide a concise summary in short paragraphs of the child client’s reason for referral, the child client’s assessment results, and a synthesis of all information. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.) [nextsteps] Recommendations * [Provide a list of evidence-based recommendations in response to the results of this assessment. Consider including recommendations related to: the child client’s schooling and academic performance; the child client’s adaptive functioning; the child client’s mental health, etc. Recommendations can be directed towards any of the following: parents and caregivers; teachers; referrals to other health specialists for further assessment; referrals to any other support services; etc.] * [List details of any future actions agreed on by the parent, caregiver, or teacher e.g., future appointment times.] [conclusion] Conclusion [In a single paragraph of less than four sentences, provide a summary of this cognitive assessment and any important ramifications for the child client.] [Print the following:] While this report identifies areas where [child client's first name] may benefit from additional support, it is important to recognise that [he/she] possesses strengths in areas not measured by these particular assessments. Building on these strengths may enhance [his/her]’s self-confidence, engagement, and overall well-being. Additionally, performance on cognitive assessments can be influenced by various factors including motivation, attention, fatigue, anxiety, and circumstances on the day of testing. The results should therefore be interpreted as one component of a comprehensive understanding of [child client's first name]'s abilities. If you have any questions or would like to discuss any aspect of this report, please do not hesitate to contact me. Yours sincerely, [Clinician’s first and last name.]

title: Child Therapy Session Note - Brief
description: When working with a child client, this template has been designed to briefly summarise the session. It reflects a similar format to the "Therapy
prompt:
(The template below will provide a summary of a session between a child client and a therapist, prioritising the language and concepts typical of child therapy. Be aware that a parent / caregiver, or other key adult, may be present for part of the session. You must take into account the child client’s age and developmental level, if known, in your output. If there is no content for a particular section heading, do not print that section heading. Organise the summary under the following headings:)

[review_since_last_session] Review since last session

* [In less than two sentences, describe the child client’s current presentation, how things have been since the last appointment, etc. Mention if anything relevant was passed on by the parent / caregiver. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Severity/Impact**: [In less than two sentences, describe the current impact of the child client's difficulties and symptoms on their functioning e.g., impact on school, friendships, activities of daily life, etc. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

[history] Past Medical & Psychiatric History

* **Psychiatric Diagnoses**: [Details of any psychiatric diagnoses. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Medical History**: [Details of any recent medical issues, interventions or treatments, or hospitalisations, if any. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Current Medications**: [List current medications, including details such as dose and frequency. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

[content] Session Content

* [In less than two sentences, describe details of other relevant discussions with the child client during the session, including life circumstances, milestones, friendships, other relationships, interactions, or events important to the child client's wellbeing that have either occurred or are upcoming. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* [In less than two sentences, describe any goals that were discussed, including if any new objectives, desires or things to work on were discussed, or if existing goals were reviewed. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* [In less than two sentences, describe any setbacks or obstacles to progress for therapy goals and objectives. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* [In less than two sentences, describe any clinician comments about how any session content revealed something about the child’s inner world, as suggested by the therapist (it’s very important that you do not come up with your own interpretation of this – just describe what the clinician has said). Describe any clinician comments about non-verbal behaviours and body language. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* [In less than two sentences, describe the level of rapport and engagement throughout the session. Mention any changes to the level of rapport and engagement, and describe what, if anything, led to such changes. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

[interventions] Interventions

* [Describe details of specific therapeutic techniques and interventions used or to be used. Pay particular attention to techniques that are regularly used with children (e.g., sand tray, art, play, games, etc.), and techniques that have been modified by the therapist for use with children (e.g., how concepts were simplified or made concrete for the child; use of metaphors, stories, or characters; etc.). (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* [Describe any ideas, concepts or frameworks that were introduced, or the content of any handouts, use of whiteboard, or the use of any other visual aid or resource. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

[key_discussions_with_parent_caregiver] Key Discussions with Parent / Caregiver

* [Describe any important discussion points between the therapist and parent / caregiver. These discussions may have occurred during the session, or at the beginning or end. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

[risk] Risk Assessment and Management

* **Suicidal Ideation**: [Describe any history of suicidal ideation, attempts, plans in detail. If current suicidal thoughts or behaviours are present, highlight text in red. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Homicidal Ideation**: [Describe any homicidal ideation. If past or current homicidal thoughts or behaviours are mentioned, highlight text in red. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Self-harm**: [Detail any history of self-harm. If current self-harm is present, highlight text in red. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Violence & Aggression**: [Describe any recent or past incidents of violence or aggression. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Addictive Behaviours**: [Detail any recent or past addictive behaviours, e.g., illicit drugs, video games, food addictions, etc. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Risk-taking/Impulsivity**: [Describe any risk-taking behaviours or impulsivity. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Risk Management Plan**: [Describe strategies or steps taken to manage suicidal ideation / homicidal ideation / self-harm / violence and aggression / substance use / risk-taking behaviour (if applicable). (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

[nextsteps] Next Steps

* **Next Session**: [Mention date and time of next session. Mention any plans about next session. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Assigned Homework**: [Describe any tasks or activities assigned to the child client or the parent / caregiver. Mention any tasks that the therapist will do e.g., follow up with parent, caregiver, or teacher, etc. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

title: Child Therapy Session Note - Comprehensive
description: When working with a child client, this template has been designed to summarise the session covering a broad range of modalities. It reflects a
prompt:
(The template below will provide a summary of a session between a child client and a therapist, prioritising the language and concepts typical of child therapy. Be aware that a parent / caregiver, or other key adult, may be present for part of the session. In this instance, you must determine which speaker is the therapist, which is the parent / caregiver or other key adult, and which is the child client. Attribute all dialogue accurately to the correct speaker and avoid misattribution. You must take into account the child client’s age and developmental level, if known, in your output. If there is no content for a particular section heading, do not print that section heading. Organise the summary under the following headings:)

[presentation] Current Presentation

* [Describe the child client's current presentation or presenting complaint. You might include information such as: how long the issue(s) have been occurring, etc. Mention if the parent / caregiver, or any other key adult, was present for any part of the session. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Severity/Impact**: [Describe current impact on functioning e.g., impact on school, friendships, other areas of life, etc. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

[history] Past Medical & Psychiatric History

* **Psychiatric Diagnoses**: [Details of any psychiatric diagnoses. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Medical history**: [Details of any recent medical issues. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Treatments / Hospitalisations**: [Details of any recent interventions or treatments, including hospitalisations, if any. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Current Medications**: [List current medications, including details such as dose and frequency (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

[mse] Mental Status Examination

* **Behaviour**: [Describe the child client's activity level, interaction with their surroundings, and any unique or notable behaviours. Describe the child client’s attention span, observed regulation behaviours, and energy levels. Describe any boundary-setting or limit-testing behaviours that occurred. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Speech**: [Describe if the pace of speech was fast or slow for the speaker who is the client. Note the pace of speech for the child client, where a “normal pace of speech” is between 130 and 200 words per minute, “fast pace of speech” is defined as 201 words per minute or over, and “slow pace of speech” is defined as less than 129 words per minute. Only include a note on the pace of speech if it is fast or slow. If it is normal, do not include it in the note. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Mood**: [Record the child client's self-described emotional state, using their own words if possible. Note if the clinician asked the child client to rate their mood or any other aspect of their mental health. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Affect**: [Describe the range and appropriateness of the child client's emotional response during the session, noting any discrepancies with the stated mood. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Thoughts**: [Assess the child client's thought process and thought content, noting any distortions, delusions, or preoccupations. In reference to thoughts, note any magical / fantasy thinking, referring to any imaginary friends, or the child’s belief in any supernatural beings such as Santa Claus; which does not have to indicate the presence of psychotic thinking, but may match the child’s developmental stage and / or how the child is responding to any external life events. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Perceptions**: [Note any reported hallucinations or sensory misinterpretations, specifying type and impact on the child client. In reference to perceptions, note any magical / fantasy thinking, referring to any imaginary friends, or the child’s belief in any supernatural beings such as Santa Claus; which does not have to indicate the presence of psychotic thinking, but may match the child’s developmental stage and / or how the child is responding to any external life events. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Cognition**: [Describe the child client's memory, orientation to time/place/person, concentration, and comprehension. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Insight**: [Describe the child client's understanding of their own condition and symptoms, noting any lack of awareness or denial. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Judgment**: [Describe the child client's decision-making ability and understanding of the consequences of their actions. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

[content] Session Content

* [Describe details of other relevant discussions and interactions with the child client during the session. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* [Describe details of any life events, milestones, or circumstances that have occurred or are upcoming for the child client. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* [Describe details of any friendships, key relationships, or interactions discussed by the child client, including names of people mentioned. Describe the current social functioning of the child client. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* [Describe any clinician comments about how any session content revealed something about the child’s inner world, as suggested by the therapist (it’s very important that you do not come up with your own interpretation of this – just describe what the clinician has said). Describe any clinician comments about non-verbal behaviours and body language. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* [Describe the level of rapport and engagement throughout the session. Mention any changes to the level of rapport and engagement, and describe what, if anything, led to such changes. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

[goals] Goals

* [Describe in detail any goals that were discussed, including if any new objectives, desires or things to work on were discussed, or if existing goals were reviewed. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* [Note anything that the therapist asked the child client to do before the next session. Note anything the therapist said the child client could try. Record if the child client agreed to any tasks between sessions. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

[obstacles_setbacks_and_progress] Obstacles, Setbacks and Progress

* [Describe in detail the progress achieved by the child client towards each therapy goal/objective. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* [Describe in detail the setbacks and obstacles to progress for each therapy goal/objective. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

[interventions] Interventions

* [Describe details of specific therapeutic techniques and interventions used or to be used. Pay particular attention to techniques that are regularly used with children (e.g., sand tray, art, play, games, etc.), and techniques that have been modified by the therapist for use with children (e.g., how concepts were simplified or made concrete for the child; use of metaphors, stories, or characters; etc.). (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* [Describe any ideas, concepts or frameworks that were introduced, or the content of any handouts, use of whiteboard, or the use of any other visual aid or resource. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

[key_discussions_with_parent_caregiver] Key Discussions with Parent / Caregiver

* [Describe any important discussion points between the therapist and parent / caregiver. These discussions may have occurred during the session, or at the beginning or end. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

[risk] Risk Assessment and Management

* **Suicidal Ideation**: [Describe any history of suicidal ideation, attempts, plans in detail. If current suicidal thoughts or behaviours are present, highlight text in red. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Homicidal Ideation**: [Describe any homicidal ideation. If past or current homicidal thoughts or behaviours are mentioned, highlight text in red. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Self-harm**: [Detail any history of self-harm. If current self-harm is present, highlight text in red. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Violence & Aggression**: [Describe any recent or past incidents of violence or aggression. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Addictive Behaviours**: [Detail any recent or past addictive behaviours, e.g., illicit drugs, video games, food addictions, etc. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Risk-taking/Impulsivity**: [Describe any risk-taking behaviours or impulsivity. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Risk Management Plan**: [Describe strategies or steps taken to manage suicidal ideation / homicidal ideation / self-harm / violence and aggression / substance use / risk-taking behaviour (if applicable). (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

[summary] Session Summary

[Summarise the child client's progress in a paragraph with emphasis on current status, risk assessment and management, interventions, progress towards achieving therapy goals/objectives, setbacks, obstacles. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

[nextsteps] Next Steps

* **Next Session**: [Mention date and time of next session. Mention any plans about next session. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* **Assigned Homework**: [Describe any tasks or activities assigned to the child client or the parent / caregiver. Mention any tasks that the therapist will do e.g., follow up with parent, caregiver, or teacher, etc. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

title: Client Safety Plan
description: The document created from this template can be given or sent to a client at risk after their therapy session. It is a copy of a safety plan made with
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by clinicians providing therapy to a client. The document generated by this template will be given to vulnerable clients who have indicated they are at some type of safety risk. Use casual, informal language, but keep the tone polite and professional. Do not use jargon or technical terms. Make sure the wording used feels supportive, non-judgmental, and easy to understand, helping the client stay reassured and confident. The template will use the following format:)



Hi \[Client’s Name],

Thanks for speaking with me today.

[summary] Session Summary

\[Provide a clear and concise, but compassionate summary of the main issues discussed by the clinician and the client in today’s session. Highlight key topics, any insights the client gained, and any emotions or themes that stood out. Focus on the client’s emotional state and concerns. Use validating language to acknowledge the client’s feelings and efforts. Write only one paragraph of no more than four sentences.]

\[Based on information contained in the transcript or contextual notes, summarise the type of risk that the client is experiencing (e.g., suicidal ideation, self-harm, harm from others, etc.). Include any information about psychosocial stressors contributing to the level of risk. Avoid alarming language. Frame it as something that can be managed with the right support. Emphasise that they are not alone and that help is available. Describe any protective factors that mitigate the risk. Write this as a paragraph of no more than four sentences.]

[safety_plan] Safety Plan

**My warning signs:** \[Describe the feelings, thoughts, behaviour, situations that might signal to the client that they are struggling.]

**Make my space safe:** \[Describe practical steps the client could take to make their environment safe e.g., removing harmful items, avoiding isolation, etc.]

**My reasons to live:** \[Describe the positive things in the client’s life, things that the client is looking forward to, and things that potentially bring joy to the client e.g., a pet, a hobby, an upcoming event, etc.]

**My coping strategies:** \[Describe healthy, practical ways that the client can manage distressing thoughts and feelings e.g., grounding techniques, distraction, relaxation exercises, etc.]

**Places and people I can connect with:** \[Describe positive places that the client can go in order to potentially feel uplifted e.g., walk in the park, exercise at the gym, etc. Describe those people that the client can connect with in a social way e.g., meet someone for a coffee, invite a friend over for dinner, etc.]

**Trusted people I can talk to:** \[Describe people in the client’s life who are a positive influence, who can be contacted by the client to have a meaningful discussion with, and who are potentially able to provide support to the client if needed. List their contact details if specified in the transcript or contextual notes.]

**My professional support:** \[Describe the professional help potentially available to the client if needed in an emergency e.g., GP, counsellor, Lifeline, etc. List their contact details if specified in the transcript or contextual notes.]

[nextsteps] Next Steps

* \[List details of any appointments mentioned, including appointments between the clinician and the client; and including time, date, and location. Describe any actions agreed to by the client (e.g., going to the emergency department at the local hospital) or the clinician (e.g., agreed time to ring the client).]

\[Provide a final affirming, empowering statement in a calm, warm, and hopeful tone, reinforcing the message that the client has strengths, support, and options. The statement should remind the client that safety is possible, and that they have steps they can take to help themselves.]

If you have any queries or concerns, please reach out to me!

title: Client Session Summary and Homework Activities
description: To be given to a client or patient after their therapy session, this template allows the clinician to easily produce a document that provides a
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by clinicians providing therapy to a client. The document is to be provided to the client following the session. Use casual, informal language, but keep the tone polite, professional, and warm. Do not use jargon and technical terms. Avoid including direct quotes. Make sure the wording used feels supportive, non-judgmental, and easy to understand, helping the client stay engaged with their progress. The template will strictly adhere to the following format:)

Hi \[Client’s First Name],

Thanks for speaking with me today.

[summary] Session Summary

\[Provide a clear and concise summary of the main issues discussed by the clinician and the client in today’s session. Highlight key topics, any insights the client gained, and any emotions or themes that stood out. Keep it simple and easy to read, avoiding technical language. Write only one paragraph of no more than six sentences.]

[activities_for_you_to_do] Activities For You To Do

1. \[List all the tasks, exercises, or reflections that the clinician has suggested, and that the client has agreed to do before the next session. Be specific, and write them in a friendly, encouraging tone. These might include evidence-based self-help strategies, self-care activities, tasks that need to be completed (such as obtaining a new referral, visiting a different specialist), or anything else discussed.]

[next_appointment] Next Appointment

\[List details of the next appointment between the clinician and the client, including time, date, and location. If no future appointment was made in the session, do not type the subheading title, and do leave this section blank.]

\[Print a positive, affirming sentence that is encouraging and supportive of the client, taking into account the information that is provided in the transcript.]

If you have any questions, please feel free to get in touch.

\[Clinician's First and Last Name.]

title: Client Session Summary and Homework Activities - KIDS
description: To be given to a child client or patient after their therapy session, this template allows the clinician to easily produce a document that provides a
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by clinicians providing therapy to a child client. The document is to be provided to the child client following the session. Use casual, informal language that children can relate to, but keep the tone polite, professional, and warm. Do not use jargon and technical terms. Avoid including direct quotes. Make sure the wording used feels supportive, non-judgmental, and easy to understand, helping the child client stay engaged with their progress. The template will strictly adhere to the following format:)

Hey \[Client’s Name],

Thanks for speaking with me today.

[summary] Session Summary

\[Provide a clear and concise summary of the main issues discussed by the clinician and the client in today’s session. Highlight key topics, any insights the client gained, and any emotions or themes that stood out. Keep it simple and easy to read, avoiding technical language. Write only one paragraph of no more than six sentences.]

[activities_for_you_to_do] Activities for you to do

\[List all the tasks, exercises, or reflections that the clinician has suggested, and that the client has agreed to do before the next session. Be specific, and write them in a friendly, encouraging tone. These might include evidence-based self-help strategies, self-care activities, tasks that need to be completed (such as obtaining a new referral, visiting a different specialist), or anything else discussed. For each point, include an emoji relevant to content instead of a dot point]

[next_appointment] Next Appointment

\[List details of the next appointment between the clinician and the client, including time, date, and location. If no future appointment was made in the session, do not type the subheading title, and do leave this section blank.]
