title: Developmental History Interview - Child Cognitive Assessment
description: For clinicians conducting an interview (with a parent, caregiver, or teacher, for example) to obtain a developmental history before completing a
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by a clinician who is interviewing a parent, caregiver or teacher of a child who has been referred for cognitive assessment. Use this template to organise the background information gathered that is relevant to a cognitive assessment into a developmental history. Analyse the transcript and provide responses according to the instructions in the section headings below. Ensure that all information is attributed to its source, such as parent report, clinician observations, or standardised assessments.)

### Identifying Information

Name: [Child’s name]
Birthdate: [Child’s DOB]
Parent(s)/Guardian(s): [Name of parent/guardian]
Age: [Child’s age at assessment]
Referred By: [Name of referrer]
Date of Referral: [Date of referral]
Assessed By: [Name of clinician]
Date of Assessment: [Date of assessment]
Date of Report: [Print Date]

### Reason for Referral

[Using paragraphs, describe the parent, carer or teacher’s reasons for seeking the cognitive assessment. Describe the symptoms, behaviours, events, and situations that have led to referring the child for a cognitive assessment. Describe when the onset of specific issues occurred, and how the specific issues have changed over time. Describe the impact on the child’s daily functioning, such as at home, with family, at school and academically, with social relationships, with hobbies, and in any other area of the child’s life. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate any of the child’s specific issues.]

### People Present

[List the individuals present during the interview and/or assessment, such as parents, guardians, teachers, or other professionals. State the relationship of each person to the child.]
[State whether the child being assessed was in attendance or not.]

### Background Information

(Use short paragraphs to detail the information in this section. Do not use bullet points.)
[Describe the current circumstances of the child. Include information such as name and place of current school; current school grade.]

### Perinatal History

(Use short paragraphs to detail the information in this section. Do not use bullet points. If no relevant information about perinatal history is noted in the transcript, type "Information related to this area not found in the transcript.")

[Describe the pregnancy history, including maternal health during pregnancy, pregnancy complications (e.g., preeclampsia, gestational diabetes, bleeding, infections), maternal use of medications, alcohol, tobacco, or other substances during pregnancy, and maternal stress or significant life events during pregnancy. Note the length of pregnancy and whether it was full-term or premature.]
[Describe the birth history, including type of delivery (vaginal or cesarean), length of labor, use of forceps or vacuum extraction, complications during delivery, birth weight, Apgar scores if known, and any immediate post-birth concerns or interventions. Document whether the child required any special medical care immediately after birth, such as NICU admission, oxygen supplementation, or treatment for jaundice, infection, or other conditions.]
[Describe the neonatal period (first 28 days), including feeding (breast, formula, difficulties), sleeping patterns, early temperament characteristics, and any health concerns or medical interventions during this period. Document the parent's/carer's recollection of the child as a newborn, including ease or difficulty of caring for the infant, bonding experiences, and any postpartum complications that might have impacted early caregiving.]

### Developmental Milestones

(Use short paragraphs to detail the information in this section. Do not use bullet points. If no relevant information related to developmental milestones is noted in the transcript, type "Information related to this area not found in the transcript.")

**Motor Development**
[Describe the child's gross motor development milestones (head control through complex movements like running, jumping, bicycle riding), timing relative to typical development, and any concerns about coordination, balance, or motor planning. Provide specific examples from the transcript.]
[Describe the child's fine motor development. Include when they achieved major milestones such as reaching/grasping objects, transferring objects between hands, pincer grasp, self-feeding, scribbling, drawing, colouring, cutting with scissors, and handwriting. Note any concerns about hand dominance, pencil grip, or dexterity. Provide details on any developmental delays. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such information is noted in the transcript, type "Information related to this area not found in the transcript."]

**Language and Communication Development**
[Describe the child's early language development. Including when they achieved major milestones such as cooing, babbling, first words, two-word phrases, sentences, and age-appropriate conversation. Document receptive language abilities (understanding directions, following instructions) and expressive language abilities (vocabulary, grammar, narrative skills). Note any history of speech sound delays, stuttering, language regression, or pragmatic language difficulties. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such information is noted in the transcript, type "Information related to this area not found in the transcript."]
[Describe the child's non-verbal communication development. Include use of eye contact, facial expressions, gestures, pointing to show interest, and understanding of non-verbal cues from others. Provide details on any developmental delays. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such information is noted in the transcript, type "Information related to this area not found in the transcript."]

**Cognitive Development**
[Describe the child's early cognitive development, including understanding of cause and effect, object permanence, problem-solving abilities, memory development, conceptual understanding, and academic readiness skills (numbers, letters, colours, shapes). Document the parent's/carer's impressions of the child's learning style, curiosity, and interest in exploring their environment. Provide details on any developmental delays. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such information is noted in the transcript, type "Information related to this area not found in the transcript."]

**Social-Emotional Development**
[Describe the child's social development. Include social interest, early attachment behaviours, separation anxiety, stranger anxiety, play with others (parallel, associative, cooperative), sharing, turn-taking, empathy, and friendship formation. Document age at which the child began showing interest in peers and developing friendships. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such information is noted in the transcript, type "Information related to this area not found in the transcript."]
[Describe the child's emotional development, including emotional expression, emotional regulation, self-soothing abilities, frustration tolerance, and response to stress. Document any concerns about tantrums, emotional outbursts, excessive fears, or mood issues. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such information is noted in the transcript, type "Information related to this area not found in the transcript."]

**Adaptive Skills Development**
[Describe the development of the child's self-care skills, including feeding (use of utensils, cup drinking), dressing, grooming, bathing, and toileting. Note the age at which major self-care milestones were achieved and whether they were age-appropriate. Document any areas of unusual independence or dependence. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such information is noted in the transcript, type "Information related to this area not found in the transcript."]
[Describe the development of the child's daily living skills, including helping with household tasks, understanding safety rules, time concepts, money concepts, and community awareness. Document any concerns about the child's ability to function independently in age-appropriate ways. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such information is noted in the transcript, type "Information related to this area not found in the transcript."]

**Play and Interests**
[Describe the development of the child's play skills, including types of play engaged in at different ages (sensory, physical, constructive, imaginative/pretend, games with rules), preferred toys and activities, and play patterns. Document any unusual play interests, repetitive play, or restricted interests. Note the child's creativity, flexibility, and enjoyment in play. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate these points. If no such information is noted in the transcript, type "Information related to this area not found in the transcript."]

### Medical History

(Use short paragraphs to briefly detail the information in this section. Do not use bullet points. If no relevant medical information is noted in the transcript, type "Information related to this area not found in the transcript.")

[Describe the child's general health history, including any chronic medical conditions, hospitalisations, surgeries, injuries (especially head injuries), seizures, or other significant medical events. Document the age at which these occurred and their impact on development. If no such information is noted in the transcript, type "Information related to this area not found in the transcript."]
[Describe any history of ear infections, hearing difficulties, or vision problems. Document when these were identified, interventions provided, and current status. Include information about hearing and vision screenings or assessments. If no such information is noted in the transcript, type "Information related to this area not found in the transcript."]
[Describe any relevant medical conditions including allergies/respiratory issues (type, severity, management) and gastrointestinal concerns (feeding patterns, selectivity, elimination). If no such information is noted in the transcript, type "Information related to this area not found in the transcript."]
[Describe the child's sleep history, including sleep patterns, bedtime routines, sleep onset difficulties, night wakings, nightmares, sleep terrors, sleep walking, snoring, or other sleep disturbances. Document typical hours of sleep per night and whether this is adequate for the child's age. If no such information is noted in the transcript, type "Information related to this area not found in the transcript."]
[Describe any relevant medical, psychiatric, or neurodevelopmental diagnoses. Include information such as who made the diagnosis, their profession, and the approximate date that the diagnosis was given. If no such information is noted in the transcript, type "Information related to this area not found in the transcript."]

### Family and Social History

(Use short paragraphs to briefly detail the information in this section. Do not use bullet points. If no relevant family and social history information is noted in the transcript, type "Information related to this area not found in the transcript.")

[Describe the child's family composition and structure, including parents, siblings, and other household members. Document relationships, living arrangements, and family dynamics. Include information about any significant changes in family structure (e.g., separations, divorces, deaths, remarriages) and how the child responded to these changes.]
[Describe the family's cultural background, including primary and secondary languages spoken in the home, cultural practices, and religious or spiritual beliefs that may influence child-rearing or views on development and behaviour.]
[Describe the family's socioeconomic factors, including parental education, occupations, work schedules, and any financial stressors that might impact the child's development or access to resources.]
[Describe the child's caregiving history, including primary caregivers across development, any changes in primary caregivers, childcare arrangements (family members, daycare, nannies), and quality of attachment relationships with primary caregivers.]
[Describe any family history of developmental, learning, cognitive, psychiatric, or medical conditions, such as ADHD, autism spectrum disorder, learning disabilities, intellectual disability, language disorders, anxiety, depression, bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, substance use disorders, or genetic conditions.]
[Describe any history of trauma, abuse, neglect, or significant adversity experienced by the child, including witnessing domestic violence, experiencing physical, sexual, or emotional abuse, neglect, transitions or disruptions, natural disasters, accidents, or other traumatic events. Document age of exposure, duration, and any protective factors or interventions provided.]
[Describe the child's social environment and support systems, including extended family involvement, community resources, and religious or cultural community connections. Document the availability and adequacy of social support for both the child and family.]

### Educational History

(Use short paragraphs to detail the information in this section. Do not use bullet points. If no relevant educational history information is noted in the transcript, type "Information related to this area not found in the transcript.")

[Describe the child's school history, including names of schools attended, grades completed at each school, reasons for any school changes, and overall academic performance. Document strengths and challenges in core academic subjects (reading, writing, mathematics), work habits, response to the classroom environment, and any concerns raised by educators.]
[Describe any specialised educational services or accommodations the child has received, including Individual Education Plans (IEPs), special education services, classroom accommodations, resource room support, or gifted programming. Document when these services began, their nature, frequency, and perceived effectiveness.]
[Describe the child's school behaviour and social functioning, including relationships with teachers and peers, behavioural challenges in the classroom, and involvement in extracurricular activities. Document any history of behavioural interventions, discipline referrals, suspensions, or expulsions.]
[Describe parent-teacher communication and parental involvement in the child's education. Document the nature of feedback typically received from teachers and how the parents/carers view their relationship with the school.]

### Current Functioning

(Use short paragraphs to detail the information in this section. Do not use bullet points. If no relevant information is noted in the transcript, type "Information related to this area not found in the transcript.")

[Describe the child's current daily routine, including typical schedule, activities, and responsibilities. Document how the child manages transitions throughout the day and whether the current routine is effective for the child and family.]
[Describe the child's current academic functioning, including present grade level, school performance across subjects, homework habits, and attitude toward school. Document any current educational concerns or accommodations in place.]
[Describe the child's current social functioning, including friendships, social skills, participation in social activities, and social communication abilities. Document quality and quantity of peer relationships and any social challenges the child experiences.]
[Describe the child's current behavioural and emotional functioning at home, school, and in the community. Document strengths and challenges in these areas, including how the child expresses and manages emotions, follows rules and expectations, and responds to authority figures.]
[Describe the child's current self-care and independence skills, including age-appropriate activities of daily living, responsibilities at home, and level of supervision required. Document areas where the child demonstrates independence and areas requiring additional support.]
[Describe the family's current management strategies and interventions, including behavioural approaches, rewards and consequences, environmental modifications, and therapeutic supports. Document which strategies have been most and least effective.]

### Previous Assessments

[Describe the results of any previous psychological, developmental, educational, speech-language, occupational therapy, physical therapy, or medical evaluations. Detail when any such assessments occurred, the name of the practitioner that administered the assessment, the profession of the practitioner who administered the assessment, and the key findings and recommendations. If no information about previous assessments is mentioned in the transcript, type "No information contained in the transcript."]

### Current and Previous Interventions

[Describe any current and previous interventions that the child has participated in. Include psychological therapies, behavioural interventions, speech-language therapy, occupational therapy, physical therapy, applied behaviour analysis, social skills groups, parent training, or other therapeutic approaches. Document when each intervention began and ended, frequency, provider, goals, and perceived effectiveness. If no information about current or previous interventions is mentioned in the transcript, type "No information contained in the transcript."]

[Describe the current medications that the child is taking. Include information on doses and frequencies, and provide the reason why the child is taking each medication. Note when each medication was started, who prescribed it, observed effects (positive and negative), and any past medication trials. If no information about current medications is mentioned in the transcript, type "No information contained in the transcript."]

### Strengths, Interests and Hobbies

[Describe the child's strengths, interests, and hobbies. Detail any unique skills that the child has, or any characteristics highlighted by the clinician or parent or carer as being above average, desirable, or beneficial. Detail any of the child's abilities described as gifted or advanced in comparison to same-aged peers. Describe any of the child's personality traits that the clinician or parent or carer state as being desirable, advantageous or in some way positive. Write this as prose. Do not use bullet points.]

### Parent/Carer Concerns and Goals

[Describe all the current concerns that the parent or carer has about the child's development, behaviour, learning, or functioning. Detail the priority concerns from the parent/carer's perspective. Describe all the ways that the parent or carer would like to see the child improve and specific goals they have for the child's development and functioning. This may include goals related to communication, social skills, behaviour, independence, academic performance, emotional regulation, or other areas. Write this as prose. Do not use bullet points.]

### Clinician Observations, Notes and Impressions

[Describe any observations or notes made by the clinician during the developmental history interview. This may include information related to parent-child interactions observed, comments on anything that the parent or carer has said, impressions about developmental patterns described, hypotheses about factors contributing to the child's presentation, or any other clinically relevant information provided by the clinician in the transcript. Write this as prose. Do not use bullet points.]

### Summary

[Provide a concise summary of the child's developmental profile, highlighting areas of strength and challenge across developmental domains. Summarise any clear patterns that emerge from the developmental history, including timing of developmental concerns, consistency across domains, and progression of issues over time. Note any significant factors from the history that may be contributing to the child's current presentation. Write this as a short paragraph. Do not use bullet points.]

[Print the following headings:]

### Assessments Administered

[For each cognitive assessment tool used, provide a short paragraph to describe each assessment. Repeat this process for each cognitive assessment conducted. Print the following subheadings, which the clinician can use as a template for recording assessment results:]

### Assessment Results

**Full Scale Score:**
**Verbal Comprehension:**
**Visual Spatial:**
**Working Memory:**
**Fluid Reasoning:**
**Processing Speed:**

### Behavioural Observations

### Summary and Clinical Impressions

### Recommendations

title: Diagnosis Confirmation Letter
description: A letter from a mental health clinician confirming a client or patient's diagnosis, functional impairment, and treatment. This letter is written in
prompt:
(Write a letter from a mental health clinician to confirm a client or patient's mental health diagnosis and presenting concerns. The letter should be written in professional but accessible language that can be easily understood by a wide variety of recipients, including employers, educational institutions, insurance companies, and legal representatives. Avoid overly medical or technical language except when stating official diagnosis terms. The letter should be structured as flowing paragraphs without sub-headings. Do not print out any title at the top of the output.)

To Whom It May Concern,

Re: [Client's First and Last Name.]

DOB: [Client's Date of Birth in day month, year format e.g., 05 September, 1998.]

I am writing to confirm the mental health diagnosis, treatment, and current presentation of [Client's first and last name], who has been under my clinical care. [If the purpose of the letter is explicitly mentioned in the Additional Context, state it here in one sentence, e.g., "This letter is provided to support their request for workplace accommodations", or "This letter is provided for their disability claim application", etc."]

[In one to two paragraphs, describe the diagnosis or diagnoses. Include: the specific name(s) of the mental health condition(s); when the condition(s) were diagnosed; who made the diagnosis (if the clinician writing the letter made the diagnosis, state this clearly; if another health professional such as a psychiatrist, GP, or psychologist made the diagnosis, state who); and a brief, accessible explanation of what each diagnosis means and how it typically affects the client or patient. For example, "Major Depressive Disorder is a condition characterised by persistent low mood, loss of interest in activities, and significant changes in sleep, appetite, and energy levels" or "Generalised Anxiety Disorder is a condition where a person experiences excessive and ongoing worry about various aspects of life, often accompanied by physical symptoms such as restlessness, difficulty concentrating, and sleep disturbance." Use language that a non-medical person would understand. Only include information explicitly mentioned in the client's notes, transcripts, added files, and Additional Context.]

[In one to two paragraphs, provide a summary of the treatment the client or patient has received and their engagement with this treatment. If known, include: when the client or patient commenced treatment with the clinician; the type of psychological treatment or therapy approach provided (e.g., Cognitive Behavioural Therapy, trauma-focused therapy, supportive counselling, etc.); the frequency and duration of sessions; the client or patient's engagement with treatment; and a brief statement about their progress or response to treatment. Keep the language accessible and avoid overly technical medical terms. Only include information explicitly mentioned in the client's notes, transcripts, added files, and Additional Context.]

[In one paragraph, describe how the mental health condition(s) affect the client or patient's day-to-day life and functioning. Use simple, clear language to explain the practical impact on their ability to: interact with others and maintain relationships; concentrate on tasks and complete activities; manage daily responsibilities such as self-care, household tasks, or attending appointments; cope with stress or unexpected changes; and regulate emotions and behaviour. Provide specific examples where possible. Focus on functional impacts that would be relevant to the letter's purpose, if known. Avoid overly clinical language and instead describe impacts in terms that are easily understood by non-medical people. Only include information explicitly mentioned in the client's notes, transcripts, added files, and Additional Context.]

[If prognosis information is specifically requested in the Additional Context, or if it is clearly relevant to the stated purpose of the letter, include one paragraph addressing: the likely course of the condition with continued treatment; factors that may influence recovery or improvement; and realistic expectations about the client or patient's functioning in the short to medium term (e.g., the next 6-12 months). If prognosis information is not specifically requested or clearly relevant to the letter's purpose, leave this paragraph out entirely.]

[In a brief concluding paragraph, summarise the diagnostic and treatment situation for this client or patient, tying this to any explicit purpose for the letter, if known. Keep this conclusion concise and clear. Only include information explicitly mentioned in the client's notes, transcripts, added files, and Additional Context.]

Should you require any further information or clarification, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Yours sincerely,

[Clinician Name]

[Clinician Profession]

[Clinician Registration Number - if applicable]

[Practice Name]

title: Dialectical Behaviour Therapy (DBT)
description: For use by a clinician using a dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT) framework, this template provides a detailed summary of a session with a client.
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by a clinician working within a dialectical behaviour therapy, or DBT, framework. Following analysis of a transcript of a therapy session between a clinician and a client, you will generate a detailed summary of that session. Use language and concepts consistent with the practice of evidence-based dialectical behaviour therapy. Ensure the note maintains a professional, strengths-based, and non-judgmental tone, emphasising the dialectical balance of acceptance and change. Avoid excessive speculation while grounding observations in dialectical behaviour therapy principles and behavioural data. The template will follow the following format:)

[session_overview] Session Overview

\[Describe in detail the client’s current emotional state, distress level (e.g., SUDS rating), and any notable observations about affect and behaviour. Describe in detail any events that have contributed to or influenced these issues. Describe any reported high-risk behaviours since the last session (e.g., self-harm, suicidality, substance use) and corresponding diary card data.]

[focus_areas] Focus Areas

\[Describe issues related to the following DBT treatment hierarchy:]

* **Life-threatening behaviours:** \[Describe any issues related to suicidal ideation, self-harm, or life-threatening impulsivity (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank).]
* **Therapy-interfering behaviours:** \[Describe any issues related to attendance, engagement, non-collaboration, or avoidance (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, other wise leave blank).]
* **Quality-of-life interfering behaviours:** \[Describe any issues related to substance use, interpersonal conflict, or emotional dysregulation (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank).]
* **Skills acquisition and reinforcement:** \[Describe any issues related to the acquisition and practise of skills such as mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, interpersonal effectiveness, and/or walking the middle path (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank).]

[therapeutic_interventions] Therapeutic Interventions

* \[Describe specific DBT interventions explained or used by the clinician in session (e.g., validation strategies, dialectical strategies, behaviour chain analysis, commitment strategies, problem-solving, irreverent communication). Describe any DBT skills reviewed or assigned as homework, with attention to generalisation to daily life. Describe any contingency management techniques e.g., reinforcement of adaptive behaviours, extinction of problematic behaviours (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank).]

[client_s_responses_and_insights] Client’s Responses and Insights

* \[Describe the client’s engagement in session, participation in exercises, and response to interventions. Describe any cognitive shifts, emotional insights, or behavioural changes noted. Describe any barriers to skill use and problem-solving strategies discussed (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank).]

[session_outcome_and_next_steps] Session Outcome and Next Steps

\[Describe any progress toward treatment goals, including evidence of behavioural change. Describe any skills to be practiced before the next session and any assigned diary card focus. Describe plans for crisis management agreed on between the clinician and the client, if needed (e.g., use of 24/7 coaching, crisis plan review). List the time and date of the next scheduled session and any anticipated challenges or focus areas.]

title: DIR / Floortime Session Note
description: This template is designed for therapists working within the DIR/Floortime (Developmental, Individual-differences, Relationship-based) framework. It
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by therapists working within a DIR/Floortime framework with child clients who present with developmental challenges including autism spectrum disorder, developmental delays, and intellectual or developmental disabilities. Using the transcript from the session or dictated note, you will generate a detailed summary of the session from a DIR/Floortime perspective. Ensure the summary is concise and adheres to DIR/Floortime terminology. Organise the summary under the following headings.) [client_presentation_and_context] Client Presentation and Context [Describe the client's presenting concerns, current emotional state, and regulation status at the start of the session. Note any relevant changes since the last session in terms of behaviour, mood, or developmental progress. Describe any significant life events or contextual factors affecting the client's presentation. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] [caregiver_parent_participation_and_obser] Caregiver/Parent Participation and Observations [Describe the level and quality of caregiver/parent involvement during the session. Note any observations, concerns, or insights shared by the caregiver about the client's functioning between sessions. Detail any caregiver-client interactions observed during the session and the quality of their attunement. (Only include if caregiver was present or if mentioned in the transcript, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] [individual_differences_profile] Individual Differences Profile [Detail the client's sensory processing patterns observed during the session, including sensory-seeking or sensory-avoiding behaviours. Note any motor planning challenges, visual-spatial processing patterns, auditory processing observations, and other relevant processing differences. Describe how these individual differences were accommodated during the session. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] [current_functional_emotional_development] Current Functional Emotional Developmental Capacities (FEDCs) [Assess and describe the client's functioning across the six core functional emotional developmental capacities, also known as FEDCs, observed during this session. Use the following framework, noting which capacities were demonstrated, emerging, or challenged. Only include capacities that were relevant to this session:] **FEDC 1** \- Self\-Regulation and Interest in the World: \[Describe the client's ability to regulate their emotional and sensory state\, and their interest in engaging with the world around them\. Note whether the client was able to achieve and maintain a calm\, alert\, focused state\. \(Only include if relevant to the session\.\)\] **FEDC 2** \- Engagement and Relating: \[Describe the quality of emotional engagement and connection between the client and therapist/caregiver\. Note warmth\, affection\, intimacy\, and the "gleam in the eye" that indicates emotional connection\. \(Only include if relevant to the session\.\)\] **FEDC 3** \- Two\-Way Purposeful Communication: \[Describe the client's ability to open and close circles of communication through gestures\, vocalisations\, or other intentional signals\. Note the number and complexity of circles achieved and whether communication was initiated by the client\, or therapist or caregiver\. \(Only include if relevant to the session\.\)\] **FEDC 4** \- Complex Communication and Shared Problem\-Solving: \[Describe the client's ability to engage in longer chains of back\-and\-forth communication \(continuous flow\) and to problem\-solve collaboratively\. Note the number of consecutive circles of communication achieved and the complexity of problem\-solving interactions\. \(Only include if relevant to the session\.\)\] **FEDC 5** \- Using Symbols and Creating Emotional Ideas: \[Describe any pretend play\, use of words or symbols to represent ideas\, and the client's ability to express emotional ideas\. Note the themes explored in symbolic play\. \(Only include if relevant to the session\.\)\] **FEDC 6** \- Logical Thinking and Building Bridges Between Ideas: \[Describe the client's ability to connect ideas logically\, answer "why" questions\, and think abstractly\. Note any cause\-and\-effect reasoning or logical connections made\. \(Only include if relevant to the session\.\)\] [play_based_activities_and_interactions] Play-Based Activities and Interactions [Describe the specific play activities engaged in during the session. Detail how the therapist followed the client's lead and built upon the client's natural interests and motivations. Note the types of play materials used and how they facilitated interaction. Describe any symbolic or pretend play themes that emerged.] [floortime_techniques_and_strategies_used] Floortime Techniques and Strategies Used [Detail the specific DIR/Floortime techniques employed during the session. Use bullet points for clarity:]
* [**Following the child's lead:** Describe how the therapist joined the client's activity and built upon their interests. (Only include if used in session.)] * [**Circles of communication:** Describe strategies used to open and close circles and extend interaction sequences. Include approximate count of circles achieved in key interactions. (Only include if used in session.)] * [**Playful obstruction:** Describe any playful obstacles or challenges introduced to extend communication and problem-solving. (Only include if used in session.)] * [**Affect and animation:** Describe how the therapist used vocal tone, facial expressions, and energy level to engage the client. (Only include if used in session.)] * [**Expanding the range:** Describe efforts to broaden emotional or thematic range during play. (Only include if used in session.)] * [**Other techniques:** Note any additional DIR/Floortime strategies used. (Only include if used in session.)]
Child's Response and Engagement: [Describe the client's level of engagement throughout the session, including any shifts in regulation, attention, or interaction quality. Note moments of particular connection or breakthrough, including any "aha moments" or increased initiation from the client. Describe any challenges to engagement and how these were addressed. Detail the client's emotional expression and range during the session. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] [therapeutic_relationship_and_co_regulati] Therapeutic Relationship and Co-Regulation [Describe the quality of the therapeutic relationship and attunement between therapist and client. Note how the therapist supported the client's regulation through co-regulation. Describe the emotional warmth, trust, and safety established during the session. Comment on any challenges in building rapport or maintaining connection. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] [progress] Progress [Summarise progress toward established therapeutic goals, with specific reference to movement through the FEDCs. Highlight any notable developmental gains or emerging capacities. Note any areas where progress has stalled or new challenges have emerged. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] [home_practice_and_caregiver_coaching] Home Practice and Caregiver Coaching [Describe any suggestions or guidance provided to caregivers for implementing Floortime principles at home. Note specific activities or strategies recommended for daily interactions. Include any caregiver questions or concerns addressed. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] [plan_for_next_session] Plan for Next Session [Outline the focus for the next session, including which FEDCs will be targeted and what types of activities might be explored. Note any materials or preparations needed. Include the time, date, and day of the next scheduled session if mentioned. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)] [summary] Session Summary [Provide a brief recap of the session in less than four sentences, highlighting the client's engagement level, key developmental capacities addressed, notable moments of connection or progress, and overall session quality.]

title: Disability Support Pension (DSP) Letter
description: A letter from a psychologist or psychiatrist supporting a client's application for the Disability Support Pension (DSP) on the basis of mental health
prompt:
(Write a letter from a psychologist to support a client's Disability Support Pension, also known as the DSP, application based on mental health impairment. The letter must address Services Australia's criteria for DSP eligibility, including diagnosis, reasonable treatment and stabilisation, functional impairment, and work capacity. Focus only on mental health aspects relevant to the DSP application. Do not print out any title at the top of the output.)

To Whom It May Concern,

Re: [Client's First and Last Name.]

DOB: [Client's Date of Birth in DD/MM/YYYY format.]

I am writing to provide supporting evidence for [Client's first and last name]'s application for the Disability Support Pension on the basis of mental health impairment.

[professional_background] Professional Background

I am a registered [Clinical Psychologist / Psychologist / Psychiatrist], with [number of] years clinical practice experience.

[diagnosis] Diagnosis

[In a single paragraph, state the mental health diagnosis or diagnoses. Include: the specific mental health condition(s); when the condition(s) were diagnosed; who made the diagnosis (if the psychologist made the diagnosis, state this clearly; if another health professional such as a psychiatrist or GP made the diagnosis, state who); and any relevant diagnostic assessments or tools used. Note: For DSP purposes, mental health conditions must be diagnosed by a registered psychologist or psychiatrist. Only include information explicitly mentioned in the client's notes, transcripts, added files, and additional context.]

[treatment_history_and_stabilisation] Treatment History and Stabilisation

[In two paragraphs, provide a summary of the treatment the client has received for their mental health condition(s). Include: when treatment commenced; the type and frequency of psychological treatment provided (e.g., Cognitive Behavioural Therapy, Acceptance and Commitment Therapy, etc.); the duration of treatment; medication prescribed (if known); other treatments undertaken; the client's engagement with and response to treatment; and whether the condition has stabilised. Address whether there are any reasonable treatments that have not been undertaken and, if so, explain why (e.g., treatment has been attempted without success, treatment is not suitable due to the severity of symptoms, treatment is contraindicated, treatment is not accessible, etc.). State whether the condition(s) are likely to improve with or without further treatment in the next two years. Only include information explicitly mentioned in the client's notes, transcripts, added files, and additional context.]

[functional_impact_and_impairment] Functional Impact and Impairment

[Using as many paragraphs as needed, describe the functional impact of the client's mental health condition(s) on their daily functioning. Where possible, reference the descriptors from Table 5 - Mental Health Function of the DSP Impairment Tables. Address the following functional domains as relevant: (1) social and interpersonal functioning - ability to form and sustain relationships, participate in social activities, manage conflict; (2) concentration, persistence and pace - ability to concentrate on tasks, complete tasks, maintain attention, work at a consistent pace; (3) self-care and independent living - ability to maintain personal hygiene, prepare meals, manage household tasks, manage personal affairs; (4) adaptation and resilience - ability to cope with stress, adapt to changes, manage setbacks, make appropriate decisions; (5) behavioural regulation - impulse control, appropriateness of behaviour, emotional regulation. Provide specific examples of how the mental health condition(s) prevent the client from performing these functions in their day-to-day life. Only include information explicitly mentioned in the client's notes, transcripts, added files, and additional context.]

[assessment_against_impairment_tables] Assessment Against Impairment Tables

[Based on the functional impacts described above, provide the clinician’s professional opinion on the severity rating under Table 5 - Mental Health Function. State whether the functional impact is mild, moderate, severe, or extreme according to the criteria set out in Table 5 - Mental Health Function. Explain the clinician’s rationale for assessing the client at this level with reference to the descriptors in Table 5. If unable to assign a specific rating, describe in detail what the client's condition(s) prevent them from doing in their day-to-day life. If the client has other mental health-related impairments that may also be assessed under other tables (e.g., Table 6 - Functioning related to Alcohol, Drug and Other Substance Use, or Table 7 - Brain Function), note this as appropriate. Only include information explicitly mentioned in the client's notes, transcripts, added files, and additional context, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.]

[work_capacity] Work Capacity

[In a single paragraph, provide the clinician’s professional opinion on the client's current and likely future capacity to work. Address: whether the client can currently work and, if so, for how many hours per week; whether the client is likely to be able to work 15 or more hours per week within the next two years; specific functional limitations that affect the client's ability to work; and any relevant work history, including difficulties maintaining employment or reduced working hours due to mental health condition(s). Consider the client's skills, qualifications, and the demands of potential work when assessing work capacity. Only include information explicitly mentioned in the client's notes, transcripts, added files, and additional context.]

[psychometric_assessments] Psychometric Assessments

[If psychometric assessments have been completed, provide a brief summary of relevant results that support the DSP application. List the relevant assessment names, dates completed, and key findings that demonstrate the severity of mental health impairment. Only include relevant assessments explicitly mentioned in the client's psychometric assessment results, notes, transcripts, added files, and additional context. If no assessments have been completed or mentioned, leave this section blank and do not print the sub-heading.]

[supporting_documentation] Supporting Documentation

[If relevant, note any additional supporting documents attached to this letter, such as previous psychological reports, treatment summaries, medication lists, or referrals to other health professionals. Only mention documents that are explicitly referenced in the client's notes, transcripts, added files, and additional context. If no supporting documents are mentioned, leave this section blank and do not print the sub-heading.]

[summary] Summary

[In a brief paragraph, summarise the clinician’s professional opinion regarding the client's eligibility for the Disability Support Pension on the basis of mental health impairment. Confirm whether the clinician considers the client's condition(s) to be fully diagnosed, reasonably treated, and stabilised, and whether the functional impairment is sufficient to prevent the client from working 15 or more hours per week for the next two years.]

Should you require any further information or clarification regarding [Client's first and last name], please do not hesitate to contact me.

Yours sincerely,

[Clinician Name]

[Clinician Profession]

[Clinician Registration Number - if applicable]

[Practice Name]

title: Discharge Summary - Letter to Referrer
description: A discharge letter providing an overview of a client / patient's complete treatment. It is appropriate to use if a clinician has finished their
prompt:
(Write a discharge letter providing an overview of a client / patient’s completed treatment. The letter/report is in the context of a clinician finishing their therapeutic relationship with a client or patient and is writing a detailed letter or report to another health professional. Focus the report on details around the client, avoiding mentioning identifiable information about other people that are not pertinent to the letter. Do not print out any title at the top of the output.)

Re: [Client’s Full Name]; DOB [Client’s Date of Birth.]

Dear Dr [Referring Clinician's Last Name] (only include the name of the addressee if explicitly mentioned, otherwise say “Dear Dr),

Thank you for referring [Client’s First and Last Name], who I have seen on [specify type and length of treatment] and now discharge from my care [specify reason for ending treatment (only if mentioned in the notes, transcripts or additional context)].

[presentation] Presentation

[Using as many paragraphs as needed, provide a concise summary of the client’s background, initial presenting issues, and initial goals for treatment. Using the client’s session notes, transcripts, and additional context, include information such as: the client’s initial presenting issues; other key client information such as circumstances, background, diagnoses and medical history, occupation, current living arrangements, and social support; trauma history; relevant people involved in the client’s life; and the client’s goals for treatment. Summarise the clinician’s initial bio-psycho-social formulation, including predisposing, precipitating, perpetuating and protective factors. If applicable, describe how any of this information significantly changed over the course of treatment.]

[treatment] Treatment

[Using as many paragraphs as needed, provide a concise summary of the treatment provided by the clinician. Using the client’s session notes, transcripts, and additional context, include information such as: date of first and last session; frequency of sessions; modality of therapy (e.g., CBT, ACT, schema therapy, psychodynamic psychotherapy); types of interventions used; types of homework or other activities completed as part of treatment; success or otherwise of treatment; the client’s engagement in treatment; and the client’s rapport or therapeutic alliance with the clinician. If applicable, include information related to medication. Only include information that is explicitly mentioned in the client’s notes, transcripts, and additional context.]

[psychometric_assessments] Psychometric Assessments

The following psychometric assessments have been completed:
[Using bullet points, list the name of any psychometric assessment result completed, including the date completed and summarised results. List the assessments from most recently completed. (Only include information that is explicitly mentioned in the client’s psychometric assessment results, otherwise exclude Psychometrics Assessments section).]

[coping_skills] Coping Skills

[In a single paragraph, describe the client’s strengths, positive coping skills, any significant successes during the treatment period, positive aspects of the client’s character, and other protective factors. Discuss how these are relevant to the client’s current situation and the client’s potential future situation. Only mention things that are accepted as helpful and useful in successfully managing and maintaining good mental health. Do not mention anything that is considered harmful to mental health (e.g., consuming alcohol, other addictive behaviours). Only include information that is explicitly mentioned in the client’s notes, transcripts, and additional context.]

[nextsteps] Recommendations

[Using as many paragraphs as needed, provide any recommendations for future treatment and management mentioned by the clinician. Provide the rationale for each of these recommendations. Only include information that is explicitly mentioned in the client’s notes, transcripts, and additional context.]

[Reason for discharge: note any reason for ending treatment that has been discussed and include any plan for re-engagement (only include if explicitly mentioned in the notes, transcript or additional context, otherwise leave blank)]

Should you have any further questions regarding [first and last name of client], please feel free to contact me.

Sincerely,

[Clinician Name]
[Clinician Profession]
[Practice Name]

title: DIVA-5 Assessment for ADHD – With Clinical Interview
description: The DIVA-5 assessment for ADHD is a comprehensive tool that aligns with DSM-5-TR diagnostic criteria. Whether you are using the DIVA-5, DIVA-5 ID, or
prompt:
(In the below template there are two clearly marked sections. You must decide which section to use based on information contained in the transcript and contextual notes. Only use either Section 1 or Section 2. Not both.)

(SECTION 1 SHOULD ONLY BE USED IF AN ADULT IS BEING ASSESSED. DO NOT USE SECTION 1 IF A CHILD UNDER THE AGE OF 18 YEARS IS BEING ASSESSED. Section 1 is designed to be used by a clinician who is using the Diagnostic Interview for ADHD in Adults, also known as the DIVA-5, or the Diagnostic Interview for ADHD in Adults with Intellectual Disability, also known as the DIVA-5 ID. The template is designed to provide a comprehensive summary of an interview with an adult client who may or may not be accompanied by another support person or people. The interview is part of a clinical investigation into whether the adult client meets criteria for a diagnosis of attention deficit hyperactivity disorder according to the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 5th Edition, Text Revision, also known as the DSM-5-TR. Analyse the transcript and provide responses according to the instructions in the section headings listed below. Use professional, clinically appropriate language that is congruent with the language used in the DSM-5-TR.)

(SECTION 2 SHOULD BE USED IF A CHILD UNDER THE THE AGE OF 18 YEARS IS BEING ASSESSED.) Section 2 is designed to be used by a clinician who is using the Diagnostic Interview for ADHD in young people aged 5-17 years, also known as the Young DIVA-5, to interview a child client under the age of 18 years and that client’s parent or caregiver as part of a clinical investigation into whether the child client meets criteria for a diagnosis of attention deficit hyperactivity disorder according to the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 5th Edition, Text Revision, also known as the DSM-5-TR. Analyse the transcript and provide responses according to the instructions in the section headings listed below. Use professional, clinically appropriate language that is congruent with the language used in the DSM-5-TR.)

\[START SECTION 1.]

\[Section 1 is only to be used when assessing an adult, 18 years of age or older.]

### DIVA-5 Questionnaire

\[If using the DIVA-5 ID, add a subheading here:] DIVA-5 ID \[If not using the DIVA-5 ID version, leave this line blank.]

### Part 1: Symptoms of Attention-Deficit

### DSM-5-TR Criteria A1

#### <u>Inattention – five or more of the following are required</u>

#### A

\[Describe if the client often fails to give close attention to details or makes careless mistakes in academic work, at work, or during other activities (e.g., overlooks or misses details, work is inaccurate). If applicable, describe how this applied to the client during childhood. If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### B

\[Describe if the client often has difficulty sustaining attention in tasks or leisure activities (e.g., has difficulty remaining focused during lectures, conversations, or lengthy reading). If applicable, describe how this applied to the client during childhood. If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### C

\[Describe if the client often does not seem to listen when spoken to directly (e.g., mind seems elsewhere, even in the absence of any obvious distraction). If applicable, describe how this applied to the client during childhood. If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### D

\[Describe if the client often does not follow through on instructions and fails to finish academic work, chores, or duties in the workplace (e.g., starts tasks but quickly loses focus and is easily sidetracked). If applicable, describe how this applied to the client during childhood. If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### E

\[Describe if the client often has difficulty organising tasks and activities (e.g., difficulty managing sequential tasks; difficulty keeping materials and belongings in order; messy, disorganised work; has poor time management; fails to meet deadlines). If applicable, describe how this applied to the client during childhood. If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### F

\[Describe if the client often avoids, dislikes, or is reluctant to engage in tasks that require sustained mental effort (e.g., academic work, preparing reports, completing forms, reviewing lengthy papers). If applicable, describe how this applied to the client during childhood. If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### G

\[Describe if the client often loses things necessary for tasks or activities (e.g., learning materials, pencils, books, tools, wallets, keys, paperwork, eyeglasses, mobile telephones). If applicable, describe how this applied to the client during childhood. If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### H

\[Describe if the client is often easily distracted by extraneous stimuli (including having unrelated thoughts). If applicable, describe how this applied to the client during childhood. If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### I

\[Describe if the client is often forgetful in daily activities (e.g., doing chores, running errands, returning calls, paying bills, keeping appointments). If applicable, describe how this applied to the client during childhood. If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### Part 2: Symptoms of Hyperactivity-Impulsivity

### DSM-5-TR Criteria A2

#### <u>Hyperactivity and Impulsivity – five or more of the following are required</u>

#### A

\[Describe if the client often fidgets with, or taps hands or feet, or squirms in seat. If applicable, describe how this applied to the client during childhood. If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### B

\[Describe if the client often leaves seat in situations when remaining seated is expected (e.g., leaves his or her place in the classroom, in the office, or other workplace, or in other situations that require remaining in place). If applicable, describe how this applied to the client during childhood. If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### C

\[Describe if the client often runs about or climbs in situations where it is inappropriate. Describe if the client says they often feel restless. If applicable, describe how this applied to the client during childhood. If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### D

\[Describe if the client is often unable to play or engage in leisure activities quietly. If applicable, describe how this applied to the client during childhood. If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### E

\[Describe if the client is often “on the go”, acting as if “driven by a motor” (e.g., is unable to be or uncomfortable being still for extended time, as in restaurants, meetings; may be experienced by others as being restless or difficult to keep up with). If applicable, describe how this applied to the client during childhood. If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### F

\[Describe if the client often talks excessively. If applicable, describe how this applied to the client during childhood. If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### G

\[Describe if the client often blurts out an answer before a question has been completed (e.g., completes people’s sentences; cannot wait for their turn in conversation). If applicable, describe how this applied to the client during childhood. If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### H

\[Describe if the client often has difficulty waiting his or her turn (e.g., while waiting in line). If applicable, describe how this applied to the client during childhood. If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### I

\[Describe if the client often interrupts or intrudes on others (e.g., butts into conversations, games, or activities; may start using other people’s things without asking or receiving permission; may intrude into or take over what others are doing.) If applicable, describe how this applied to the client during childhood. If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### Part 3: Impairment on Account of the Symptoms

### DSM-5-TR Criteria B

\[Describe in a few sentences if several of the inattentive or hyperactive-impulsive symptoms were present prior to the age of 12. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria C

\[Describe in a few sentences if several of the inattentive or hyperactive-impulsive symptoms are present in two or more settings (e.g., at home, academic setting, or work; with friends or relatives; in other activities). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate where these settings are. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria D

\[Describe in a few sentences if there is clear evidence that the inattentive or hyperactive-impulsive symptoms interfere with, or reduce the quality of, social, academic, or occupational functioning. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate how these symptoms interfere with, or reduce the quality of, social, academic, or occupational functioning. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria E

\[Print this statement:] *Before applying a diagnosis of ADHD, the clinician must ensure that the symptoms do not occur exclusively during the course of schizophrenia or another psychotic disorder and are not better explained by another mental disorder such as mood disorder, anxiety disorder, dissociative disorder, personality disorder, substance intoxication or withdrawal.*

### Clinical Interview

### Referral Reason

\[Describe in detail the client’s reasons for seeking the attention deficit hyperactivity disorder assessment. Describe the symptoms, behaviours, events, and situations that have led the client to be assessed for attention deficit hyperactivity disorder. Describe when the onset of specific issues occurred, and how the specific issues have changed over time. Describe the impact on the client’s daily functioning, such as at home, with family, at work, in education environments, with social relationships, with hobbies, and in any other area of the client’s life. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate any of the client’s specific issues.]

### People Present

* \[List the person or people who were in attendance during the interview, other than the clinician. Identify who the client is. State what the relationship is of the client to any other person in attendance (e.g., partner, carer, other relative, case worker).]

### Mental Status Examination (MSE)

\[Provide a paragraph using language consistent with accepted psychiatric and medical terms of no more than four sentences that summarises the client’s presentation using the following standard MSE domains (only include each domain if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual note, otherwise leave blank): Appearance (the client’s general physical appearance, hygiene, grooming, and attire); Behaviour (the client’s interaction with their surrounds, activity level, psychomotor activity, eye contact, and rapport; including any hyperactive behaviour or behaviour that is related to the client’s concentration abilities); Speech (the rate, volume, tone, coherence, and articulation of the client’s speech; including if the client communicates in any other non-verbal way, such as with gestures, noises, or eye contact); Mood and Affect (including the client’s reported mood and emotional state; including the range and appropriateness of the client’s emotional response during the appointment, and note any discrepancies with the client’s stated mood; including any observations about emotional expression made by the clinician; including any behaviours that provide insight into mood and affect, such as anger outbursts, withdrawal, and laughing; including any other factors that may be providing insight into the client’s mood or affect, such as expressions of illness, or other interactions with the clinician; including the response of the client to the use of any visual aids regarding mood or affect, such as mood scales); Thought Process (the client’s coherence, organisation, and flow of thoughts; including any magical / fantasy thinking; including any “flight of ideas” type thinking that may indicate that the client’s speech is unable to keep up with their thought processes); Thought Content (the content of the client’s thoughts, and note the presence of delusions, distortions, obsessions, or intrusive thoughts, if any; including any tangential thinking that the client is having); Perception (hallucinations or other perceptual disturbances of the client, if any; including if the client indicates that these experiences are distressing or impairing); Cognition (the client’s orientation to time / place / person, attention, memory, concentration, and comprehension; including the results of any stated cognitive assessment that has previously been carried out on the client; including the client’s attention and concentration abilities); and Insight and Judgment (the client's understanding of their own difficulties and challenges, noting any lack of awareness or denial).]

### Background

#### <u>Personal and Developmental History</u>

\[Describe as much detail as possible related to the client’s personal and developmental history. Include information related to the client’s early development, such as milestones and delays, such as crawling, walking, speech, and toileting; and emotional development. Describe the client’s education history, such as academic performance, school behaviour, being the victim or perpetrator of past bullying, and willingness to attend school. Describe the client’s gross and fine motor skills (e.g., handwriting, bike riding, ball skills).]

\[Describe the client’s family history and structure, current living arrangements (including the names of all people known to live at home with the client and their relationship to the client), and social support. If relevant, include any collateral information about siblings or other family members of the client.]

\[Describe any history of trauma, including family or intergenerational trauma, or significant adversity or any type of traumatic event.]

\[Describe any family history of any illness, medical condition, psychiatric illness, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, autism, and other neurodevelopmental conditions. Include any information about family suicides or family addiction behaviours, such as alcoholism, and other drug abuse.]

\[Describe the current circumstances of the client. Include information such as type of employment or education currently engaged in; current marital status; current friendships and other relationships; quality and satisfaction with relationships; current daily routine; and the current level of insight that the client has about the impact of symptoms on their everyday functioning. Describe any support that the client currently receives from any government or non-government source.]

#### <u>Previous Assessments</u>

\[Describe the results of any previous psychological, cognitive, or medical testing. Detail when any such assessments occurred, the name of the practitioner that administered the assessment, and the profession of the practitioner who administered the assessment. If no information about previous assessments is mentioned in the transcript, leave this section out.]

#### <u>Medical History</u>

\[Describe any previous medical, psychiatric, or neurodevelopmental diagnoses. Include information such as who made the diagnosis, their profession, and the approximate date that the diagnosis was given. If no information about medical history is mentioned in the transcript, leave this section out.]

#### <u>Current Medications</u>

\[Describe all the current medications that the client is taking, both psychotropic and non-psychotropic. Include information on doses and frequencies, and provide the reason why the client is on each medication. If no information about current medications is mentioned in the transcript, leave this section out.]

#### <u>Other Previous Treatment</u>

\[Describe any previous non-medication interventions that the client has participated in. Include things such as psychological therapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, and any other treatments that have been conducted by a registered health practitioner. If no information about other previous treatment is mentioned in the transcript, leave this section out.]

### Strengths, Interests, and Hobbies

\[Describe the client’s strengths, interests, and hobbies. Detail any unique skills that the client has, or any characteristics highlighted by the clinician or anyone else as being above average, desirable, or beneficial. Detail any of the client’s abilities described as gifted or advanced in comparison to others. Describe any of the client’s personality traits that the clinician or anyone else has highlighted as being desirable, advantageous or in some way positive.]

### Client Goals

\[Describe all the ways that the client would like to improve. According to the client, what would it take for them to flourish. This may include improved ability to form friendships; improved employment or academic outcomes; improvements to the client’s mood; improved ability to regulate other emotions such as anxiety and anger.]

### Clinician Observations, Notes, and Diagnostic Impressions

\[Describe any other observations or notes made by the clinician during the interview. This may include information related to their assessment, comments on anything that the client has said, comments in response to anything the client has done, or any other clinically relevant information provided by the clinician in the transcript. If provided, describe the clinician’s diagnosis, formulation, explanation of the client’s cognitive or neurodivergent profile, and observations about comorbidities, differential diagnosis, and co-occurring traits such as anxiety, trauma, executive dysfunction.]

### Risk

\[Based on clinician comments in the transcript, make a risk assessment in a statement (e.g., suicidal or homicidal ideation, self-harm, or harm to others, if any). Include any information related to past suicide attempts or past self-harming behaviour. Include any information about psychosocial stressors contributing to the level of risk. Describe any risk for social isolation. Describe any risk for being the victim or perpetrator of bullying, and include any stated history of bullying. Describe any protective factors that mitigate the risk.]

### Next Steps

* \[Specify if any evidence-based recommendations have been made by the clinician for further referral to a psychiatrist or other specialist regarding a medication review for the client. If the clinician is a qualified prescribing doctor, specify any evidence-based recommendations for new or existing medication, and specify if any existing medications should be continued or discontinued. Provide the clinician’s rationale for these recommendations. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* \[Specify any suggested non-pharmacological interventions made by the clinician, such as psychotherapy or lifestyle modifications. Provide the clinician’s rationale for these recommendations. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* \[Specify if any referrals to other specialist services have been or will be made by the clinician (e.g., lab tests). Provide the clinician’s rationale for these recommendations. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* \[Detail what the plan is for ongoing assessment, follow-up, and monitoring, as detailed by the clinician. If the clinician has not made any plans for ongoing assessment, follow-up, or monitoring, leave blank.]
* \[Detail if any Safety Plan has been made by the clinician in the event that the client poses a risk to safety, including steps to take in a crisis. Document the client’s understanding of the Safety Plan. If the clinician has not made any Safety Plan, leave blank.]
* \[Summarise any education provided by the clinician to the client about the client’s ongoing assessment, diagnosis, and treatment options. Document the client’s understanding and agreement with this education. If the clinician has not provided any education to the client about the ongoing assessment, diagnosis, and treatment options, leave blank.]
* \[List the details of any future appointment between the clinician and the client, including the time, day, and date.]

### Summary

\[Provide a one paragraph summary of the appointment with the client in no more than four sentences.]

\[END SECTION 1.]

\[START SECTION 2.]

\[Section 2 is only to be used when assessing a child, 17 years of age or younger.]

### Young DIVA-5 Questionnaire

### Part 1: Symptoms of Attention-Deficit

### DSM-5-TR Criteria A1

#### <u>Inattention – six or more of the following are required</u>

#### A

\[Describe if the child often fails to give close attention to details or makes careless mistakes in schoolwork, at work, or during other activities (e.g., overlooks or misses details, work is inaccurate). If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. \[Describe if the child often does not follow through on instructions and fails to finish schoolwork, chores, or duties in the workplace (e.g., starts tasks but quickly loses focus and is easily sidetracked). If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

\[Describe if the child often has difficulty organising tasks and activities (e.g., difficulty managing sequential tasks; difficulty keeping materials and belongings in order; messy, disorganised work; has poor time management; fails to meet deadlines). If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### F

\[Describe if the child often avoids, dislikes, or is reluctant to engage in tasks that require sustained mental effort (e.g., school work or homework; for older adolescents and adults, preparing reports, completing forms, reviewing lengthy papers). If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

\[Describe if the child often loses things necessary for tasks or activities (e.g., school materials, pencils, books, tools, wallets, keys, paperwork, eyeglasses, mobile telephones). If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

\[Describe if the child is often easily distracted by extraneous stimuli (for older adolescents and adults, this may include unrelated thoughts). If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. #### I
