Describe if the child is often forgetful in daily activities (e.g., doing chores, running errands; for older adolescents and adults, returning calls, paying bills, keeping appointments). If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### Part 2: Symptoms of Hyperactivity-Impulsivity

### DSM-5-TR Criteria A2

#### <u>Hyperactivity and Impulsivity – six or more of the following are required</u>

#### A

\[Describe if the child often fidgets with, or taps hands or feet, or squirms in seat. If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### B

\[Describe if the child often leaves seat in situations when remaining seated is expected (e.g., leaves his or her place in the classroom, in the office, or other workplace, or in other situations that require remaining in place). If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### C

\[Describe if the child often runs about or climbs in situations where it is inappropriate. Describe if the child says they often feel restless. If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### D

\[Describe if the child is often unable to play or engage in leisure activities quietly. If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### E

\[Describe if the child is often “on the go”, acting as if “driven by a motor” (e.g., is unable to be or uncomfortable being still for extended time, as in restaurants, meetings; may be experienced by others as being restless or difficult to keep up with). If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### F

\[Describe if the child often talks excessively. If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### G

\[Describe if the child often blurts out an answer before a question has been completed (e.g., completes people’s sentences; cannot wait for their turn in conversation). If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

#### H

\[Describe if the child often has difficulty waiting his or her turn (e.g., while waiting in line). If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

I

\[Describe if the child often interrupts or intrudes on others (e.g., butts into conversations, games, or activities; may start using other people’s things without asking or receiving permission; may intrude into or take over what others are doing). If applicable, provide relevant real-life examples from the transcript. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### Part 3: Impairment on Account of the Symptoms

### DSM-5-TR Criteria B

\[Describe in a few sentences if several of the inattentive or hyperactive-impulsive symptoms were present prior to the age of 12. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria C

\[Describe in a few sentences if several of the inattentive or hyperactive-impulsive symptoms are present in two or more settings (e.g., at home, academic setting, or work; with friends or relatives; in other activities). Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate where these settings are. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria D

\[Describe in a few sentences if there is clear evidence that the inattentive or hyperactive-impulsive symptoms interfere with, or reduce the quality of, social, academic, or occupational functioning. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate how these symptoms interfere with, or reduce the quality of, social, academic, or occupational functioning. If no information about this diagnostic criterion is found in the transcript or contextual notes, print “Information related to this diagnostic criterion not found in the transcript.”]

### DSM-5-TR Criteria E

\[Print this statement:] *Before applying a diagnosis of ADHD, the clinician must ensure that the symptoms do not occur exclusively during the course of schizophrenia or another psychotic disorder and are not better explained by another mental disorder such as mood disorder, anxiety disorder, dissociative disorder, personality disorder, substance intoxication or withdrawal.*

### Clinical Interview

### Referral Reason

\[Describe in detail the parent or carer’s reasons for seeking the attention deficit hyperactivity disorder assessment. Describe the symptoms, behaviours, events, and situations that have led the parent or carer to have the child assessed for attention deficit hyperactivity disorder. Describe when the onset of specific issues occurred, and how the specific issues have changed over time. Describe the impact on the child’s daily functioning, such as at home, with family, at school, with social relationships, with hobbies, and in any other area of the child’s life. Describe the child’s response to or perspective on any of these issues. Provide examples from the transcript that illustrate any of the child’s specific issues.]

### People Present

* \[List the person or people who were in attendance during the interview, other than the clinician. State what the relationship of the person or people are to the child (e.g., parent, carer, other relative, case worker). Confirm that the child being assessed was in attendance or not.]

### Mental Status Examination (MSE)

\[If the child being assessed was not in attendance during the interview, do not include this Mental Status Examination.]

\[If the child being assessed was in attendance during the interview, do include this Mental Status Examination.]

\[Provide a paragraph of no more than four sentences using language consistent with accepted psychiatric and medical terms, and taking into account the child's developmental stage, that summarises the client’s presentation using the following standard MSE domains (only include if each domain is explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual note, otherwise leave blank): Appearance (the child’s general physical appearance, hygiene, grooming, and attire); Behaviour (the child’s interaction with their surrounds, activity level, psychomotor activity, eye contact, and rapport; including information about the child’s interactions with any toys, creative behaviours such as drawing, and how the parent or carer has influenced the child’s behaviour in the session; including any hyperactive behaviour or behaviour that is related to the child’s concentration abilities); Speech (the rate, volume, tone, coherence, and articulation of the child’s speech; including if the child communicates in any other non-verbal way, such as with gestures, noises, or eye contact); Mood and Affect (the child’s reported mood and emotional state; including the range and appropriateness of the child’s emotional response during the appointment, and note any discrepancies with the child’s stated mood; including any observations about emotional expression made by the clinician; including any behaviours that provide insight into mood and affect, such as tantrums, withdrawal, and laughing; including any other factors that may be providing insight into the child’s mood or affect, such as expressions of illness, contents of drawings and other creative play, and interactions with the clinician, or parent or carer; including the response of the child to the use of any visual aids regarding mood or affect, such as mood scales); Thought Process (the child’s coherence, organisation, and flow of thoughts; including any magical / fantasy thinking, the child referring to any imaginary friends, or the child’s belief in any supernatural beings such as Santa Claus, which does not have to indicate the presence of psychotic thinking, but may match the child’s developmental stage and / or how the child is responding to any external life events; including any “flight of ideas” type thinking that may indicate that the child’s speech is unable to keep up with their thought processes); Thought Content (the content of the child’s thoughts, and note the presence of delusions, distortions, obsessions, or intrusive thoughts, if any; including any magical / fantasy thinking, the child referring to any imaginary friends, or the child’s belief in any supernatural beings such as Santa Claus, which does not have to indicate the presence of psychotic thinking, but may match the child’s developmental stage and / or how the child is responding to any external life events; including any tangential thinking that the child is having); Perception (hallucinations or other perceptual disturbances of the child, if any; including if the child indicates that these experiences are distressing, impairing, or inconsistent with typical play themes for a child of similar age; including the presence of any stated nightmares or night terrors, and distinguish these from any mistaken belief that they are considered to be perceptual disturbances); Cognition (the child’s orientation to time / place / person, attention, memory, concentration, and comprehension; including the results of any stated cognitive assessment that has previously been carried out on the child; including the child’s attention and concentration abilities); and Insight and Judgment (the child's understanding of their own difficulties and challenges, noting any age-appropriate lack of awareness or denial).]

### Background

#### <u>Personal and Developmental History</u>

\[Describe as much detail as possible related to the child’s personal and developmental history. Include information related to the child’s early development, such as issues with pregnancy and birth; milestones and delays, such as crawling, walking, speech and toileting; and emotional development. Describe the child’s education history, such as academic performance, school behaviour, being the victim or perpetrator of past bullying, and willingness to attend school. Describe the child’s gross and fine motor skills (e.g., handwriting, bike riding, ball skills).]

\[Describe the child’s family history and structure, current living arrangements (including the names of all people known to live at home with the child and their relationship to the child), and social support. If relevant, include any collateral information about siblings or other family members living at home with the child.]

\[Describe any history of trauma, including family or intergenerational trauma, or significant adversity or any type of traumatic event.]

\[Describe any family history of any illness, medical condition, psychiatric illness, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, autism, and other neurodevelopmental conditions. Include any information about family suicides or family addiction behaviours, such as alcoholism, and other drug abuse.]

\[Describe the current circumstances of the child. Include information such as name and place of current school; current school grade; current status of friendships; current daily routine; and the current level of insight that the child has about the impact of symptoms on their everyday functioning.]

#### <u>Previous Assessments</u>

\[Describe the results of any previous psychological, cognitive, or medical testing. Detail when any such assessments occurred, the name of the practitioner that administered the assessment, and the profession of the practitioner who administered the assessment. If no information about previous assessments is mentioned in the transcript, leave this section out.]

#### <u>Medical History</u>

\[Describe any previous medical, psychiatric, or neurodevelopmental diagnoses. Include information such as who made the diagnosis, their profession, and the approximate date that the diagnosis was given. If no information about medical history is mentioned in the transcript, leave this section out.]

#### <u>Current Medications</u>

\[Describe all the current medications that the child is taking, both psychotropic and non-psychotropic. Include information on doses and frequencies, and provide the reason why the child is on each medication. If no information about current medications is mentioned in the transcript, leave this section out.]

#### <u>Other Previous Treatment</u>

\[Describe any previous non-medication interventions that the child has participated in. Include things such as psychological therapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, and any other treatments that have been conducted by a registered health practitioner. If no information about other previous treatment is mentioned in the transcript, leave this section out.]

### Strengths, Interests, and Hobbies

\[Describe the child’s strengths, interests, and hobbies. Detail any unique skills that the child has, or any characteristics highlighted by the clinician or parent or carer as being above average, desirable, or beneficial. Detail any of the child’s abilities described as gifted or advanced in comparison to same-aged peers. Describe any of the child’s personality traits that the clinician or parent or carer state as being desirable, advantageous or in some way positive.]

### Parent / Caregiver and Client Goals

\[Describe all the ways that the parent or caregiver would like to see the child improve. According to the parent or caregiver, what would it take for the child to flourish. This may include improved ability to form friendships; improved academic engagement and performance; improvements to the child’s mood; improved ability to regulate other emotions such as anxiety and anger. Describe the child’s response to or perspective on any of these issues.]

### Clinician Observations, Notes, and Diagnostic Impressions

\[Describe any other observations or notes made by the clinician during the interview. This may include information related to their assessment, comments on anything that the parent or caregiver has said, comments in response to anything the child has done, or any other clinically relevant information provided by the clinician in the transcript. If provided, describe the clinician’s diagnosis, explanation of the child’s cognitive or neurodivergent profile, and observations about comorbidities, differential diagnosis, and co-occurring traits such as anxiety, trauma, executive dysfunction.]

### Risk

\[Based on clinician comments in the transcript, make a risk assessment in a statement (e.g., suicidal or homicidal ideation, self-harm, or harm to others, if any). Include any information related to past suicide attempts or past self-harming behaviour. Include any information about psychosocial stressors contributing to the level of risk. Describe any risk for social isolation. Describe any risk for being the victim or perpetrator of bullying, and include any stated history of bullying. Describe any protective factors that mitigate the risk.]

### Next Steps

* \[Specify if any evidence-based recommendations have been made by the clinician for further referral to a paediatrician, psychiatrist, or other specialist regarding a medication review for the child. If the clinician is a qualified prescribing doctor, specify if any evidence-based recommendations for new or existing medications have been given, and specify if the clinician has recommended that any existing medications be continued or discontinued. Provide the clinician’s rationale for these recommendations. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* \[Specify any suggested non-pharmacological interventions made by the clinician, such as psychotherapy or lifestyle modifications. Provide the clinician’s rationale for these recommendations. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* \[Specify if any referrals to other specialist services have been or will be made by the clinician (e.g., lab tests). Provide the clinician’s rationale for these recommendations. If the clinician has not made any such recommendations, leave blank.]
* \[Detail what the plan is for ongoing assessment, follow-up, and monitoring, as detailed by the clinician. If the clinician has not made any plans for ongoing assessment, follow-up, or monitoring, leave blank.]
* \[Detail if any Safety Plan has been made by the clinician in the event that the child poses a risk to safety, including steps to take in a crisis. Document the parent or caregiver’s understanding of the Safety Plan. If the clinician has not made any Safety Plan, leave blank.]
* \[Summarise any education provided by the clinician to the parent or caregiver or child client about the child’s ongoing assessment, diagnosis, and treatment options. Document the parent or caregiver’s understanding and agreement with this education. If the clinician has not provided any education to the parent or caregiver about the ongoing assessment, diagnosis, and treatment options, leave blank.]
* \[List the details of any future appointment between the clinician and the parent or caregiver, or the child, including the time, day, and date.]

### Summary

\[Provide a one paragraph summary of the appointment in no more than four sentences.]

\[END SECTION 2.]

title: Drug and Alcohol Initial Assessment
description: For use by a clinician working in a drug and alcohol counselling environment, this template provides a detailed summary of a first session / initial
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by a clinician working in a drug and alcohol counselling environment. The template is to be used to summarise a first session, or initial assessment, between a clinician and a client. After analysing the transcript from a first session / initial assessment between a therapist and a client, you will generate a detailed summary of the session. Include detailed information that is considered appropriate and necessary in the context of evidence-based drug and alcohol counselling. Use terms and language that are consistent with evidence-based drug and alcohol counselling. Organise the summary under the following headings:)

[presenting_concerns_and_background_infor] Presenting Concerns and Background Information

\[Provide client identifying information, such as name, age, gender, cultural background, pronouns (if shared). Describe client’s reason for seeking counselling (self-referred, mandated, recommended by a healthcare provider). Describe relevant medical and mental health history, including diagnosed conditions. Describe current living situation and support network (e.g., family, friends, case managers). Describe the client’s current employment status. Describe any history of trauma. Describe the client's specific concerns related to current substance use.]

[substance_use_history_and_pattern_of_use] Substance Use History and Pattern of Use

\[Describe the types of substances used (e.g., alcohol, cannabis, stimulants, opioids, prescription drugs, etc.). Describe the route of administration (e.g., oral, intravenous, smoking). Describe the frequency, duration, and quantity of use (e.g., daily, weekly, binge episodes). Describe the duration of use (e.g., when it started, escalation over time). Describe the context of use (e.g., alone, social use, work-related). Describe the most recent use (including time of last use, withdrawal symptoms if any). Describe history of previous treatment or attempts to reduce use. Describe any other treatments that the client is currently involved in (e.g., medication type and dose, group therapy, relationship counselling).]

[impact_of_substance_use] Impact of Substance Use

\[Describe the impact of substance use on the client’s physical health (e.g., liver issues, respiratory issues, sleep disturbances, weight changes), mental health (e.g., mood, anxiety, psychosis, cognitive effects), socialising (e.g., relationships, family dynamics, social withdrawal), employment or education (e.g., employment status, performance issues, absenteeism), illegal or criminal activity (e.g., drink driving charges, arrests, probation), finances (e.g., spending on substances, financial stress).]

[withdrawal_and_dependence_indicators] Withdrawal and Dependence Indicators

\[Describe the client’s tolerance levels (e.g., increased amounts needed for the same effect); withdrawal symptoms (e.g., tremors, nausea, sweating, anxiety, agitation); failed attempts to cut down or control use; time spent obtaining, using, or recovering from substances.]

[motivational_interviewing_and_readiness_] Motivational Interviewing and Readiness for Change

\[Describe the client’s stage of change (Precontemplation, Contemplation, Preparation, Action, Maintenance). Describe the presence of any ambivalence or concerns about stopping or reducing use. Describe the client’s personal goals regarding substance use. Describe previous attempts to change (including any positive or successful outcomes) and barriers encountered.]

[risk] Risk Assessment

\[Describe any suicidal ideation or self-harm risk. Describe any risk of overdose (e.g., history of overdose, polysubstance use, fentanyl exposure). Describe any harm reduction strategies in place (e.g., safe use practices, naloxone availability, support system). Describe any domestic violence or safety concerns. Describe any protective factors that mitigate the risk. Describe any safety plans that are in place in response to any risk.]

[strengths] Strengths

\[Describe any existing coping strategies that the client utilises. Describe the client’s support from family, friends, or community (including involvement in any community organisations). Describe the client’s engagement in work, hobbies, healthy recreational interests, or other meaningful activities. Describe past periods of successful abstinence or reduction for the client.]

[assessment_and_testing] Assessment and Testing

\[Describe the results of any psychometric testing, self-report questionnaires, or surveys. Describe any future plans for further assessment by the clinician.]

[treatment_plan_and_next_steps] Treatment Plan and Next Steps

* \[Describe any agreed treatment goals (e.g., abstinence, harm reduction, stabilisation) between the clinician and the client. Describe any planned interventions described by the clinician (e.g., cognitive behavioural therapy, motivational interviewing, relapse prevention, medication-assisted treatment). Describe any other referral needs to specialist services (e.g., medical detox, psychiatric evaluation, social services). Describe follow-up session details (e.g., time and date of next appointment) and ongoing support plan. Describe any activities that the client has agreed to do to help address the presenting issues.]

sections.label: Substance Use History and Pattern of Use
Motivational Interviewing and Readiness for Change

title: Eating Disorder Session (Adult)
description: For clinicians providing eating disorder treatment to adults in individual therapy, this template provides a structured summary of a session based on
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by clinicians working within Cognitive Behavioural Therapy for Eating Disorders (CBT-E), Dialectical Behaviour Therapy (DBT) and Interpersonal Therapy (IPT) approaches to the treatment of eating disorders in adults. Using the transcript from the session, generate a detailed summary of the session. Ensure the summary is concise and adheres to CBT, DBT and IPT terminology. Include direct quotes from the transcript if they are pivotal to the therapeutic process. Organise the summary under the following headings. Do not print out any title at the top of the output.)

[mse] Mental Status Examination

* Behaviour: [Observe the client's activity level, interaction with their surroundings, and any unique or notable behaviors (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank).]
* Speech: [If the pace of speech was fast or slow for the speaker who is the client, note the pace of speech for the client, where a “normal pace of speech” is between 130 and 200 words per minute, “fast pace of speech” is defined as 201 words per minute or over, and “slow pace of speech” is defined as less than 129 words per minute. Only include a note on the pace of speech if it is fast or slow, if it is normal, do not include it in the note]
* Mood: [Record the client's self-described emotional state, using their own words if possible (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank).]
* Affect: [Describe the range and appropriateness of the client's emotional response during the examination, noting any discrepancies with the stated mood (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank).]
* Thoughts: [Assess the client's thought process and thought content, noting any distortions, delusions, or preoccupations (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank).]
* Perceptions: [Note any reported hallucinations or sensory misinterpretations, specifying type and impact on the client (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank).]
* Cognition: [Describe the client's memory, orientation to time/place/person, concentration, and comprehension (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank).]
* Insight: [Describe the client's understanding of their own condition and symptoms, noting any lack of awareness or denial (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank).]
* Judgment: [Describe the client's decision-making ability and understanding of the consequences of their actions (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank).]

[presentation] Presenting Concerns

* [Summarise the client’s main concerns or difficulties discussed in the session, including any changes in eating disorder symptoms since the last session (e.g., restriction, binge eating, purging, compulsive exercise, body image distress).]
* [Detail any impacts on the client’s physical health, mood, and daily functioning.]
* [Note any relevant life events or stressors influencing eating disorder behaviours.]

[homework_review] Homework Review

* [Summarise the client’s completion of self-monitoring records, including patterns of eating, thoughts, emotions, and any compensatory behaviours. Note any key observations from self-monitoring, including changes in eating behaviour or emotional triggers.]
* [Document the weekly weighing, including any education provided. (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank).]

[key_content_discussed] Key Content Discussed

* [Summarise core issues explored and how they relate to the client’s presentation and treatment goals.]

[cbt_e_formulation] CBT-E Formulation

(Below is a model of a CBT-E formulation. Use the CBT-E formulation model with the five subheadings below to describe the client’s circumstances discussed.)

* [Overevaluation of shape and weight: [Describe any discussions about the client’s preoccupation with weight, shape, and their control.]
* [Dietary restraint: [Summarise rigid food rules, avoidance of certain foods, patterns of restriction discussed, or other weight-control behaviour.]
* [Binge/purge cycles: [Detail any patterns of binge eating and/or compensatory behaviours such as vomiting, laxative use and excessive exercise, and factors maintaining these behaviours.]
* [Events and mood change: [Describe the triggering external circumstances or events and associated changes in mood and disordered eating behaviours. Describe any coping mechanisms discussed.]
* [Perfectionism, core beliefs, or interpersonal difficulties: [Describe any perfectionistic tendencies, over-evaluation of achieving and achievement, core beliefs, or relational difficulties impacting the client’s eating disorder.]

[interventions] Interventions

* [Detail which specific therapeutic interventions were applied (e.g., CBT-E, DBT and IPT techniques). Document CBT-E techniques such as cognitive restructuring (CBT-E), DBT techniques such as distress tolerance and emotion regulation skills, meal planning, behavioural experiments or exposure to feared foods, or psychoeducation. Summarise how these interventions were received and any feedback from the client, including level of understanding shown. Detail any notable shifts in thinking or behaviour expressed by client. (Use as many dot points as needed to list separate interventions.)]
* [Describe any ideas, concepts or frameworks that were introduced, or the content of any handouts or use of whiteboard)(use as many bullet points as needed to capture all the details discussed) (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank)
* [Summarise the client’s response to interventions, engagement level, and any insights gained. Include motivation for change, ambivalence, or resistance.]

[treatment_progress] Treatment Progress

* [Summarise the client’s progress toward treatment goals and any identified barriers. Document changes in eating behaviours, thought patterns, or emotional regulation.]
* [Highlight important themes, key insights or realisations from the session, including any shifts in the client’s understanding of their eating disorder and progress toward treatment goals.]

[therapeutic_relationship] Therapeutic Relationship

* [Briefly comment on the therapeutic alliance or rapport between therapist and patient observed in the session. Note any areas where rapport was strengthened or challenges arose.]

[risk] Risk Assessment

* [Summarise any risk factors, including self-harm, suicidal ideation, or medical instability. List protective factors. Note any action plans, referrals, or coordination with other healthcare providers discussed if risk is present. (only include if mentioned in the transcript otherwise leave blank).]

[summary] Summary

* [Provide a concise recap of key points from the session in no more than four sentences that summarise the session.]

[nextsteps] Plan

* Homework: [Describe any homework or behavioural experiments assigned, such as food challenges, cognitive restructuring exercises, or alternative coping strategies. Include the rationale and expected outcomes, as well as any client concerns or barriers to completion. (only include if mentioned in the transcript otherwise leave blank).]
* Next Session: [Outline the preliminary plan for the next session based on today’s discussion, and any adjustments to the treatment approach based on the session. Provide the time, day and date of any future session agreed upon between the therapist and the client.]

title: EMDR Therapy
description: This Eye Movement Desensitisation and Reprocessing structured template is for standardised documentation of EMDR sessions, aligned with the 8-phase
prompt:
(Use the template below to generate a session note summarising an EMDR therapy session. Ensure the summary is aligned with the 8-phase protocol and provides a clear record of therapy progress and therapeutic outcomes. Use professional and clinical language that adheres to EMDR concepts, and incorporate trauma-informed principles. Structure the summary using the headings provided below.)

[session_overview] Session Overview

* Phase of EMDR protocol: [e.g., history-taking, preparation, assessment, desensitisation, installation, body scan, closure, re-evaluation]
* Focus of the session: [target memory or issue] (only include if mentioned in a clinical note, transcript or contextual note, otherwise leave blank)
* [Current life circumstances impacting therapy or life] (only include if mentioned in a clinical note, transcript or contextual note, otherwise leave blank)

[target_memory_and_components] Target Memory and Components

[There may be more than one target memory, if so, repeat the structure below for each target memory. Use as many dot points as necessary.]

* Target Memory/Incident:
* Negative Cognition:
* Positive Cognition (PC):
* Validity of Cognition (VOC) Rating:
* Emotions Associated with Memory:
* Subjective Units of Disturbance (SUD) Rating (0–10): (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)
    -Body Sensations (Somatic Awareness): (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)

[processing_details] Processing Details

* Bilateral Stimulation (BLS) method used (e.g., eye movements, taps, tones):
* Client’s response during BLS (e.g., thoughts, emotions, sensations, shifts):
* Observed themes, patterns, or insights:
* Adjustments to BLS or processing approach:
* New associations or memory links:

[outcome_of_processing] Outcome of Processing

* SUD Rating at End of Session: (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, otherwise leave blank.)
* VOC Rating at End of Session:(only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, otherwise leave blank.)
* Reduction/change in distress or shifts in perspective: (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)
* Changes in emotional or somatic reactions: (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)

[installation_phase] Installation Phase

* Reinforced Positive Cognition (PC):
* Strength of VOC after installation (1–7):
* Client’s acceptance of PC and any barriers:

[body_scan_phase] Body Scan Phase

* Somatic feedback: \[e\.g\.\, residual tension\, relaxation\]\(only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript\, otherwise leave blank\.\)
* Remaining disturbances or neutral sensations: (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, otherwise leave blank.)
* Integration of Positive Cognition into body awareness:(only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, otherwise leave blank.)

[closure] Closure

* Techniques used for closure (e.g., grounding exercises, visualisation):
* Client’s emotional state at session end:
* Homework or between-session activities assigned: (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, otherwise leave blank.)
* Psychoeducation or coping strategies discussed: (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, otherwise leave blank.)

[re_evaluation] Re-evaluation

(Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes or clinical note, otherwise do not include section)

* Review of previously processed targets: (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, otherwise do not include)
* Client’s current perspective on past targets: (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, otherwise do not include)
* Any emerging memories or themes for future sessions: (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, otherwise do not include)

[therapist_s_reflections] Therapist’s Reflections

(Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, otherwise do not include section)

* \[Observations about the session’s flow and effectiveness\]\(only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript\, otherwise do not include\)
* \[Client’s engagement and responses to interventions\]\(only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript\, otherwise do not include\)
* \[Considerations for future sessions or adjustments\]\(only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript\, otherwise do not include\)

title: Emotion Focused Therapy (EFT)
description: For clinicians practising Emotion Focused Therapy (EFT) to document client sessions, this template incorporates EFT’s principles of emotional
prompt:
(The template below is designed to generate session progress notes for therapists using Emotion Focused Therapy (EFT) with clients in individual or couples therapy. Using the session transcript, produce a concise, professional summary of the session using EFT terminology and concepts. Include direct quotes when they are pivotal to the therapeutic process. Organise the summary under the headings below.)

[presentation] Presenting Concerns

* [Summarise the client's main concerns or difficulties discussed in the session, using precise emotional language rather than general terms (e.g., "client described feeling shame about being defective" rather than "low self-esteem"). Document recent triggers or stressors affecting the client's emotional state. Record any changes since previous session.]

[emotional_schemes_and_patterns] Emotional Schemes and Patterns

* [1. Describe any emotional schemes identified and explored in the session, 2. describe how these present in the client's current life, and 3. link them to any developmental experiences if discussed, for example "Identified abandonment scheme triggered by partner's work travel, explored origins of scheme in early loss of father". Note connections between current reactions and past experiences if discussed. Document any shifts in maladaptive emotional schemes.]

[interventions] Interventions

* [Document all of the specific therapeutic interventions and techniques used, including Emotion Focused Therapy techniques such as empty chair work, two-chair dialogue, focusing on bodily felt sense, systematic evocative unfolding. For each intervention, you must specify: 1) the rationale for using it or purpose, 2) what it targeted or what was explored, 3) how it was implemented, and 4) the client's response, including any insights, shifts in awareness or understanding, or resistance.] (use as many bullet points as needed to capture all therapeutic interventions)
* [Note EFT markers addressed, such as unclear felt sense, self-critical split, unfinished business, vulnerability, and interventions used.]

[emotional_processing] Emotional Processing

* [Summarise the emotional processing and transformation in the session. Provide specific examples that show the client's journey from discussing emotions to actually experiencing them in the body. Identify specific emotions accessed: primary adaptive, primary maladaptive, or secondary emotions, noting any transformation from secondary to primary emotions. Note emotional shifts where one emotion transforms another - e.g., adaptive anger replacing stuck sadness, or healthy grief emerging from defensive numbness. Document moments of deeper emotional contact and any breakthroughs with detail.]
* Somatic and Bodily Experience: [Document specific bodily sensations the client reported and link these to emotional states (if described, otherwise leave blank). Note any tension, sensations, or physical manifestations of emotional states observed or reported. Describe how attention to bodily experience facilitated emotional processing or provided information about emotional needs - using examples. Document the client's developing ability to track and stay with felt sense as a guide to emotional experience.]

[progress_emotional_transformation_and_in] Progress, Emotional Transformation, and Integration

* [Summarise the client's progress in the core EFT principle of using adaptive emotions to transform maladaptive ones. Note any changes in emotional awareness, regulation, and the client's ability to access primary adaptive emotions, using examples from the session to demonstrate. Document changes in emotional flexibility and the client's capacity to stay with and process difficult emotions. Note any symptom changes. Document any integration of emotional experiences into the client’s narrative and life.]

[therapeutic_relationship] Therapeutic Relationship

* [Briefly comment on therapeutic alliance and client's engagement in one to two sentences.]

[risk] Risk Assessment

* [Document any risk factors, such as self-harm or suicidal ideation. (if current suicidal thoughts or behaviours are present, highlight text in red). List protective factors and any action plans if risk is present. (Only include if mentioned in the transcript otherwise exclude subsection)]

[homework_between_session_tasks] Homework/Between-Session Tasks

* [Describe homework or tasks assigned to the client, including rationale and expected outcomes. Note any feedback or concerns from the client about the assignments. (Only include if mentioned in the transcript otherwise exclude subsection)]

[nextsteps] Next Steps

* [Outline focus for next session based on today's emotional processing work.]
* [Provide time, day, and date of next session if mentioned.]

[summary] Summary

* [In a paragraph, provide a concise 2-4 sentence recap that summarises the key points from the session.]

title: Family Therapy
description: Designed for family therapy approaches including structural, strategic, narrative, CBT-informed and solution-focused family therapy. This template
prompt:
(Provide a progress note for a clinician facilitating a family therapy session, which may include systemic family therapy, CBT-informed, structural family therapy, narrative therapy, solution-focused family therapy, or other evidence-based family therapeutic approaches. The transcript contains multiple family members and the therapist. When documenting statements, concerns, or observations, clearly identify who made each point using their relationship and name where available (e.g., "Sarah (mother) expressed...", "Therapist explored..."). Keep documentation concise and focused on clinically relevant information. Avoid repetitive content across sections. Use the transcript, notes and contextual notes to summarise the session, using family therapy terminology and reflecting family systems concepts, relational patterns, and therapeutic interventions. Organise the summary according to the subheadings below.) (Start the output at Family Composition and Attendees, do not print anything else prior) [family_composition_and_attendees] Family Composition and Attendees * [List family members present in session with their relationship to the family system and names where provided (e.g., Sarah (mother), David (father), Alex (daughter, age 15)). Use only actual names and roles - never include transcript speaker labels like "Speaker A" or "Speaker B" in the attendee list. Note any absent family members if discussed.] [presenting_concerns_and_goals] Presenting Concerns and Goals [Summarise the family's primary concerns, conflicts, or difficulties discussed in the session. Include how different family members describe the presenting issues and any differences in perspective. Detail any significant family events, transitions, or stressors that have occurred between sessions. Note any shifts or developments since the previous session. (Only include information explicitly mentioned in the transcript, note or contextual note.)] [content] Session Content [Review of homework/family practices: Briefly document progress with previously assigned family exercises, communication tasks, or behavioural changes, including barriers and successes. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual note, otherwise leave blank.)] [Key topics discussed: Summarise the main clinically relevant issues explored during the session and their relevance to treatment goals. Note any significant patterns, cycles, or dynamics that emerged.] [family_systems_assessment_and_dynamics] Family Systems Assessment and Dynamics [Describe key systemic patterns observed including communication styles, boundaries, alliances, family rules, roles, and any notable strengths or dysfunctional patterns. Include relevant theoretical conceptualisation as appropriate.] [therapeutic_alliance_and_engagement] Therapeutic Alliance and Engagement [Briefly note the engagement level of each family member, therapeutic relationship quality, and any alliance issues. Document any resistance, ambivalence, or collaborative moments observed during the session.] [interventions] Interventions [Provide specific detail about all therapeutic interventions used and the therapeutic rationale. This may include family therapy techniques like systemic questioning, reframing, enactments, boundary-making interventions, externalising conversations, scaling questions, miracle questions, genograms, family sculpting, or psychoeducation. For each intervention, identify the specific technique, specify the therapeutic modality, detail what was targeted (e.g., rigid boundaries, negative cycle, problem story), and summarise how family members responded, including any notable shifts in understanding or interaction patterns.] (Note: provide sufficient detail for another family therapist to continue treatment. Use as many bullet points as needed for separate interventions) [systemic_themes_and_progress] Systemic Themes and Progress [Provide a concise summary of key themes and progress. Focus on clinically significant insights and changes. Include any shifts in the family's understanding of their difficulties, relational patterns, or progress toward therapy goals. Note any changes in family narratives, interaction patterns, or problem-solving approaches.] [Describe the family's progress toward therapeutic goals. Identify areas where progress is evident and areas that remain challenging. Note any improvements in communication, conflict resolution, emotional connection, parenting effectiveness, or other targeted systemic changes.] [homework_or_family_tasks] Homework or Family Tasks [Describe homework, experiments, or tasks assigned to the family, including rationale and expected outcomes. Note any feedback or concerns from family members about the assignments. Specify which family members are responsible for particular tasks if relevant. (Only include if mentioned in the transcript otherwise leave blank)] [risk] Risk Assessment [Summarise any safety concerns, risk factors, or child protection issues identified. Include any risk management strategies implemented or planned. Note protective factors within the family system. (Only include if mentioned in the transcript otherwise leave blank)] [nextsteps] Next Steps [Outline the preliminary agenda or therapeutic focus for the next session based on today's discussion. Include any specific family members who need to attend or particular systemic issues to address. Provide the time, day and date of any future session agreed upon.] [summary] Summary [Provide a brief recap of key points from the session in no more than three sentences, summarising the family dynamics explored, interventions used, systemic shifts observed, and treatment direction.]
(Review the entire note to confirm that statements are consistently attributed to the correct individual throughout the note, including both family members and the therapist. Correct any inconsistencies to maintain clear and accurate attribution.)

title: First Session
description: This initial session template is designed for use when conducting a thorough assessment appointment with general mental health clients. It covers a
prompt:
(This template will be used to provide a detailed summary of a first session or initial assessment between a mental health clinician and a client. Use language and tone that reflects accepted evidence-based mental health assessment and treatment practices. You will strictly adhere to the following layout:)

### Intake and Consent

[Detail any discussions around referrals, confidentiality, limits of confidentiality, informed consent, fees or rebates, and the nature of the services provided or introductory administrative remarks by the clinician (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]

### Presenting Problem(s)

* [Detail the presenting problems as discussed by the clinician and the client. Describe the issues and associated stressors.]

### History of Presenting Problem(s)

* [Detail the history of the presenting problems and include onset, duration, course, and severity of the symptoms or problems (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]

### Current Functioning

* **Occupation:** [Detail the client's current employment or educational status. Do they find their occupation satisfying or challenging and how does it interact with their problems/symptoms. Describe what type of work the client does, and detail if it is full time or part time (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Family**: [Detail the client's family dynamics and relationships. Who lives in the home? Do they know about the presenting problem? Capture names, ages of family members and the relationships with each other (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Social:** [Describe the client's social relationships, interactions and the client's social supports. If mentioned, include romantic relationships (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Hobbies/Interests:** [Describe the client's hobbies, recreational activities, interests or what they typically do in their free time. Describe any interaction that the client's symptoms have on their hobbies, interests or what they do for fun or on the weekend (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Sleep:** [Detail the client's sleep patterns. Bedtime, wake times, sleep onset, sleep quality, energy levels, and how the presenting problems have affected sleep patterns (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Energy Levels:** [Detail the client's energy levels or fatigue throughout the day. Describe the interaction of the presenting problem symptoms on energy levels or fatigue (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Eating/Appetite:** [Detail the client's eating habits and appetite (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Physical Health:** [Detail the client's physical health, exercise routines or physical activities. Describe the interaction of the client's presenting problem symptoms on exercise or physical activity (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]

### Current Medications

* [List type, frequency, and daily dose of the client's current medications (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]

### Psychiatric History

* [Detail the client's psychiatric history, if applicable, including hospitalisations, treatment from psychiatrists, psychological treatment, counselling, and past medications – type, frequency and dose (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* [Detail any other mental health interventions not mentioned above. Describe any personal philosophies about mental health or psychology and how these interact with the client's symptoms or problems (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]

### Medical History

* [Detail the client's personal and family medical history, including history of family illness (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]

### Personal History

#### Family of Origin:

* [Detail the client's family of origin, including names, occupations, personalities and circumstances surrounding parents, grandparents, brothers or sisters, including the nature of the relationship with the client, especially the relationships and situations growing up (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]

#### Cultural or Religious Background:

* [Detail the client's cultural background, country of origin, religious, or spiritual background (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]

#### Relationship History:

* [Detail the client's romantic relationship history and any issues, history of boyfriends, girlfriends, marriages, divorces or sexual orientation (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]

#### Developmental History:

* [Detail the client's developmental milestones and any issues, childhood health problems, social problems, childhood assessments, and any other issues related to the client's upbringing (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]

#### Educational History:

* [Detail the client's educational history, including name of schools, school grades, other school or education achievements, relationships with peers, experience of school, and any other school or education issues (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]

#### Forensic/Legal History:

* [Detail the client's forensic or legal history, and involvement with police, lawyers, and the legal system (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]

#### Substance Use:

* [Detail the client's current and past recreational drug or alcohol use, including any misuse of prescription medication, and include the type of substance and frequency) (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]

### Risk Assessment

* **Suicidal Ideation:** [Detail the client's history, attempts, and plans in relation to suicidal ideation. If there is current suicidal ideation or behaviours, highlight text in red (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Homicidal Ideation:** [Describe any homicidal ideation by the client. If there is current or past homicidal ideation or behaviours, highlight text in red (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Self-harm:** [Detail any history of self-harm by the client. If there is current self harm or ideation, highlight text in red (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Violence & Aggression:** [Describe any incidents or accusations of violence or aggression by the client (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Risk-taking/Impulsivity:** [Describe any risk-taking behaviours or impulsivity by the client (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, otherwise exclude section).]

### Mental State Exam

* **Behaviour:** [Describe the client's behaviour (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section.]
* **Speech:** [Describe the client's speech patterns, e.g., slow, fast (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Mood:** [Describe the client's mood. Use sentiment analysis of words spoken to assess for mood (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Affect:** [Describe the client's affect. Use sentiment analysis of words spoken to assess the client's affect. If applicable, include a quote (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Perception:** [Describe any hallucinations or dissociations (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Thought Process:** [Describe the client's thought process e.g., concrete, abstract, emotive (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Thought Form:** [Detail the client's form of thoughts, including any disordered thoughts (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Orientation:** [Detail the client's orientation to time and place (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Memory:** [Describe the client's memory function (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Concentration and Attention:** [Detail the client's concentration, focus and attention levels (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Judgement:** [Detail the client's judgement capabilities, do they take responsibility for their decisions, have they made any poor decisions recently? (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Insight:** [Describe the client's insight into their condition. Was the client aware of their problem? (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]

### Assessment Results

* [Describe any results from psychometric assessments discussed in the session, such as NovoPsych questionnaires. Do not generate an "Assessment Results" section unless standardised psychometric assessments have been explicitly discussed in the transcript or contextual notes. Do not include qualitative observations, therapy progress, client self-reflections, or any other information in this section. If no psychometric assessment results were mentioned, exclude section.)]

### Diagnosis

* [List any DSM-5-TR diagnosis or other mental disorders, including who made the diagnosis if available (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript the contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]

### 5P Formulation

(Below is the structure of a 5P formulation. Use this structure and all five subheadings to create a formulation using information from the transcript and contextual notes.)

* **Presenting Problem:** [Summarise the client's presenting problems (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Predisposing Factors:** [List predisposing factors to the client's condition, such as family, genetics, background of mental illness, childhood trauma or social determinants of health (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Precipitating Factors:** [List the precipitating factors that may have triggered the client's condition (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes or clinical note, otherwise exclude section)
* **Perpetuating Factors:** [List the situations or personal characteristics that are perpetuating the condition and keeping it going for the client (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Protective Factors:** [List the factors that protect the client from worsening of the condition, such as family support, personal strengths or other positive life circumstances (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]

### Biopsychosocial Formulation

* **Biological:** [Detail biological factors including genetic, medical, hormonal, sleep, substance use and physiological arousal impacting the client's problems or symptoms (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Psychological:** [Detail psychological factors including attachment style, schemas, unhelpful thinking styles, emotion regulation, lack of self-compassion, defence mechanisms, attitudes or behaviours that interact with the client's problem or symptoms (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]
* **Social:** [Detail social circumstances, family, situational factors, relationship dysfunction, financial factors and social determinants of health that interact with the client's problem or symptoms (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]

### Goals

* [Describe in detail any goals that were discussed, including if any objectives, desires or things to work on were discussed, or if existing goals were reviewed. When available, describe SMART goals and quote specific aims (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]

### Next Steps

* [Detail a treatment plan, next scheduled appointment time and date, frequency, or number of planned sessions (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]

### Other Practitioners

* [Detail names, locations and organisations and contact details of other practitioners involved in the client's care, including doctors, case managers, lawyers, or other contacts (only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise exclude section).]

title: GIRP (Goals, Intervention, Response, and Plan)
description: Designed to summarise a session between a mental health clinician and a client using the GIRP (Goals, Intervention, Response, and Plan) note format.
prompt:
(Provide a summary of a session between a mental health clinician and a client using the GIRP note format. GIRP stands for Goals, Intervention, Response, and Plan. Consistent with typical GIRP notes, only present key information, and each sentence must be as short and sharp as possible. You must adhere to the following layout:)
 

[goals] Goals


[Print this section in a single paragraph of less than four sentences. Summarise the client’s current therapeutic goals or objectives that were the focus of this session. Include any progress, barriers, or revisions to goals discussed. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
 

[intervention] Intervention


[Print this section in a single paragraph of less than four sentences. Describe the clinician’s therapeutic interventions, strategies, or techniques used to help the client move toward their goals. If known, include the therapeutic framework used by the clinician (e.g., CBT, ACT, DBT, etc.). Describe the specific tools or exercises used (e.g., thought diary, meditation, role-play, etc.). Describe the clinician’s personal style and rationale. Describe any collaboration or problem-solving steps taken during the session. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
 

[response] Response


[Print this section in a single paragraph of less than four sentences. Describe the client’s level of engagement, insight, and emotional response. Describe any evidence of progress (e.g., emotional shifts, self-awareness, or cognitive breakthroughs), change, or resistance. Describe the client’s feedback on techniques or discussion topics. Describe any observations of affect, motivation, or ambivalence. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]
 

[nextsteps] Plan


[Print this section in a single paragraph of less than four sentences. Describe the agreed-upon goals or homework tasks for the client. Describe the plan for upcoming sessions or interventions. Describe any referrals, consultations, or coordination with other providers. Note any risk management issues, or details of safety plans. Describe any updates to treatment focus or diagnosis. Specify the next appointment details and any ongoing monitoring plan. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)]

title: GP Letter - Brief - After First Session(s)
description: This letter provides a brief summary of a first session, and a treatment plan, to a GP or other referrer. This template may also be appropriate if
prompt:
(Write a brief letter to the referring doctor after a client / patient has had their first session or sessions of treatment. The letter is in the context of a clinician just commencing a course of treatment with the client / patient. If a client’s name is not mentioned in the transcript, notes or contextual notes, refer to them as “client” - do not invent any names. Use the formatting layout below:)

Re: \[Print the client’s first and last name. If unknown, leave blank.]

DOB: \[Print the client’s date of birth in DD/MM/YYYY format. If unknown, leave blank.]

Dear Dr \[Referring clinician's last name],

Thank you for referring \[Client’s first and last Name (only include if mentioned in the transcript, notes or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank)].

\[Provide a summary in only a few sentences of the most important and relevant details about the client’s circumstances, diagnosis, and other relevant information, using the client’s session notes, transcripts, and contextual notes, as they relate to the client’s presenting issues. Information you might consider includes: the client’s presenting issues; other key client information such as circumstances, background, diagnoses and medical history, occupation, current living arrangements, and social support; trauma history; relevant people involved in the client’s life; and the client’s goals for treatment. Only include information that is explicitly mentioned in the client’s notes, transcripts, and contextual notes.]

\[Provide a summary in only a few sentences of the intended treatment plan (and, if applicable, treatment already provided) using the client’s session notes, transcripts, and contextual notes. Information you might consider includes: modality of therapy (e.g., CBT, ACT, schema therapy, psychodynamic psychotherapy, etc.); intended frequency of sessions; and anything else that has been discussed by the clinician related to intended treatment. If applicable, include information related to treatment with medication. Only include information that is explicitly mentioned in the client’s notes, transcripts, and contextual notes.]

\[Provide a summary of psychometric assessments completed by the client. Only include information that is explicitly mentioned in the client’s psychometric assessments, otherwise leave blank.]

\[If applicable, provide any recommendations for referrals to other health professionals or other health services that were made by the clinician. Provide the rationale for each of these recommendations. Include any request that the clinician has made for the treating doctor to review the client’s medication and the reasons provided for this request. Only include information that is explicitly mentioned in the client’s notes, transcripts, and contextual notes. If not explicitly mentioned in the client’s notes, transcripts, or contextual notes, leave blank.]

I will provide you with another update on the progress of treatment in due course. Should you have any further questions regarding \[Client's first and last Name (only include if mentioned in the transcript, notes or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank)], please feel free to contact me.



Sincerely,

\[Clinician Name]

\[Clinician Profession]

\[Practice Name]

title: GP Letter - Progress Update or Mental Health Treatment Plan Reporting
description: Generate a GP letter after psychological treatment (for example, on completion of a course of treatment for a client referred under a Mental Health
prompt:
(Generate a brief professional letter to a busy referring doctor from the treating psychologist or mental health clinician. The letter concisely summarises the client’s episode of care, based on the client’s notes, contextual notes, psychometric assessment results, and transcript data. The letter must concisely summarise only relevant clinical information, such as any assessments carried out on the client, treatment provided, and recommendations on future management. Limit to 1-1.5 pages or approximately 300-500 words. The language used must be formal and reflect evidence-based medicine and mental health care. If a client’s name is not mentioned in the transcript, notes or contextual notes, refer to them as “client” - do not invent any names. Use the letter format layout below:)
Re: [Print the client’s first and last name. If unknown, leave blank.] DOB: [Print the client’s date of birth in DD/MM/YYYY format. If unknown, leave blank.] Dear Dr [Referring Clinician's Name (use appropriate title if applicable, if the addressee’s name is not known, simply address as “Dear Dr”)], "Thank you for referring [Client’s Full Name (only include if mentioned in the transcript, notes or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank)] to me." [Provide a brief summary of the most important, clinically relevant findings about the patient’s circumstances, diagnosis and information from the sessions. Do not include detailed session content, only a brief summary focusing on the most relevant clinical details only. Using the client’s session notes, transcripts, and contextual notes during the specified treatment period, include information such as the client’s presenting issues, symptoms, the client's goals for treatment, and other key client information only if relevant to their presentation and therapy. If applicable, describe how any of this information has significantly changed during the treatment period.] (Only include information that is explicitly mentioned in the transcripts, notes, or contextual notes.) [Provide a brief summary of the treatment provided by the clinician. Using the client’s session notes, transcripts, and contextual summaries, include information such as: how many sessions have been completed to date; frequency of sessions; modality of therapy (e.g., CBT, ACT, schema therapy, psychodynamic psychotherapy); types of interventions used; types of homework or other activities completed as part of treatment; success or otherwise of treatment; the client’s engagement in treatment; and the client’s rapport or therapeutic alliance with the clinician.] (Do not include detailed session content, only a brief summary with the most important findings and information. Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript(s), note(s) or contextual note(s), otherwise leave blank.) Psychometric Assessment Results (only include section if psychometric results are present): * [Using bullet points, list the name of each psychometric assessment. For each assessment, you must include: 1) Assessment name, 2) One sentence description explaining what the tool measures (e.g, "The Depression Anxiety Stress Scale measures the severity of depressive symptoms over the past week"), 3) Results summary including the first paragraph of each assessment verbatim, and 4) Full quantitative results (scores, percentiles, clinical ranges). If the same assessment has been administered on more than one occasion, only list the most recent and include any change in symptoms over time. List assessment results from most recently completed. (Only include information that is explicitly in the client’s psychometric assessments results if available, otherwise leave blank.) If no psychometric assessments were administered, do not include this section).] [Provide a brief summary of the plan moving forward or future treatment directions between the clinician and the client. If applicable, include a brief outline of any risk to safety posed by the client, including protective factors that mitigate the risk. Provide any recommendations for future treatment and management mentioned by the clinician that may involve other health professionals or other health services. Provide the rationale for each of these recommendations. Include any request that the clinician has made for the treating doctor and the reasons provided for this request. (Only include information that is explicitly mentioned in the client’s notes, transcripts, and contextual summaries.)] [If the client requires a new referral to continue treatment or review of the Mental Health Treatment Plan, request that the referring doctor provide a new referral so the patient can continue to engage in psychological therapy] (be brief, only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript(s), note(s) or contextual notes) "Should you have any further questions regarding [Client's first and last Name (only include if mentioned in the transcript, notes or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank)], please do not hesitate to contact me." Sincerely, [Clinician Name] [Clinician Profession] [Practice Name]

title: Grief Counselling
description: For clinicians providing grief counselling to clients experiencing various forms of loss. This template helps clinicians document sessions using
prompt:
(This template is designed for mental health professionals providing grief counselling. Using the transcript from the session, generate a detailed yet concise summary of the session. Ensure that the summary adheres to theories of grief and bereavement and contemporary grief counselling frameworks, including continuing bonds theory, dual process model, and meaning reconstruction. Avoid pathologising normal grief responses. Analyse the transcript and provide responses according to the instructions in the section headings below.)

[presenting_concerns_and_context] Presenting Concerns and Context

* \[Summarise the client's current grief presentation, including emotional, cognitive, physical, and behavioural responses to loss. Note any changes in grief intensity or expression since previous sessions.]
* \[Describe the nature of the loss being addressed, such as the death of a loved one, non-death loss such as divorce, job loss, health loss, and the client's relationship to what was lost.]
* \[Note any anniversary reactions, significant dates, or triggering events discussed in this session.]

[grief_expression_and_processing] Grief Expression and Processing

* \[Document specific grief reactions observed or reported during the session, such as yearning, sadness, anger, guilt, relief, numbness, confusion, searching behaviours. Document the client’s emotional expressions, including intensity and variability.]
* \[Describe how the client expressed their grief during the session, including any metaphors, stories, or meaning-making narratives shared.]
  \[Note any significant memories, reflections, or meaning-making attempts related to the loss. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* \[Note any oscillation between loss-oriented and restoration-oriented coping as described in the Dual Process Model of grief, if observed. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual note, otherwise leave blank.)]
  \[Include any cultural, spiritual, or religious beliefs that inform the client's grieving process. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* \[(Use a subheading "Coping Strategies:") Summarise the client’s adaptive and maladaptive coping strategies, including avoidance, seeking social support, engaging in rituals, or using distraction.]

[interventions] Interventions

* \[Document the specific therapeutic approaches applied, such as validation, mindfulness, expressive techniques, narrative therapy, and meaning reconstruction. Describe any structured interventions, such as guided imagery, letter-writing to the deceased, empty chair work, or role-playing unfinished conversations. Note any psychoeducation provided about grief processes, common reactions, or coping strategies. Detail any resources, referrals, or recommendations provided during the session. Summarise the client’s responses to interventions, including any notable insights, emotional shifts, or resistance. Use as many dot points as needed to list separate interventions.]

[key_insights_or_themes] Key Insights or Themes

* \[Identify any key themes discussed, such as guilt, anger, numbness, relief, longing, existential concerns, or identity shifts. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* \[Highlight any new awareness, perspectives, or emotional shifts noted in the session. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* \[Document any movement toward acceptance, integration of loss, or changes in the client’s relationship with the deceased, such as continued bonds, adjusting roles, meaning reconstruction.]
* \[Summarise any areas where the client expressed a sense of relief, resilience, or emerging hope.]

[challenges_and_barriers] Challenges and Barriers

* \[Identify any difficulties in the grieving process, such as self-blame, unresolved issues, societal expectations, family conflict. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* \[Note any emotional avoidance, stuck points, or areas where the client feels unable to move forward. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual note, otherwise leave blank.)]
* \[If relevant, note any indications of complex, prolonged or traumatic grief and potential need for further support. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual note, otherwise leave blank.)]

[risk] Risk Assessment

* \[Summarise any risk factors, including suicidal ideation, self-harm, or significant functional impairment due to grief. (Only include if mentioned in the transcript, otherwise leave blank).]
