(Only include if explicitly mentioned in the client's notes, transcripts, added files, or additional context, otherwise leave blank.)]

**Ongoing care:** [In 1-2 sentences, briefly indicate the planned ongoing psychological treatment and coordination of care. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the client's notes, transcripts, added files, or additional context, otherwise leave blank.)]

I trust this information is helpful for [the patient's / patient's name's] ongoing psychiatric management. Please contact me if you require any further information or wish to discuss [the patient's / patient's name's] care.

Kind regards,

[Clinician Name]

[Clinician Profession]

title: Letter to Referrer under Eating Disorder Treatment and Management Plan (EDP) - Progress Update or EDP Review
description: Generate a letter to a referring medical practitioner (GP, psychiatrist or paediatrician) within the context of psychological treatment provided
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by clinicians providing treatment to a client referred by a medical practitioner under an Eating Disorder Treatment and Management Plan (EDP) as provided by Australia's Medicare Benefits Schedule. The client has been referred to the clinician by a medical practitioner. You will construct a draft letter to the referring medical practitioner from the clinician following the completion of a treatment episode. The letter will only be informed by information contained within the transcript, and by evidence-based treatments. The letter will provide the referring medical practitioner with a concise summary of the treatment episode, and will include such information as: a summary of the presenting issue; risk statement; treatment; goals; progress; psychometric assessment results; and plan for future management. The letter will follow the formal letter structure laid out below. Do not print out any title at the top of the output.)
Dr [Referring medical practitioner’s full name.] (If the referring medical practitioner’s name is not stated, just write "Referring Medical Practitioner's Name".) [Referring medical practitioner’s address.] (If the referring medical practitioner’s address is not stated, just write "Referring Medical Practitioner's Address".) Re: [client name.], DOB [client date of birth.], [client address.] (If the client's address is not stated, just write "Client's Address".) Dear [Referring medical practitioner’s name (using appropriate title if applicable).] (If the referring medical practitioner’s name is not stated, just write "Referring Medical Practitioner".), “Thank you for referring [name of client] for management of [diagnosis] under an Eating Disorder Treatment and Management Plan (EDP). They attended an initial assessment with me on [date of initial assessment] and have since attended [number of sessions attended] sessions for psychological treatment. As [client’s Name] has now completed the sessions available under your EDP referral, I write to update you on the treatment progress.” [Provide a brief summary of the presenting problem. Write this as prose. Do not use bullet points. Include information such as type and nature of eating disorder symptoms, length of time with symptoms, external stressors that are relevant to the symptoms, interpersonal or relationship factors that are relevant to the symptoms, and any other information that is relevant to the presenting problem. Include information related to the impact on functioning for the client (e.g., occupational, family, social, academic).] [Provide a brief summary of the episode of care, including how many sessions were received in the 12-month period since the EDP was created by the referring medical practitioner. Include the therapy type if mentioned, such as CBT-E, DBT, IPT. Include a brief summary of the patient’s circumstances] (Do not include the entire content of the session, only a brief summary with the most important findings and information. Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript, contextual notes or notes) [Detail the first aim, goal or intention of the client or therapist] (be brief, only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or note) [Detail the second aim or goal of the client or therapist] (be brief, only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or note) [Detail the first key point of the plan] (be brief, only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or note) [Detail the second key point of the plan] (be brief, only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or note) Over the past [duration or number of sessions], I have provided [Patient's Name] with [type of treatment, e.g., Cognitive Behaviour Therapy for Eating Disorders, DBT, IPT] to address [specific issues/diagnosis]. The primary objectives of this treatment are [objectives]. The progress made includes [observations and progress details]. [Patient's Name] has shown [positive/negative changes, any significant details]. Psychometric Assessment Results (Only include this section if results for psychometric assessments are available, otherwise exclude) The following psychometric assessments have been completed: [Using bullet points, list the name of each psychometric assessment. For each assessment, you must include: 1) Assessment name, 2) One sentence description explaining what the tool measures (e.g, "The Beck Depression Inventory-II measures the severity of depressive symptoms over the past two weeks"), 3) Results summary including the first paragraph of each assessment verbatim, and 4) Full quantitative results (scores, percentiles, clinical ranges). If the same assessment has been administered on more than one occasion, only list the most recent and include any change in symptoms over time. List assessment results from most recently completed. (Only include information that is explicitly in the client’s psychometric assessments results, transcript(s), notes(s) or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank.) If no psychometric assessments were administered, leave this section blank.] [Make a risk assessment in a statement (e.g., medical instability, suicidal or homicidal ideation, self-harm or harm to others, and risk of relapse, if any). (Do not use bullet points. Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or note, otherwise leave blank)] [Detail any case conferences, multidisciplinary care or liaison with other healthcare practitioners involved in the patient's care, such as dietitians, occupational therapists and psychiatrists. (be brief, only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or note)] [Detail what the plan is for follow-up and monitoring, as detailed by the clinician (e.g., next appointment details). If the clinician has not made any plans for follow-up or monitoring, leave blank.] [Detail if any Safety Plan has been made by the clinician to address medical instability or in the event that the client poses a risk to safety, including steps to take in a crisis. If the clinician has not made any Safety Plan, leave blank.] As we move forward, I would recommend [specific recommendations for continued care or adjustments, e.g. review of the Eating Disorder Treatment and Management Plan (EDP). If renewing the referral or Eating Disorder Treatment and Management Plan (EDP), request that the EDP be reviewed so that the patient can continue to engage in psychological therapy. If the patient has received 20 sessions and requires ongoing treatment, request that the patient be referred for specialised review by a psychiatrist or paediatrician so that the patient can access the additional 20 sessions.] (be brief, only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcripts, contextual notes or notes) [Summarise any other recommendations made specifically to the General Practitioner about ongoing care and management for the client.] Thank you again for your referral. Please do not hesitate to contact me if you have any questions. Yours sincerely, [Clinician name] (If the clinician's name is not stated, just write "Clinician Name".) [Clinician Title]
# Output Format * A formal letter structure. * Start with a greeting and end with a sign-off. * Ensure the tone is professional and respectful, maintaining confidentiality and using appropriate medical terminology. # Examples **Example Start:** Dear Dr. [Last Name], Thank you for referring [name of client] for management of [diagnosis] under an Eating Disorder Treatment and Management Plan (EDP). They attended an initial assessment with me on [date of initial assessment] and have since attended [number of sessions attended] sessions for psychological treatment. As [client’s Name] has now completed the sessions available under your EDP referral, I write to update you on the treatment progress. Over the past [duration or number of sessions], I have provided [Patient's Name] with [type of treatment, e.g., Cognitive Behaviour Therapy for Eating Disorders, DBT, IPT] to address [specific issues/diagnosis]. The primary objectives of this treatment are [objectives]. The progress made includes [observations and progress details]. [Patient's Name] has shown [positive/negative changes, any significant details]. Psychometric Assessments Results Symptoms as measured by the [details and results of any psychometric assessments used]. As we move forward, I would recommend [specific recommendations for continued care or adjustments, e.g. review of the Eating Disorder Treatment and Management Plan (EDP) or referral for specialist review by a psychiatrist or paediatrician so that the patient can access the additional 20 sessions under their EDP]. Please feel free to contact me to discuss any further details. Sincerely, **Example End:** *Note: Replace placeholders with the patient's specific details and relevant treatment specifics. The real letters should include more detailed descriptions and specifics based on the patient's case.*

title: Mental Status Examination (MSE)
description: For use by a wide variety of clinicians, this template provides a mental status examination (MSE) on a client or patient after their session. Even
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by clinicians in a mental health setting to generate a mental status examination, or MSE, after an appointment with a client or patient. Use language that is consistent with accepted standards of evidence-based psychiatry and medicine. Describe the client’s presentation across the following domains:)



[mse] Mental Status Examination (MSE)

**Appearance:** \[Describe the patient’s general physical appearance, hygiene, grooming, and attire. If this is not stated in the transcript, print "Appearance:" and leave blank.]

**Behaviour:** \[Describe the patient’s interaction with their surrounds, activity level, psychomotor activity, eye contact, and rapport. If this is not stated in the transcript, print "Behaviour:" and leave blank.]

**Speech:** \[Describe the rate, volume, tone, coherence, and articulation of the patient’s speech. If this is not stated in the transcript, print "Speech:" and leave blank.]

**Mood and Affect:** \[Describe the patient’s reported mood and emotional state using their own words if possible. Describe the range and appropriateness of the patient’s emotional response during the appointment, and note any discrepancies with the patient’s stated mood. Note any observations about emotional expression made by the clinician. If this is not stated in the transcript, print "Mood and Affect:" and leave blank.]

**Thought Process:** \[Describe the patient’s coherence, organisation, and flow of thoughts. If this is not stated in the transcript, print "Thought Process:" and leave blank.]

**Thought Content:** \[Describe the content of the patient’s thoughts, and note the presence of delusions, distortions, obsessions, or intrusive thoughts, if any. If this is not stated in the transcript, print "Thought Content:" and leave blank.]

**Perception:** \[Describe hallucinations or other perceptual disturbances of the patient, if any. If this is not stated in the transcript, print "Perception:" and leave blank.]

**Cognition:** \[Describe the patient’s orientation to time / place / person, attention, memory, concentration, and comprehension. If this is not stated in the transcript, print "Cognition:" and leave blank.]

**Insight and Judgment:** \[Describe the patient's understanding of their mental health condition and their decision-making capacity, noting any lack of awareness or denial. If this is not stated in the transcript, print "Insight and Judgment:" and leave blank.]

title: Mentalization Based Treatment (MBT) – DVA End of Cycle Report
description: For use by a DVA clinician using an MBT framework, this template provides a draft End of Cycle Report. It may be useful in instances where the
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by a Department of Veterans Affairs mental health clinician working within a mentalization based treatment, or MBT, framework. Following analysis of the transcript, you will generate a detailed report on a cycle of treatment provided by a clinician to a client. Use professional language that is consistent with the practice of mentalization based treatment and psychiatric medicine. The template will follow the following format:)

[dva_treatment_cycle_end_of_cycle_report_] DVA Treatment Cycle - End of Cycle Report for Allied Health Providers

[client_details] Client Details

**Name:** \[Enter the client's full name exactly as provided in the transcript or contextual notes. Leave blank if not mentioned.]

**DOB:** \[Enter the client's date of birth in DD/MM/YYYY format as explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes. Leave blank if not mentioned.]

**DVA file number:** \[Enter the DVA file number as stated in the transcript or contextual notes. Leave blank if not mentioned.]

**Referring clinician:** \[Enter the referring clinician's full name and title as explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes. Leave blank if not mentioned.]

**Referral date for this cycle:** \[Enter the referral date for this treatment cycle in DD/MM/YYYY format if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes. Leave blank if not mentioned.)

**Previous treatment:** \[Summarise details of any previous treatments or interventions for this client explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes. Leave blank if not mentioned.]

[conditions_being_managed] Conditions Being Managed

* \[List the conditions being managed for the client as explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes. Conditions could include posttraumatic stress disorder, personality factors, and trauma history. Do not infer or add conditions that are not explicitly stated. Ensure each condition is listed on its own line.]

[client_goals_and_date_assessed] Client Goals and Date Assessed

* \[List all the client’s goals as explicitly discussed or assessed in the transcript or contextual notes. Write each goal in full sentences, followed by its status e.g., Not met, Ongoing, or Achieved. Do not add inferred or speculative goals. List each goal on a separate line for clarity as follows:]
* \[State the first goal. Indicate its status: Not met / Ongoing / Achieved.]
* \[State the next goal. Indicate its status: Not met / Ongoing / Achieved.]

[management_summary] Management Summary

\[Provide a detailed management summary. Summarise the therapeutic approach and interventions provided during the cycle, including treatment focus, therapeutic methods, and any recommendations for further support. Ensure the content is based only on details explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes. Cover all key points such as therapeutic strategies, ongoing plans, and additional recommendations. Do not speculate or add any additional information.]

[scales] Outcome Measures

**Measure:** \[State the name of the psychometric measure, questionnaire or survey used to assess the client.]

**Initial assessment score:** \[Enter the abbreviated name of the assessment, the score of the client’s initial assessment, and the date as explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes. For example, "DASS-21: Total distress score 42 on 01/01/2024."]

**Start of this cycle score:** \[Enter the abbreviated name of the assessment, the start-of-cycle score, and the date as explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes. Leave blank if not mentioned.)

**End of this cycle score:** \[Enter the abbreviated name of the assessment, the end-of-cycle score, and the date as explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes. Leave blank if not mentioned.)

\[When multiple measures are used, start each new measure with its details on a new line, and repeat the format of the above instructions.]

[interpretation_of_outcome_measures_and_a] Interpretation of Outcome Measures and Additional Comments

\[Provide a detailed interpretation of the outcome measures. Explain the scores for each measure and their implications, based solely on the transcript or contextual notes. Cover all explicitly mentioned measures and their relevance to the client’s condition. Do not infer outcomes or provide speculative details. Leave blank if interpretation is not mentioned.]

**Further management required Y/N:** \[Indicate Yes or No according to information provided by the clinician explicitly contained in the transcript or contextual notes.]

**Reasoning to support recommendation:** \[Provide a concise paragraph detailing the rationale for further management only if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes.]

[allied_health_provider_details] Allied Health Provider Details

**Name:** \[Enter the allied health provider's full name as explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes. Leave blank if not provided.]

**Provider number:** \[Enter the allied health provider’s number as explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes. Leave blank if not provided.]

**Allied health profession:** \[Enter the allied health provider’s profession as explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes. Leave blank if not provided.]

**Practice name and address:** \[Enter the practice name and address as explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes. Leave blank if not mentioned.]

**Phone:** \[Enter the allied health provider’s phone number as explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes. Leave blank if not mentioned.]

**Fax:** \[Enter the allied health provider’s fax number as explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes. Leave blank if not mentioned.]

**Email:** \[Enter the allied health provider’s email address as explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes. Leave blank if not mentioned.]

**Provider signature:** \[Leave space for the provider's signature. This should remain blank for completion by the allied health provider.]

**Date:** \[Leave space for the date. This should remain blank for completion by the allied health provider.]

sections.label: DVA Treatment Cycle - End of Cycle Report for Allied Health Providers
Interpretation of Outcome Measures and Additional Comments

title: Mentalization Based Treatment (MBT) Session Note
description: For use by a clinician using an MBT framework, this template provides a detailed summary of a session with a client.
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by a mental health clinician working within a mentalization based treatment, or MBT, framework. Following analysis of a transcript of a therapy session between a clinician and a client, you will generate a detailed summary of that session. Use language that is consistent with the practice of mentalization based treatment. The template will follow the following format:)

[content] Session Content

\[Describe the content of the session, using as many paragraphs as necessary. Information in your note should cover: the main focus areas, the client’s responses and insights, and key discussions and explorations. Your language should be congruent with a mentalization based treatment approach.]

[mse] Mental Status Examination

**Mood:** \[Describe the client's reported mood and clinician's observations.]

**Thoughts:** \[Describe the content and process of the client’s thoughts.]

**Affect:** \[Describe the client’s emotional state and range of expressed emotions.]

**Behaviour:** \[Describe observed behaviours of the client during the session.]

**Insight:** \[Describe the client's level of awareness about their condition.]

**Judgement:** \[Describe the client's decision-making capacity.]

[mentalising] Mentalising

\[Describe the client’s ability to understand their own mental states and those of others, including: Any impairments in mentalising; impact on social interactions and self-perception; and coping mechanisms related to mentalising difficulties.]

[self_other_differentiation] Self-Other Differentiation

\[Describe the client's ability to distinguish between self and others, including: Any blurred boundaries; impact on interpersonal relationships; and self-perception in relation to others.]

[mentalising_poles] Mentalising Poles

\[Describe the client's imbalances in the different dimensions of mentalising, including: If the patient is more focused on the self or other dimension of mentalising; if the client is more focused on the affective or cognitive dimension of mentalising; if the client is more focused on the internal or external dimension of mentalising; and if the client is more focused on the implicit or explicit dimension of mentalising.]

[non_mentalising_modes] Non-Mentalising Modes

\[Identify and describe the client’s non-mentalising modes (e.g., teleological mode, psychic equivalence, or pretend mode), and their significance.]

[interventions] Interventions

\[Describe the therapeutic mentalising interventions used during the session, including specific mentalising techniques or approaches, such as the not knowing stance, empathic validation (e.g., reflection on the emotion or affect being felt in a specific situation, and linking it to the client’s behaviour, or the effect that this has on them), contrary moves, stop and rewind, increased focus on personal interactions between the therapist and the client, marked affect, and gentle challenges. Describe the goals and rationale of the interventions used, and the client’s response to the interventions used.]

[metaphors_used] Metaphors Used

* \[List the metaphors used during the session, if any, and describe their significance and relationship to the client.]

[nextsteps] Next Steps

* \[Outline the plan for future sessions as agreed between the clinician and the client, including: Date and time of next appointment, focus areas for upcoming sessions, and the therapeutic modality.]

title: MIGDAS-2 Autism Assessment – Adults With Verbal Fluency
description: A detailed summary of an autism assessment conducted using the Diagnostic Interview for Adults With Verbal Fluency, as part of the Monteiro Interview
prompt:
(The template below will summarise a clinical interview being conducted by a clinician who is using the Diagnostic Interview for Adults With Verbal Fluency Protocol, as part of the Monteiro Interview Guidelines for Diagnosing the Autism Spectrum - 2nd Edition, also known as the MIGDAS-2. Use neurodiversity-affirming language as much as possible in your output. Counterbalance this language with terminology that is also consistent with the MIGDAS-2 and the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders - 5th Edition - Text Revision, also known as the DSM-5-TR. After analysing the session notes, contextual notes, and transcript from one or more sessions between a clinician and a client, you will generate a detailed summary according to the headings as detailed below. Note that there may be a case worker, caregiver, or other type of support person in the interview with the individual being assessed, and you should note any relevant comments they make as well.)

### Diagnostic Interview For Adults With Verbal Fluency

### People Present

* \[List the name of the individual being assessed. List any other people who were in attendance during the interview, other than the clinician. State what the relationship of any other person in attendance is to the individual being assessed (e.g., parent, caregiver, other family member, support worker, case manager, etc.).]

### Introduction

\[Describe all the issues, events, situations, and factors that led the individual to attend this autism assessment. If another person is also present during the interview, describe their response and perspective about why the individual being assessed has sought out an autism assessment.]

### Interests

#### Current

**1:** \[Describe the types of activities that the individual enjoys doing when they have free time e.g., hobbies, pastimes, games, etc.]

**2:** \[Describe the individual’s ability to transition away from doing one activity to doing another.]

**3:** \[Describe the impact on the individual when doing their favourite things. Describe if the individual finds it calming to participate in their favourite activities.]

**4:** \[Describe how doing their favourite things helps the individual to block out unpleasant thoughts or feelings.]

**5:** \[Describe the impact of doing their favourite activities on the individual’s sleep, work, college/school coursework, household chores, and social obligations.]

**6:** \[Describe what the important people in the individual’s life tell them about spending too much time doing or talking about their favourite things?]

**7:** \[Describe the extent to which the individual thinks about details related to their favourite activities when they are not doing those activities.]

**8:** \[Describe what things about their current pattern of activities the individual would like to change and why.]

**9:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about the individual’s preferred/favourite activities.]

#### History of Interests

**1:** \[Describe the individual’s recollections of having any particular areas of passionate interest when they were a child, including any activities they liked to do for long periods of time repeatedly.]

**2:** \[Describe how the individual’s areas of passionate interest as a child changed as they got older and became a teenager.]

**3:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about the individual’s particular areas of passionate interest when they were a child.]

### Sensory

#### Visual details

**1:** \[Describe the individual’s ability to notice visual details that other people may not pay attention to or notice.]

**2:** \[Describe the individual’s ability to remember details after seeing or reading something.]

**3:** \[Describe the individual’s reactions when they notice that someone has moved or rearranged something at home, in their home, or in other places.]

**4:** \[In relation to the individual’s reactions when they notice that someone has moved or rearranged something at home, in their home, or in other places, describe any changes in pattern between when the individual was a child to becoming an adult.]

**5:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about the individual’s attention to visual details, including how the individual reacts when they notice that someone has moved or rearranged something at home, in their home, or in other places.]

#### Noises

**1:** \[Describe the types of noises that bother or distract the individual. Describe how the individual reacts when they are bothered or distracted by these noises.]

**2:** \[Describe the types of sounds that have a soothing or calming effect on the individual.]

**3:** \[Describe how the impact of noises and sounds has changed for the individual between childhood and becoming an adult.]

**4:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about the impact of noises and sounds on the individual.]

#### Smells

**1:** \[If the individual talks about particular smells that they dislike or get bothered by, describe what those smells are and how and why the individual is bothered by them.]

**2:** \[Describe in what ways the individual smells objects, and which objects the individual likes to smell and why.]

**3:** \[Describe how the pattern of smelling objects has changed for the individual over time. Describe if there are any differences to how the individual finds the smell of particular objects as an adult compared to when they were a child.]

**4:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about the impact of smells on the individual.]

#### Touch

**1:** \[Describe if the individual says that they constantly touch or actively avoid touching things.]

**2:** \[Describe if the individual finds the textures of any items appealing or unappealing, and describe what the items are.]

**3:** \[Describe how the pattern of the individual’s response to touching things has changed over time. Describe if there are any differences to how the individual responds to touching particular objects as an adult compared to when they were a child.]

**4:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about the impact of touching particular objects or textures on the individual.]

#### Food

**1:** \[Describe the individual’s favourite foods.]

**2:** \[Describe the individual’s least favourite foods, or foods that they do not like.]

**3:** \[Describe how the individual is bothered by any particular foods e.g., due to the food’s taste, or texture. Describe how the individual reacts when different foods are mixed together, or when one food touches another on a plate.]

**4:** \[Describe how the pattern of the individual’s response to particular foods has changed over time. Describe if there are any differences to how the individual responds to particular foods as an adult compared to when they were a child.]

**5:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about the impact of particular foods on the individual.]

#### Clothing

**1:** \[Describe the individual’s favourite types of clothes to wear, and describe why they like to wear these types of clothes.]

**2:** \[If applicable, describe any sensitivities the client has to clothing e.g., being bothered by tags, seams in socks, wearing certain types of shoes, wearing woollen jumpers, etc.]

**3:** \[If applicable, describe how the pattern of the individual’s response to particular clothing has changed over time. Describe if there are any differences to how the individual responds to particular clothing as an adult compared to when they were a child.]

**4:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about the impact of particular clothing on the individual.]

### Sleep

**1:** \[Describe the individual’s sleeping habits e.g., does the individual have trouble going to sleep or staying asleep, when does the client go to bed and get out of bed, how much sleep do they get each night, etc.]

**2:** \[Describe how the pattern of the individual’s sleeping habits has changed over time. Describe if there are any differences to how the individual’s sleeping pattern is as an adult compared to when they were a child.]

**3:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about the sleeping habits of the individual.]

### Body boundaries

**1:** \[Describe the individual’s attitude towards people standing close to them. That is, to what extent is the individual bothered by other people encroaching on their personal space.]

**2:** \[Describe if the individual has ever been told by another person that they need to stand further away from them.]

**3:** \[Describe the individual’s preferences in relation to touch, such as using deeper pressure like a firm handshake or deep pressure hug, or a lighter touch, or no touch at all.]

**4:** \[Describe the individual’s reactions when they are in crowded places.]

**5:** \[Describe how the pattern of the individual’s experiences with personal space and body boundaries has changed over time. Describe if there are any differences to how the individual experiences personal space and body boundaries as an adult compared to when they were a child.]

**6:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about the individual’s experiences of personal space and body boundaries.]

### Pain

**1:** \[Describe the individual’s tolerance for pain, and detail any examples the individual gives to reflect this.]

**2:** \[Describe how the pattern of the individual’s tolerance for pain has changed over time. Describe if there are any differences to how the individual’s tolerance for pain as an adult compared to when they were a child.]

**3:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about the individual’s tolerance for pain.]

### Changes in routine

**1:** \[Describe the experiences of the individual when they are interrupted from doing an enjoyable activity.]

**2:** \[Describe the experiences of the individual when they unexpectedly have a change in their regular routine.]

**3:** \[Describe the individual’s response when they know about a change in routine in advance.]

**4:** \[Describe how the pattern of the individual’s experience of being interrupted and changes in routine have changed over time. Describe if there are any differences to how the individual’s experience of being interrupted and changes to routine as an adult compared to when they were a child.]

**5:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about the individual’s experience of being interrupted and changes in routine.]

### Body movements

**1:** \[If applicable, describe the individual’s body movement routines that help to soothe and regulate their emotions.]

**2:** \[If applicable, describe how the pattern of the individual’s body movement routines has changed over time. Describe if there are any differences to the individual’s body movement routines as an adult compared to when they were a child.]

**3:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about the individual’s body movement routines.]

### Sensory issues in daily life

**1:** \[Describe the degree to which, and in what ways, the individual’s sensory sensitivities and sensory needs interfere with their current quality of life.]

**2:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about the individual’s current quality of life being affected by their sensory sensitivities and sensory needs.]

### Communication

**1:** \[Describe the extent to which the individual seeks out other people to have conversations.]

**2:** \[Describe the individual’s preferences about who starts conversations.]

**3:** \[Describe the individual’s favourite topics of conversation.]

**4:** \[Describe the individual’s opinion about whether other people ever request that they talk about something else other than the individual’s areas of interest.]

**5:** \[Describe the individual’s ability to maintain and contribute to conversations. Describe the individual’s ability to do this even when the conversation is not focused on their areas of interest.]

**6:** \[Describe the individual’s opinion on their own thinking abilities e.g., do they feel like they need more time than the average person to respond to a question.]

**7:** \[Describe the individual’s attitude towards using metaphors and figures of speech versus being more literal with language. Describe any examples used by the individual or the clinician.]

**8:** \[Describe the individual’s attitude towards using humour when communicating with others. Describe any examples used by the individual or clinician.]

**9:** \[Describe the individual’s use of repeated expressions in conversations. Describe any examples used by the individual or clinician.]

**10**: \[Describe any aspects of the individual’s communication style that they would like to change.]

**11:** \[Describe how the individual’s communication pattern has changed over time. Describe if there are any differences to the individual’s communication pattern as an adult compared to when they were a child.]

**12:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about the individual’s communication pattern.]

### Social

**1:** \[Describe the individual’s closest social connections e.g., friends, family members, etc.]

**2:** \[Describe the individual’s attitude towards spending time with other people.]

**3:** \[Describe the ages of the individual’s friends. Describe the history of these friendships e.g., how the individual met them, what activities the individual enjoys doing with them, and how often the individual socialises with them.]

**4:** \[Describe the individual’s attitude towards spending time on their own, in solitary pursuits. Describe how the individual sees their need for time alone in comparison to others.]

### Emotional

**1:** \[Describe the ways that other people annoy, irritate, or upset the individual.]

**2:** \[Describe the individual’s reactions to people who annoy, irritate, or upset them.]

**3:** \[Describe the things, situations, or events that make the individual nervous or anxious.]

**4:** \[Describe the individual’s behaviour when they are highly anxious.]

**5:** \[If applicable, describe any medications the individual takes for anxiety or agitation, and describe the effectiveness or otherwise of these medications.]

**6:** \[Describe the individual’s emotion management abilities when they were a child. Describe if this pattern has changed since then.]

**7:** \[Describe any changes that the individual would like to make to their social or emotional style.]

**8:** \[Describe the individual’s self-perceptions. Describe the good and bad qualities used by the individual to describe the type of person they are.]

**9:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about the individual’s social relationships or emotions.]

**10:** \[If applicable, describe the ways that any other person in the interview would characterise the individual, such as good and bad qualities.]

### Health

**1:** \[If applicable, describe any physical health conditions experienced by the individual.]

**2:** \[If applicable, describe the individual’s medications and what they are for.]

**3:** \[If applicable, describe the effectiveness of the individual’s medications.]

**4:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about the individual’s medications.]

### Employment / Academic Details

\[START SECTION FOR INDIVIDUALS WHO CURRENTLY WORK. If the individual does not work, do not complete this section.]

### For adults who work

**1:** \[If applicable, describe the individual’s employment and work activities.]

**2 and 3:** \[If applicable, describe the most liked and least liked aspects of the individual’s job.]

**4:** \[Describe the individual’s ideal job. Describe why the individual believes they would enjoy this job.]

**5:** \[If applicable, describe the individual’s relationship with their boss at work.]

**6:** \[If applicable, describe the individual’s relationship with co-workers at their place of employment.]

**7:** \[If applicable, describe how the individual travels to and from work.]

**8:** \[If applicable, describe how the individual manages the money they earn from work.]

**9:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about the individual’s ability to manage work demands.]

\[END SECTION FOR INDIVIDUALS WHO CURRENTLY WORK.]

\[START SECTION FOR INDIVIDUALS WHO DO NOT CURRENTLY WORK. If the individual does work, do not complete this section.]

#### For adults who do not work

**1:** \[If applicable, describe any past jobs that the individual has had.]

**2:** \[Describe the type of job that the individual would like to have now.]

**3:** \[If applicable, describe the individual’s beliefs in whether or not they need help finding a job, and provide any examples given about the type of help they might find useful.]

**4:** \[Describe how the individual believes they would get to and from work if they had a job.]

**5:** \[Describe what the individual would do with their income if they had a job.]

**6:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about the individual’s plan or ability to get a job.]

\[END SECTION FOR INDIVIDUALS WHO DO NOT CURRENTLY WORK.]

\[START SECTION FOR INDIVIDUALS WHO ARE ENROLLED IN AN ACADEMIC PROGRAM SUCH AS UNIVERSITY OR COLLEGE. If the individual is not enrolled in an academic program, do not complete this section.]

#### For adults who are studying

**1:** \[If applicable, describe the academic study of the individual, including where they are studying, and what they like and dislike about the course.]

**2:** \[If applicable, describe the special provisions being received by the individual as part of their academic study e.g., receiving additional time in examinations, taking examinations in different locations to other students, receiving one-on-one tutorial support, etc.]

**3:** \[If applicable, describe the individual’s system for managing their academic workload. Describe the individual’s ability to manage deadlines and hand work in on time.]

**4:** \[If applicable, describe the type of help received by the individual to help them stay organised. Describe the individual’s assessment of how useful these types of help are.]

**5:** \[If applicable, describe the individual’s beliefs about what type of coursework is the best fit for their way of thinking e.g., math, science, creative writing, etc.]

**6:** \[If applicable, describe the individual’s involvement in social activities related to their academic study. Describe the individual’s preferences for such activities, including what they like and dislike about such activities.]

**7:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about the individual’s ability to manage their academic demands.]

\[END SECTION FOR INDIVIDUALS WHO ARE ENROLLED IN AN ACADEMIC PROGRAM SUCH AS UNIVERSITY OR COLLEGE.]

\[START SECTION FOR INDIVIDUALS WHO ARE NOT ENROLLED IN AN ACADEMIC PROGRAM SUCH AS UNIVERSITY OR COLLEGE. If the individual is enrolled in an academic program, do not complete this section.]

#### For adults who are not studying

**1:** \[If applicable, describe the individual’s previous adult academic study experience, including where they studied and what subjects they studied.]

**2:** \[If applicable, describe the individual’s plans to return to formal study. Describe why or why not.]

**3:** \[If applicable, describe the individual’s attitude to receiving help and guidance to explore the possibility of finding an academic situation that would be appropriate for their brain style.]

**4:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about the possibility of the individual developing a plan for future academic study.]

\[END SECTION FOR INDIVIDUALS WHO ARE NOT ENROLLED IN AN ACADEMIC PROGRAM SUCH AS UNIVERSITY OR COLLEGE.]

### Independent living

**1:** \[Describe if the individual lives with any other people, or if they live alone. Describe how the individual gets along with the other people in their household.]

**2:** \[Describe the individual’s ability to function with everyday tasks. Consider such things as: getting up out of bed each morning; making meals; shopping; cleaning the house; taking care of pets; managing finances; driving a car, etc.]

**3:** \[Describe where the individual would like to be living in five years’ time and with whom.]

**4:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about the individual’s independent living skills.]

### Closing

**1:** \[Describe the individual’s knowledge of autism. Describe if the individual has read or learned anything specific about autism.]

**2:** \[If applicable, describe any additional questions that the individual has for the clinician. Describe the clinician’s answers to any such questions.]

**3:** \[If applicable, describe any other information that the individual provides to the clinician that the individual believes may be relevant to this clinical interview which has not been stated at any other time in the interview.]

**4:** \[If applicable, describe the point of view of any other person in the interview about any other aspect of the individual that is deemed relevant to this clinical interview which has not been stated at any other time in the interview.]

### Diagnostic Interview Profile

#### Sensory Use

**1:** \[Provide a detailed interpretation in one paragraph of the individual’s preferred interests and sensory-seeking behaviours, noting whether such behaviour is current or historical in nature e.g., the individual has specific highly developed areas of interest that occupy a significant amount of available time; developed areas of interest that are diverse and secondary to other pursuits such as work or school; etc. Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank.]

**2:** \[Provide a detailed interpretation in one paragraph of the individual’s reported experiences of sensory sensitivities, both currently and historically e.g., reports specific sensory sensitivities to sounds; has a pattern of reacting to sensory triggers that is consistently unusual; etc. Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank.]

**3:** \[Provide a detailed interpretation in one paragraph of the individual’s reported experience of body movements and mannerisms, both currently and historically e.g., reports and/or is observed engaging in repetitive sensory-based behaviours, such as picking at face and body tensing; unusual or repetitive body movements and mannerisms are currently interfering with quality of life; etc. Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank.]

#### Language and Communication

**1:** \[Provide a detailed interpretation in one paragraph of how the individual’s speech style varied throughout the session e.g., speaks with an unusual cadence that is distinctive and unvarying throughout the session that was high-pitched; speaks in a formal or scripted style; etc. Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank.]

**2:** \[Provide a detailed interpretation in one paragraph of how the individual’s language use varies when describing preferred topics e.g., speaks in a narrative that has a sensory, self-directed quality focused on details of the narrative to the exclusion of the participation of the clinician; repeatedly interrupts and overrides comments made by the clinician; etc. Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank.]

**3:** \[Provide a detailed interpretation in one paragraph of how the quality, clarity, and relevance of the individual’s use of language changes throughout the interview e.g., displays speech that has a driven and one-sided quality when relaying information about a preferred topic; does not initiate conversation or make comments that are relevant to the surroundings and circumstances; etc. Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank.]

**4:** \[Provide a detailed interpretation in one paragraph of the individual’s ability to respond to the clinician’s conversational attempts e.g., responds to prompts from the clinician to discuss topics but with little sense of a shared conversation; requires insistent interruption or intrusion by the clinician to allow the clinician to participate in the conversation; etc. Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank.]

**5:** \[Provide a detailed interpretation in one paragraph of the individual’s ability to describe or respond to nonliteral language and humour e.g., shows a preference for literal communication over figurative or metaphorical language use; ignores the clinician’s use of an idiom or colloquial expression; etc. Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank.]

#### Social Relationships and Emotional Responses

**1:** \[Provide a detailed interpretation in one paragraph of the individual’s use of eye contact and eye gaze and facial expressions e.g., consistently avoids eye contact; uses sporadic, fleeting eye contact; etc. Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank.]

**2:** \[Provide a detailed interpretation in one paragraph of the individual’s use of and response to facial expressions and emotional responses e.g., displays a limited range of emotional expressions / maintains a neutral facial expression with little change throughout the interview regardless of the topic; most responsive when the clinician uses exaggerated facial expressions; etc. Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank.]

**3:** \[Provide a detailed interpretation in one paragraph of the individual’s response when prompted to discuss self, friends, and family e.g., requires repeated prompts to talk about family or friends; endorses desire to improve social relationships and relationship skills; etc. Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank.]

**4:** \[Provide a detailed interpretation in one paragraph of how the individual’s anxiety or agitation level is affected by sensory sensitivities and by social conversation and interaction e.g., anxiety or agitation triggers reported by the individual are linked to areas of sensory sensitivity; displays anxiety or agitation when the clinician interrupts the individual’s narrative or discusses feelings and social topics; etc. Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank.]

**5:** \[Provide a detailed interpretation in one paragraph of how much awareness the individual displays in discussing situations that cause anger or agitation e.g., has difficulty identifying situations that have prompted angry feelings or agitation; identifies a situation but has difficulty providing details or expanding on their description of events; etc. Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual notes, otherwise leave blank.]

### Pattern of Observations

#### Sensory Use and Interests

**1:** \[Based on the clinician's comments, describe if the individual’s sensory-seeking routines and their function are consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

**2:** \[Based on the clinician's comments, describe if the individual’s sensory sensitivities and triggers are consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

**3:** \[Based on the clinician's comments, describe if the individual’s body movements and mannerisms are consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

#### Language and Communication

**1:** \[Based on the clinician's comments, describe if the individual’s intonation and inflection are consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

**2:** \[Based on the clinician's comments, describe if the individual’s variation in language use when describing preferred topics and special interests is consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

**3:** \[Based on the clinician's comments, describe if the quality, clarity, and relevance of the individual’s use of language throughout the session is consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

**4:** \[Based on the clinician's comments, describe if the individual’s speech reciprocity capabilities are consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

**5:** \[Based on the clinician's comments, describe if the individual’s understanding of nonliteral language, humour, idioms, and colloquial expressions are consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

#### Social Relationships and Emotional Responses

**1:** \[Based on the clinician's comments, describe if the individual’s use of eye contact and eye gaze are consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

**2:** \[Based on the clinician's comments, describe if the individual’s use of, and response to, facial expressions and emotional responses are consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

**3:** \[Based on the clinician's comments, describe if the individual’s response when prompted to discuss peers and family, and when requested to ask a question of the clinician, is consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

**4:** \[Based on the clinician's comments, describe if the individual’s anxiety or agitation level in response to social conversation and social interaction is consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

**5:** \[Based on the clinician's comments, describe if the individual’s self-awareness of their own anxiety or agitation triggers is consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

### Behavioural Profile Summary

#### Sensory Use and Interests

\[In a paragraph of less than five sentences, provide a detailed summary of the individual’s strengths in the areas of sensory use and interests.]

### Educational / Intervention Recommendations

title: MIGDAS-2 Autism Assessment – Children and Adolescents With Verbal Fluency
description: A detailed summary of an autism assessment conducted using the Diagnostic Interview for Children and Adolescents With Verbal Fluency, as part of the
prompt:
(The template below will summarise a clinical interview being conducted by a clinician who is using the Diagnostic Interview for Children and Adolescents With Verbal Fluency Protocol, as part of the Monteiro Interview Guidelines for Diagnosing the Autism Spectrum - 2nd Edition, also known as the MIGDAS-2. Use neurodiversity-affirming language as much as possible in your output. Counterbalance this language with terminology that is also consistent with the MIGDAS-2 and the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders - 5th Edition - Text Revision, also known as the DSM-5-TR. After analysing the session notes, contextual notes, and transcript from one or more sessions between a clinician and a client, you will generate a detailed summary according to the headings as detailed below. Note that there may be a parent, caregiver, or other type of support person in the interview with the individual being assessed, and you should note any relevant comments they make as well.)

### Diagnostic Interview with Children and Adolescents with Verbal Fluency

### People Present

* \[List the name of the individual being assessed. List any other people who were in attendance during the interview, other than the clinician. State what the relationship of any other person in attendance is to the individual being assessed (e.g., parent, caregiver, other family member, support worker, case manager, etc.).]

### Sensory Use and Interests

**1:** \[Describe any observations or notes made by the clinician while the child is responding to materials that have visual and movement properties. For example, the child may be intensely focused and repetitive; they may place their face close to visual objects or otherwise display close visual inspection; etc. IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO DETERMINE ANY DETAILS ABOUT THE CHILD’S RESPONSE TO MATERIALS THAT HAVE VISUAL AND MOVEMENT PROPERTIES, LEAVE BLANK.]

**2:** \[Describe any observations or notes made by the clinician while the child is responding to materials that have auditory properties. Describe if the child responds to any other auditory stimuli. For example, the child may cover their ears with apparent apprehension or anxiety; the child may appear unresponsive to the source of the sound; etc. IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO DETERMINE ANY DETAILS ABOUT THE CHILD’S RESPONSE TO MATERIALS THAT HAVE AUDITORY PROPERTIES, LEAVE BLANK.]

**3:** \[Describe any observations or notes made by the clinician while the child is responding to materials that have tactile properties. For example, the child may show pronounced aversions/preferences; the child may hesitate/avoid touching specific objects; etc. IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO DETERMINE ANY DETAILS ABOUT THE CHILD’S RESPONSE TO MATERIALS THAT HAVE TACTILE PROPERTIES, LEAVE BLANK.]

**4:** \[Describe any observations or notes about how the child responds to and uses language to describe their preferred topics and special interests. For example, the child may display a notable increase in alertness and engagement when their preferred topic is introduced; the child may respond to a wide range of introduced topics with no pronounced alertness to any one topic; etc. Describe any comments made by the clinician in relation to how the child responds to and uses language to describe their preferred topics. IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO DETERMINE ANY DETAILS ABOUT THE CHILD’S RESPONSE TO AND USE OF LANGUAGE TO DESCRIBE PREFERRED TOPICS AND SPECIAL INTERESTS, LEAVE BLANK.]

**5:** \[Describe any observations or notes made by the clinician about the child’s body movements and mannerisms. For example, the child may engage in repetitive sensory-based behaviours, such as hand or body tensing, body rocking, etc. IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO DETERMINE ANY DETAILS ABOUT THE CHILD’S BODY MOVEMENTS AND MANNERISMS, LEAVE BLANK.]

### Language and Communication

**1:** \[Describe any observations or notes about the child’s speech, including intonation, inflection, cadence, and voice volume. For example, the child may speak with an unusual cadence that is distinctive and unvarying throughout the session, such as lilting, or exclamatory, etc. Describe any comments made by the clinician in relation to how the child speaks. IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO DETERMINE ANY DETAILS ABOUT THE CHILD’S SPEECH, LEAVE BLANK.]

**2:** \[Describe any observations or notes about how the child’s language use varies when describing their preferred topics and special interests. For example, the child may speak in a narrative that has a sensory, self-directed quality that is focused on details of their narrative to the exclusion of the clinician’s participation; the child may repetitively manipulate one or more of the sensory items throughout the conversation; etc. Describe any comments made by the clinician in relation to how the child’s language use varies when describing their preferred topics and special interests. IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO DETERMINE ANY DETAILS ABOUT THE CHILD’S LANGUAGE USE WHEN DESCRIBING THEIR PREFERRED INTERESTS AND SPECIAL TOPICS, LEAVE BLANK.]

**3:** \[Describe any observations or notes about how the quality, clarity, and relevance of the child’s use of language changes throughout the session. For example, the child may show a marked deterioration in the use of speech when the clinician asks questions or directs the conversation to social topics, which may lead to longer processing time before responding, less detail in the child’s responses, etc. Describe any comments made by the clinician in relation to how the quality, clarity, and relevance of the child’s use of language changes throughout the session. IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO DETERMINE ANY DETAILS ABOUT HOW THE QUALITY, CLARITY, AND RELEVANCE OF THE CHILD’S USE OF LANGUAGE CHANGES THROUGHOUT THE SESSION, LEAVE BLANK.]

**4:** \[Describe any observations or notes about how the child responds to the clinician’s conversational attempts. For example, the child may respond to prompts from the clinician to discuss topics but with little sense of a shared conversation; the child may respond to prompts from the clinician and discuss topics with a strong sense of a shared conversation; etc. Describe any comments made by the clinician in relation to how the child responds to the clinician’s conversational attempts. IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO DETERMINE ANY DETAILS ABOUT HOW THE CHILD RESPONDS TO THE CLINICIAN’S CONVERSATIONAL ATTEMPTS, LEAVE BLANK.]

**5:** \[Describe any observations or notes about how the child responds to the clinician’s use of idioms or colloquial expressions. For example, the child may ignore the clinician’s use of an idiom or colloquial expression; the child may acknowledge or show recognition of the clinician’s use of an idiom or colloquial expression; etc. Describe any comments made by the clinician in relation to how the child responds to the clinician’s use of idioms and colloquial expressions. IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO DETERMINE ANY DETAILS ABOUT HOW THE CHILD RESPONDS TO THE CLINICIAN’S USE OF IDIOMS AND COLLOQUIAL EXPRESSIONS, LEAVE BLANK.]

**6:** \[Describe any observations or notes about how the child behaves in response to telling or hearing jokes or riddles. For example, the child may ignore or display some agitation in response to the request to tell a joke or a riddle; the child may respond to the request in a way that is expected for typically developing peers; etc. Describe any comments made by the clinician in relation to how the child behaves in response to telling or hearing jokes or riddles. IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO DETERMINE ANY DETAILS ABOUT HOW THE CHILD BEHAVES IN RESPONSE TO TELLING OR HEARING JOKES OR RIDDLES, LEAVE BLANK.]

### Social Relationships and Emotional Responses

**1:** \[Describe any observations or notes made by the clinician in relation to how the child uses eye contact and eye gaze. For example, the child may show the following differences in the use of eye contact and eye gaze during the session: consistently avoids eye contact; uses sporadic, fleeting eye contact; etc. IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO DETERMINE ANY DETAILS ABOUT HOW THE CHILD USES EYE CONTACT AND EYE GAZE, LEAVE BLANK.]

**2:** \[Describe any observations or notes made by the clinician in relation to how the child uses and responds to facial expressions and emotional responses. For example, the child may maintain a neutral facial expression with little change throughout the session; or the child may use exaggerated facial expressions in a pronounced way, especially during the social conversation portion of the session; etc. IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO DETERMINE ANY DETAILS ABOUT HOW THE CHILD USES AND RESPONDS TO FACIAL EXPRESSIONS AND EMOTIONAL RESPONSES, LEAVE BLANK.]

**3:** \[Describe any observations or notes about how the child responds when prompted to discuss peers and family, and when requested to ask a question of the clinician. For example, the child may exhibit difficulty engaging in a conversation about peers and family, in one or more of the following ways: requires repeated prompts to engage in the conversational exchange or does not engage despite repeated prompts; displays agitation during the conversation; etc. Describe any comments made by the clinician in relation to how the child responds when prompted to discuss peers and family, and when requested to ask a question of the clinician. IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO DETERMINE ANY DETAILS ABOUT HOW THE CHILD RESPONDS WHEN PROMPTED TO DISCUSS PEERS AND FAMILY, AND WHEN REQUESTED TO ASK A QUESTION OF THE CLINICIAN, LEAVE BLANK.]

**4:** \[Describe any observations or notes about how the child’s anxiety or agitation level is affected by social conversation and interaction. For example, the child may display anxiety or agitation when the clinician drives the topic of conversation, interrupts the child’s narrative, or discusses feelings or social topics; the child may display some mild anxiety or awkwardness initially, but anxiety lessens as the session progresses and the child engages in conversation and play with the clinician; etc. Describe any comments made by the clinician in relation to how the child’s anxiety or agitation level is affected by social conversation and interaction. IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO DETERMINE ANY DETAILS ABOUT HOW THE CHILD’S ANXIETY OR AGITATION LEVEL IS AFFECTED BY SOCIAL CONVERSATION AND INTERACTION, LEAVE BLANK.]

**5:** \[Describe any observations or notes about how much self-awareness the child displays in discussing situations or triggers that cause anger or agitation. For example, the child may have difficulty identifying situations that have prompted angry feelings or agitation; the child may accurately identify situations that prompt angry feelings or agitation; etc. Describe any comments made by the clinician in relation to how much self-awareness the child displays in discussing situations that cause anger or agitation. IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO DETERMINE ANY DETAILS ABOUT HOW MUCH SELF-AWARENESS THE CHILD DISPLAYS IN DISCUSSING SITUATIONS THAT CAUSE ANGER OR AGITATION, LEAVE BLANK.]

**6:** \[Describe any observations or notes about how much self-awareness the child displays in discussing a range of feelings. This includes during the discussion of the feelings cards activity. For example, the child may display a rote understanding of each feeling but is unable to provide a personalised example; the child may respond by reading the definition on each card; etc. Describe any times when the child has experienced various feelings. Describe anything that the child wishes for. Describe any words that the child uses to describe themselves. Describe anything the child would change about the school to make it a better place. Describe any comments made by the clinician in relation to how much self-awareness the child displays in discussing a range of feelings. IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO DETERMINE ANY DETAILS ABOUT HOW MUCH SELF-AWARENESS THE CHILD DISPLAYS IN DISCUSSING A RANGE OF FEELINGS, LEAVE BLANK.]

title: MIGDAS-2 Autism Assessment – Individuals With Limited to No Verbal Fluency
description: A detailed summary of an autism assessment conducted using the Diagnostic Interview for Individuals With Limited to No Verbal Fluency Protocol, as
prompt:
(The template below will summarise a clinical interview being conducted by a clinician who is using the Diagnostic Interview for Individuals With Limited to No Verbal Fluency Protocol, as part of the Monteiro Interview Guidelines for Diagnosing the Autism Spectrum - 2nd Edition, also known as the MIGDAS-2. Use neurodiversity-affirming language as much as possible in your output. Counterbalance this language with terminology that is also consistent with the MIGDAS-2 and the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders - 5th Edition - Text Revision, also known as the DSM-5-TR. After analysing the session notes, contextual notes, and transcript from one or more sessions between a clinician and a client, you will generate a detailed summary according to the headings as detailed below. Note that this questionnaire is specifically intended and designed for use with individuals who have limited to no verbal fluency. As such, you will need to obtain most of your information from comments made by the clinician, or comments made by any other person in attendance with the individual, or from information contained in any contextual notes.)

### Diagnostic Interview For Individuals With Limited To No Verbal Fluency

### People Present

* \[List the name of the individual being assessed. List any other people who were in attendance during the interview, other than the clinician. State what the relationship of any other person in attendance is to the individual being assessed (e.g., parent, caregiver, other family member, support worker, case manager, etc.).]

### Sensory Use and Interests

\[Describe the individual’s response to materials that have visual and movement properties. Pay close attention to comments made by the clinician.]

\[Describe the individual’s response to materials that have auditory properties. Pay close attention to comments made by the clinician.]

\[Describe the individual’s response to materials that have tactile properties. Pay close attention to comments made by the clinician.]

\[Describe the individual’s response to materials related to preferred interests. Pay close attention to comments made by the clinician.]

\[Describe the individual’s body movements and mannerisms. Pay close attention to comments made by the clinician.]

### Language and Communication

\[Describe the individual’s use of and response to vocalisations. Pay close attention to comments made by the clinician.]

\[Describe the individual’s use of emerging language. Pay close attention to comments made by the clinician.]

\[Describe the individual’s use of nonverbal communication. Pay close attention to comments made by the clinician.]

### Social Relationships and Emotional Responses

\[Describe the individual’s use of eye contact and eye gaze. Pay close attention to comments made by the clinician.]

\[Describe the individual’s use of and response to facial expressions and emotional responses. Pay close attention to comments made by the clinician.]

\[Describe the individual’s anxiety and agitation levels. Describe how these levels are affected by social conversation and interaction. Pay close attention to comments made by the clinician.]

\[Describe the individual’s shared enjoyment and joint attention. Describe how the individual responds to the clinician’s attempts at shared play. Pay close attention to comments made by the clinician.]

### Pattern of Observations

#### Sensory Use and Interests

**1:** \[Describe if the individual’s responses to materials with visual and movement properties are consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

**2:** \[Describe if the individual’s responses to materials with auditory properties are consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

**3:** \[Describe if the individual’s responses to materials with tactile properties are consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

**4:** \[Describe if the individual’s responses to materials related to preferred interests are consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

**5:** \[Describe if the individual’s body movements and mannerisms are consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

#### Language and Communication

**1:** \[Describe if the individual’s use of and response to vocalisations are consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

**2:** \[Describe if the individual’s use of emerging language is consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

**3:** \[Describe if the individual’s use of nonverbal communication is consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

#### Social Relationships and Emotional Responses

**1:** \[Describe if the individual’s use of eye contact and eye gaze are consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

**2:** \[Describe if the individual’s use of, and response to, facial expressions and emotional responses are consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

**3:** \[Describe if the individual’s anxiety or agitation levels are consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

**4:** \[Describe if the individual’s shared enjoyment and joint attention abilities are consistent with autism, not consistent with autism, or unclear. And then provide a rationale for your statement about this. Remember that there may be visual / non-verbal elements that you are not able to comment on if the clinician has not commented on it.]

### Behavioural Profile Summary

#### Sensory Use and Interests

\[In a paragraph of less than five sentences, provide a detailed summary of the individual’s strengths in the areas of sensory use and interests.]

\[In a paragraph of less than five sentences, provide a detailed summary of the individual’s differences, challenges, and weaknesses in the areas of sensory use and interests.]

#### Language and Communication

\[In a paragraph of less than five sentences, provide a detailed summary of the individual’s strengths in the areas of language and communication.]

\[In a paragraph of less than five sentences, provide a detailed summary of the individual’s differences, challenges, and weaknesses in the areas of language and communication.]

#### Social Relationships and Emotional Responses

\[In a paragraph of less than five sentences, provide a detailed summary of the individual’s strengths in the areas of social relationships and emotional responses.]

\[In a paragraph of less than five sentences, provide a detailed summary of the individual’s differences, challenges, and weaknesses in the areas of social relationships and emotional responses.]

### Diagnostic Impressions

\[If provided, describe the clinician’s diagnosis, explanation of the individual’s neurodivergent profile, and observations about comorbidities, differential diagnosis, and co-occurring traits such as anxiety, trauma, executive dysfunction. IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO DETERMINE ANY DETAILS ABOUT THE CLINICIAN’S DIAGNOSIS, LEAVE BLANK.]

### Educational / Intervention Recommendations

\[If applicable in a paragraph of less than three sentences, provide up to three evidence-based recommendations related to supporting the individual’s communication abilities. If not applicable, leave blank.]

\[If applicable, in a paragraph of less than three sentences, provide up to three evidence-based recommendations related to supporting the individual’s social skills abilities. If not applicable, leave blank.]

\[If applicable, in a paragraph of less than three sentences, provide up to three evidence-based recommendations related to supporting the individual’s organisational abilities. If not applicable, leave blank.]

\[If applicable, in a paragraph of less than three sentences, provide up to three evidence-based recommendations related to supporting the individual’s sensory/self-regulation abilities. If not applicable, leave blank.]

\[If applicable, in a paragraph of less than three sentences, provide up to three evidence-based recommendations related to supporting the individual’s abilities in the home environment, including interacting with family members. If not applicable, leave blank.]

### Summary Notes

### Psychometric Assessment

\[Summarise any psychometric testing results. For each test, include: the name and purpose of each test; quantitative results (e.g., scores, percentiles); qualitative interpretation (e.g., within average range, clinically significant); and implications of results in the clinical context. If no psychometric testing has been carried out, leave blank.]

### Findings According to DSM-5-TR Criteria A

#### <u>Social Communication and Interaction</u>

#### A1

\[Describe any deficits in social-emotional reciprocity.
