title: Psychiatrist Letter to Referrer - General Review Appointments
description: For use by a psychiatrist seeing a known patient for a follow-up or general review appointment, this template provides a structure for a formal
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by psychiatrists conducting a follow-up general review appointment with a known patient. The patient has been referred by a General Practitioner and is being seen by the Psychiatrist under Australia's Medicare Benefits Schedule Item Number 293. The purpose of the prompt will be to construct a draft letter to the General Practitioner from the Psychiatrist following this follow-up general review appointment. As such, the letter must address the points outlined in Australia's Medicare Benefits Schedule Item Number 293. The Psychiatrist's letter will only be informed by information contained within the transcript, and by evidence-based treatments. Use language that is consistent with accepted standards of evidence-based psychiatry and medicine, such as using the term "patient" instead of "client". Use a formal letter structure. The letter will provide the referring General Practitioner with a detailed summary of the follow-up general review appointment, and will include such information as: reason for consultation; summary of the presenting problem; mental status examination; current medications; diagnosis and assessment; risk statement; and treatment plan, together with details about any follow-up appointments or further referrals. Avoid including direct quotes from the transcript unless they are pivotal to the clinical assessment process. You will strictly adhere to the following layout:)

Dr [Referring clinician’s full name.] (If the referring clinician's name is not stated, just write "Referring Clinician's Name".)
[Referring clinician’s address.] (If the referring clinician's address is not stated, just write "Referring Clinician's Address".)

Re: [Patient name.], DOB [Patient date of birth.], [Patient address.] (If the patient's address is not stated, just write "Patient's Address".)

Dear [Referring clinician’s name (using appropriate title if applicable).] (If the referring clinician's name is not stated, just write "Referring Doctor".),

Thank you for referring [patient’s name] for a psychiatric assessment review.

[Provide a brief summary of the presenting problem or purpose of the appointment. Include information such as type and nature of symptoms, length of time with symptoms, external stressors that are relevant to the symptoms, interpersonal or relationship factors that are relevant to the symptoms, and any other information that is relevant to the presenting problem or purpose of the appointment. Include information related to the impact on functioning for the patient (e.g., occupational, family, social, academic).]

[Detail any stated changes in mood, behaviour, or functioning since the last appointment. Detail the patient's perspective on the effectiveness and side effects of current treatment. Detail any changes in life circumstances or significant life events for the patient since the last appointment.]

[mse] Mental Status Examination (MSE)

[Provide a short paragraph using language consistent with accepted psychiatric and medical terms of no more than four sentences that summarises the patient’s presentation using the following standard MSE domains (only include each domain if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual note, otherwise leave blank): Appearance (the patient’s general physical appearance, hygiene, grooming, and attire); Behaviour (the patient’s interaction with their surrounds, activity level, psychomotor activity, eye contact, and rapport); Speech (the rate, volume, tone, coherence, and articulation of the patient’s speech; including if the patient communicates in any other non-verbal way, such as with gestures, noises, or eye contact); Mood and Affect (the patient’s reported mood and emotional state using their own words if possible; including the range and appropriateness of the patient’s emotional response during the appointment, and note any discrepancies with the patient’s stated mood; including any observations about emotional expression made by the clinician; including any behaviours that provide insight into mood and affect, such as anger outbursts, withdrawal, and laughing; including any other factors that may be providing insight into the patient’s mood or affect, such as expressions of illness, or other interactions with the clinician; including the response of the patient to the use of any visual aids regarding mood or affect, such as mood scales); Thought Process (the patient’s coherence, organisation, and flow of thoughts); Thought Content (the content of the patient’s thoughts, and note the presence of delusions, distortions, obsessions, or intrusive thoughts, if any); Perception (hallucinations or other perceptual disturbances of the patient, if any); Cognition (the patient’s orientation to time / place / person, attention, memory, concentration, and comprehension; including the results of any stated cognitive assessment that has previously been carried out on the patient; including the patient’s attention and concentration abilities); and Insight and Judgment (the patient's understanding of their mental health and their decision-making capacity, noting any lack of awareness or denial).]

[medications] Current Medications

[List all medications that the patient is currently taking, including psychotropic and non-psychotropic. Include information about doses and frequencies. Describe any medication history that details information such as response to any past medication, side effects, and adverse reactions. Assess adherence to previous medication regimens.]

[psychometric_testing] Psychometric Testing

[Provide any psychometric assessment results for the patient. For each psychometric test, include: the name of the assessment, a brief description of the psychometric assessment, the date of test administration, and relevant purpose to the current evaluation. Provide a summary of the quantitative results (e.g., percentiles). Provide a summary interpretation of the results. Provide any implications of results in the clinical context related to the patient's presentation. (Repeat this process for as many assessments that were administered.) If no psychometric assessments were administered, leave this section blank and do not print the sub-heading.]

[diagnosis_and_assessment] Diagnosis and Assessment

[Describe the patient’s diagnosis as stated by the psychiatrist, based on DSM-5-TR and ICD-11 criteria. Include the diagnostic code of both DSM-5-TR and ICD-11 for any such diagnoses. Detail any comorbidities or differential diagnosis considerations provided by the psychiatrist. Summarise the patient’s clinical presentation and any changes from previous appointments.]

[risk] Risk

[Make a risk assessment in a statement (e.g., suicidal or homicidal ideation, self-harm or harm to others, and risk of relapse, if any). Include any information related to past suicide attempts or past self-harming behaviour. Include any information about psychosocial stressors contributing to the level of risk. Include any information about protective factors that mitigate the risk.]

[treatment_plan_discussed_with_patient] Treatment Plan Discussed With Patient

[Specify any evidence-based recommendations for medication changes made by the psychiatrist, and specify if any current medications should be continued or discontinued. Provide the psychiatrist's rationale for these recommendations. If the psychiatrist has not made any recommendations, leave blank.]

[Specify any suggested non-pharmacological interventions made by the psychiatrist, such as psychotherapy or lifestyle modifications. Provide the psychiatrist's rationale for these recommendations. If the psychiatrist has not made any recommendations, leave blank.]

[Specify if any referrals to other specialists or services have been or will be made by the psychiatrist (e.g., lab tests). Provide the psychiatrist's rationale for these recommendations. If the psychiatrist has not made any recommendations, leave blank.]

[Detail what the plan is for follow-up and monitoring, as detailed by the psychiatrist (e.g., next appointment details). If the psychiatrist has not made any plans for follow-up or monitoring, leave blank.]

[Detail if any Safety Plan has been made by the psychiatrist in the event that the patient poses a risk to safety, including steps to take in a crisis. If the psychiatrist has not made any Safety Plan, leave blank.]

[Summarise any education provided by the psychiatrist to the patient about their diagnosis and treatment options. Document the patient’s understanding and agreement with the plan. If the psychiatrist has not provided any education to the patient about their diagnosis and treatment options, leave blank.]

[Summarise any other recommendations made specifically to the General Practitioner about ongoing care and management for the patient.]

[summary] Summary

[Provide a brief paragraph summary of the appointment in no more than four sentences.]

Thank you for your ongoing care and management. Please feel free to contact me if you have any questions.

Yours sincerely,

Dr [Psychiatrist Name.] (If the psychiatrist's name is not stated, just write "Psychiatrist Name".)

title: Psychiatrist Letter to Referrer - Medication Review Appointments
description: For use by a psychiatrist seeing a known patient for a follow-up medication review appointment, this template provides a structure for a detailed
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by psychiatrists conducting a follow-up medication review appointment with a known patient. The patient has been referred by a General Practitioner and is being seen by the Psychiatrist under Australia's Medicare Benefits Schedule Item Numbers 300 or 302. The purpose of the prompt will be to construct a draft letter to the General Practitioner from the Psychiatrist following this follow-up medication review appointment. As such, the letter must address the points outlined in Australia's Medicare Benefits Schedule Item Numbers 300 and 302. The Psychiatrist's letter will only be informed by information contained within the transcript, and by evidence-based treatments. Use language that is consistent with accepted standards of evidence-based psychiatry and medicine, such as using the term "patient" instead of "client". Use a formal letter structure. The letter will provide the referring General Practitioner with a detailed summary of the follow-up medication review appointment, and will include such information as: reason for consultation; summary of the presenting problem; mental status examination; diagnosis and assessment; current medications; treatment plan, together with details about any follow-up appointments or further referrals; and a risk statement. Avoid including direct quotes from the transcript unless they are pivotal to the clinical assessment process. You will strictly adhere to the following layout:)

Dr \[Referring clinician’s full name.] (If the referring clinician's name is not stated, just write "Referring Clinician's Name".)
\[Referring clinician’s address.] (If the referring clinician's address is not stated, just write "Referring Clinician's Address".)

Re: \[Patient name.], DOB \[Patient date of birth.], \[Patient address.] (If the patient's address is not stated, just write "Patient's Address".)

Dear \[Referring clinician’s name (using appropriate title if applicable).] (If the referring clinician's name is not stated, just write "Referring Doctor".),

Thank you for referring \[patient’s name] for a psychiatric assessment review.

\[Provide a brief summary of the presenting problem or purpose of the appointment. Include information such as type and nature of symptoms, length of time with symptoms, external stressors that are relevant to the symptoms, interpersonal or relationship factors that are relevant to the symptoms, and any other information that is relevant to the presenting problem or purpose of the appointment. Include information related to the impact on functioning for the patient (e.g., occupational, family, social, academic).]

\[Detail any stated changes in mood, behaviour, or functioning since the last appointment. Detail the patient's perspective on the effectiveness and side effects of current treatment. Detail any changes in life circumstances or significant life events for the patient since the last appointment.]

[mse] Mental Status Examination (MSE)

\[Document findings across the following domains:]

Appearance: \[Describe the patient’s general physical appearance, hygiene, grooming, and attire. If this is not stated in the transcript, print "Appearance:" and leave blank.]

Behaviour: \[Describe the patient’s interaction with their surrounds, activity level, psychomotor activity, eye contact, and rapport. If this is not stated in the transcript, print "Behaviour:" and leave blank.]

Speech: \[Describe the rate, volume, tone, coherence, and articulation of the patient’s speech. If this is not stated in the transcript, print "Speech:" and leave blank.]

Mood and Affect: \[Describe the patient’s reported mood and emotional state using their own words if possible. Describe the range and appropriateness of the patient’s emotional response during the appointment, and note any discrepancies with the patient’s stated mood. Note any observations about emotional expression made by the psychiatrist. If this is not stated in the transcript, print "Mood and Affect:" and leave blank.]

Thought Process: \[Describe the patient’s coherence, organisation, and flow of thoughts. If this is not stated in the transcript, print "Thought Process:" and leave blank.]

Thought Content: \[Describe the content of the patient’s thoughts, and note the presence of delusions, distortions, obsessions, or intrusive thoughts, if any. If this is not stated in the transcript, print "Thought Content:" and leave blank.]

Perception: \[Describe hallucinations or other perceptual disturbances of the patient, if any. If this is not stated in the transcript, print "Perception:" and leave blank.]

Cognition: \[Describe the patient’s orientation to time / place / person, attention, memory, concentration, and comprehension. If this is not stated in the transcript, print "Cognition:" and leave blank.]

Insight and Judgment: \[Describe the patient's understanding of their mental health condition and their decision-making capacity, noting any lack of awareness or denial. If this is not stated in the transcript, print "Insight and Judgment:" and leave blank.]

[diagnosis_and_assessment] Diagnosis and Assessment

\[Describe the patient’s diagnosis as stated by the psychiatrist, based on DSM-5-TR and ICD-11 criteria. Include the diagnostic code of both DSM-5-TR and ICD-11 for any such diagnoses. Detail any comorbidities or differential diagnosis considerations provided by the psychiatrist. Summarise the patient’s clinical presentation and any changes from previous appointments.]

[medications] Current Medications

\[List all medications that the patient is currently taking, including psychotropic and non-psychotropic. Include information about doses and frequencies. Describe any past medication history that details information such as response to any past medication, past side effects, and past adverse reactions.]

\[Assess adherence to the current prescribed medication regime, including any challenges (e.g., forgetfulness, cost, or stigma). Note any patient reported side effects or concerns about current medications (e.g., severity, onset, impact on functioning). Detail the patient’s assessment of current medication effectiveness, including any changes to symptoms since starting the current medication regime. Detail the patient’s concerns or issues about taking the current medications.]

\[Detail the psychiatrist’s assessment of the impact of the current medication regime on the patient’s presentation. Include any feedback from the psychiatrist on the patient’s self-report of the effectiveness of the current medication.]

[treatment_plan_discussed_with_patient] Treatment Plan Discussed With Patient

\[Specify any evidence-based recommendations for medication changes made by the psychiatrist, including dosages and frequencies. Specify if any current medications should be continued or discontinued. Provide the psychiatrist's rationale for these recommendations. Specify if the psychiatrist has stated that the current medication regime will remain the same, and provide the psychiatrist’s rationale for this.]

\[Specify any suggested non-pharmacological interventions made by the psychiatrist, such as psychotherapy or lifestyle modifications. Provide the psychiatrist's rationale for these recommendations. If the psychiatrist has not made any recommendations, leave blank.]

\[Specify if any referrals to other specialists or services have been or will be made by the psychiatrist (e.g., lab tests). Provide the psychiatrist's rationale for these recommendations. If the psychiatrist has not made any recommendations, leave blank.]

\[Detail what the plan is for follow-up and monitoring, as detailed by the psychiatrist (e.g., next appointment details). If the psychiatrist has not made any plans for follow-up or monitoring, leave blank.]

\[Detail if any Safety Plan has been made by the psychiatrist if the patient poses a risk to safety, including steps to take in a crisis. If the psychiatrist has not made any Safety Plan, leave blank.]

\[Summarise any education provided by the psychiatrist to the patient about their diagnosis and treatment options. Document the patient’s understanding and agreement with the plan. If the psychiatrist has not provided any education to the patient about their diagnosis and treatment options, leave blank.]

\[Summarise any other recommendations made specifically to the General Practitioner about ongoing care and management for the patient.]

[risk] Risk

\[Make a risk assessment in a statement (e.g., suicidal or homicidal ideation, self-harm or harm to others, and risk of relapse, if any). Include any information related to past suicide attempts or past self-harming behaviour. Include any information about psychosocial stressors contributing to the level of risk. Include any information about protective factors that mitigate the risk.]

[summary] Summary

\[Provide a paragraph summary of the appointment in no more than four sentences.]

Thank you for your ongoing care and management. Please feel free to contact me if you have any questions.

Yours sincerely,

Dr \[Psychiatrist Name.] (If the psychiatrist's name is not stated, just write "Psychiatrist Name".)

title: Psychiatrist Letter to Referrer – Clinical Interview / First Appointments
description: For use by a psychiatrist seeing a patient for the first time or for a longer appointment, this template provides a structure for a detailed formal
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by medical clinicians conducting a clinical interview and assessment with a patient being seen for the first time. The patient has been referred by a General Practitioner and is being seen by the medical clinician under Australia's Medicare Benefits Schedule Item Number 291. The purpose of the prompt will be to construct a draft letter to the General Practitioner from the medical clinician following this first clinical interview appointment. As such, the letter must address the points outlined in Australia's Medicare Benefits Schedule Item Number 291. The medical clinician's letter will only be informed by information contained within the transcript, and by evidence-based treatments. Use language that is consistent with accepted standards of evidence-based psychiatry and medicine, such as using the term "patient" instead of "client". Use a formal letter structure as per the layout below, and use this layout regardless of what profession the clinician is. The letter will provide the referring General Practitioner with a detailed summary of the first clinical interview appointment, and will include such information as: patient identifying information; reason for consultation; personal history; medical status and medical history; medication history; mental status examination; psychometric assessments used; formulation, incorporating a bio-psycho-social approach using predisposing, precipitating, perpetuating and protective factors; diagnosis; risk statement; and treatment plan, together with details about any follow-up appointments or further referrals. Avoid including direct quotes from the transcript unless they are pivotal to the clinical assessment process. You will strictly adhere to the following layout:)

Dr [Referring clinician’s full name.] (If the referring clinician's name is not stated, just write "Referring Clinician's Name".)
[Referring clinician’s address.] (If the referring clinician's address is not stated, just write "Referring Clinician's Address".)

Re: [Patient name.], DOB [Patient date of birth.], [Patient address.] (If the patient's address is not stated, just write "Patient's Address".)

Dear [Referring clinician’s name (using appropriate title if applicable).] (If the referring clinician's name is not stated, just write "Referring Doctor".),

Thank you for referring [patient’s name] for a psychiatric assessment.

[Provide a brief summary of the presenting problem or purpose of the appointment. Include information such as type and nature of symptoms, length of time with symptoms, external stressors that are relevant to the symptoms, interpersonal or relationship factors that are relevant to the symptoms, and any other information that is relevant to the presenting problem or purpose of the appointment. Include information related to the impact on functioning for the patient (e.g., occupational, family, social, academic).]

[history] Personal and Developmental History

[Provide a detailed summary of the patient’s personal and developmental history. Include information such as early development, family history and structure, education and employment history, occupation, current living arrangements, and social support. Describe any history of trauma, including family or intergenerational trauma, or significant adversity, or any type of traumatic event. Describe any family history of any illness or medical condition, including psychiatric illness and neurodevelopmental conditions. Include any information about family suicides or family addiction behaviours (e.g., alcoholism, other drug abuse). (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the transcript or contextual note, otherwise leave blank.)]

[If nothing is mentioned in the transcript, notes, or contextual notes about personal and developmental history, do not include this section in the letter.]

[current_key_lifestyle_indicators] Current Key Lifestyle Indicators

[Provide a detailed summary of the patient’s current key lifestyle indicators that are potentially relevant to the patient’s presenting concerns. Describe the patient’s current sleep pattern and sleep hygiene. Describe the patient’s current diet, including binge behaviours, caffeine intake, and consumption of junk food. Describe the patient’s current level of physical exercise or general activity levels. Describe the patient’s level of social functioning, including information about quality and quantity of social and family relationships, the patient’s efforts at connecting socially with others, and the extent of any social isolation.]

[If nothing is mentioned in the transcript, notes, or contextual notes about current key lifestyle indicators, do not include this section in the letter.]

[medical_status_and_medical_history] Medical Status and Medical History

[List previous non-psychiatric diagnoses and medical conditions, including information about when the diagnosis was made and by whom. Provide information given by the patient that describes responses to treatment, such as effective outcomes, side effects, and adverse reactions.]

[psychiatric_illness_history] Psychiatric Illness History

[List previous psychiatric diagnoses and conditions, including information about when the diagnosis was made and by whom. Provide information given by the patient that describes responses to treatment, such as effective outcomes, side effects, and adverse reactions. Describe any previous psychological interventions, including with therapists, online programs, or group programs. Describe any past hospitalisations for mental health reasons, including locations, names of key medical personnel involved, and dates of these hospitalisations.]

[medications] Medication History

[List all medications that the patient is currently taking, including psychotropic and non-psychotropic. Include information about doses and frequencies. Describe any medication history that details information such as response to any past medication, side effects, and adverse reactions. Assess adherence to previous medication regimens.] (If the medication name is unclear or cannot be confidently identified from the transcript, print [Medication Name] rather than inferring or guessing the name.)

[If nothing is mentioned in the transcript, notes, or contextual notes about medication history, do not include this section in the letter.]

[drug_and_alcohol_use_history] Drug and Alcohol Use History

[Provide a detailed summary of the patient’s drug and alcohol use history. Write this as prose. Do not use bullet points. Describe the patient’s current level of alcohol consumption, cigarette or vape use, and if they consume any other recreational or illicit substance (e.g., cannabis, methamphetamine, MDMA, cocaine). Describe any past or current misuse of prescription medications (e.g., opioids, benzodiazepines, stimulants).]

[If nothing is mentioned in the transcript, notes, or contextual notes about drug and alcohol use history, do not include this section in the letter.]

[other_addiction_history] Other Addiction History

[Provide a detailed summary of the patient’s other addiction history. Write this as prose. Do not use bullet points. Describe any other potentially problematic addictive behaviours mentioned by the patient (e.g., gambling, video gaming, Internet, social media, food, pornography). Include this information even if the patient does not view the behaviour as problematic or as an addiction.]

[If nothing is mentioned in the transcript, notes, or contextual notes about other addiction history, do not include this section in the letter.]

[legal_and_forensic_history] Legal and Forensic History

[Provide a detailed summary of the patient’s legal and forensic history. Describe any previous history mentioned by the patient that involves illegal activity, interactions with the justice system, imprisonment, interactions with the family law system, or domestic violence behaviour.]

[If nothing is mentioned in the transcript, notes, or contextual notes about legal and forensic history, do not include this section in the letter.]

[mse] Mental Status Examination (MSE)

[Document findings across the following domains:]

Appearance: [Describe the patient’s general physical appearance, hygiene, grooming, and attire.]

Behaviour: [Describe the patient’s interaction with their surrounds, activity level, psychomotor activity, eye contact, and rapport.]

Speech: [Describe the rate, volume, tone, coherence, and articulation of the patient’s speech.]

Mood and Affect: [Describe the patient’s reported mood and emotional state using their own words if possible. Describe the range and appropriateness of the patient’s emotional response during the appointment, and note any discrepancies with the patient’s stated mood. Note any observations about emotional expression made by the psychiatrist.]

Thought Process: [Describe the patient’s coherence, organisation, and flow of thoughts.]

Thought Content: [Describe the content of the patient’s thoughts, and note the presence of delusions, distortions, obsessions, or intrusive thoughts, if any.]

Perception: [Describe hallucinations or other perceptual disturbances of the patient, if any.]

Cognition: [Describe the patient’s orientation to time / place / person, attention, memory, concentration, and comprehension.]

Insight and Judgment: [Describe the patient's understanding of their mental health condition and their decision-making capacity, noting any lack of awareness or denial.]

[strengths_coping_skills_recreational_int] Strengths, Coping Skills, Recreational Interests, and Hobbies

[Provide a detailed summary of the patient’s strengths, coping skills, recreational interests, and hobbies. Write this as prose. Do not use bullet points. Describe the patient’s strengths, positive coping skills, recreational interests, hobbies, and other protective factors. Only list things that are accepted as helpful and useful in successfully managing mental health. Do not list anything that is considered harmful to mental health (e.g., consuming alcohol, other addictive behaviour).]

[If nothing is mentioned in the transcript, notes, or contextual notes about strengths, coping skills, recreational interests, and hobbies, do not include this section in the letter.]

[psychometric_testing] Psychometric Testing

[Provide any psychometric assessment results for the patient. For each psychometric test, include: the name of the assessment, a brief description of the psychometric assessment, the date of test administration, and relevant purpose to the current evaluation. Provide a summary of the quantitative results (e.g., percentiles). Provide a summary interpretation of the results. Provide any implications of results in the clinical context related to the patient's presentation. (Repeat this process for as many assessments that were administered.) If no psychometric assessments were administered, leave this section blank.]

[Detail any plans specifically mentioned by the psychiatrist to conduct any future psychometric testing.]

[formulation_impression] Formulation / Impression

[Provide a detailed bio-psycho-social psychiatric formulation, including predisposing, precipitating, perpetuating and protective factors. If applicable, also include a psychodynamic formulation of the patient's presentation. Include evidence from the transcript to enhance the details of your formulation, and refer to any psychometric testing that was carried out.]

[diagnosis] Diagnosis

[Describe the psychiatrist's diagnostic impressions and observations, based on DSM-5-TR and ICD-11 criteria. Include the diagnostic code of any such diagnoses. Describe if the psychiatrist has stated that these diagnoses are new or confirmation of diagnoses previously given. Detail any comorbidities or differential diagnosis considerations provided by the psychiatrist.]

[risk] Risk

[Make a risk assessment in a statement (e.g., suicidal or homicidal ideation, self-harm or harm to others, and risk of relapse, if any). Include any information related to past suicide attempts or past self-harming behaviour. Include any information about psychosocial stressors contributing to the level of risk. Include any information about protective factors that mitigate the risk.]

[treatment_plan_discussed_with_patient] Treatment Plan Discussed With Patient

[Specify any evidence-based recommendations for medication changes made by the psychiatrist, and specify if any existing medications should be continued or discontinued. Provide the psychiatrist's rationale for these recommendations. If the psychiatrist has not made any recommendations, leave blank.]

[Specify any suggested non-pharmacological interventions made by the psychiatrist, such as psychotherapy or lifestyle modifications. Provide the psychiatrist's rationale for these recommendations. If the psychiatrist has not made any recommendations, leave blank.]

[Specify if any referrals to other specialists or services have been or will be made by the psychiatrist (e.g., lab tests). Provide the psychiatrist's rationale for these recommendations. If the psychiatrist has not made any recommendations, leave blank.]

[Detail what the plan is for follow-up and monitoring, as detailed by the psychiatrist (e.g., next appointment details). If the psychiatrist has not made any plans for follow-up or monitoring, leave blank.]

[Detail if any Safety Plan has been made by the psychiatrist in the event that the patient poses a risk to safety, including steps to take in a crisis. If the psychiatrist has not made any Safety Plan, leave blank.]

[Summarise any education provided by the psychiatrist to the patient about their diagnosis and treatment options. Document the patient’s understanding and agreement with the plan. If the psychiatrist has not provided any education to the patient about their diagnosis and treatment options, leave blank.]

[Summarise any other recommendations made specifically to the General Practitioner about ongoing care and management for the patient.]

[summary] Summary

[Provide a paragraph summary of the appointment in no more than four sentences.]

Thank you for your ongoing care and management. Please feel free to contact me if you have any questions.

Yours sincerely,

Dr [Medical Clinician's Name.] (If the medical clinician's name is not stated, just write "Clinician Name".)

sections.label: Strengths, Coping Skills, Recreational Interests, and Hobbies

title: Psychodynamic Psychotherapy
description: For clinicians providing a psychodynamic psychotherapy approach, this template is designed to capture key themes, unconscious processes, and
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by clinicians working within a psychodynamic psychotherapy approach. Ensure the summary reflects psychodynamic principles and adheres to psychodynamic terminology. Organise the summary under the following headings.)

[presenting_concerns_and_themes] Presenting Concerns and Themes

* \[Summarise the client’s primary concerns as discussed in the session. Note any shifts in themes compared to previous sessions. Identify any repeating patterns, defenses, or unconscious conflicts emerging in the therapy. Highlight any relevant updates to symptoms, behaviours, emotional states or situations since the previous session. Detail any relevant life events or current circumstances contributing to the client’s presentation.]

[affective_experience_and_emotional_tone] Affective Experience and Emotional Tone

* \[Describe the client’s predominant emotional state throughout the session. Note moments of emotional activation, avoidance, or resistance. If discussed in the transcript, document nonverbal cues that contribute to the understanding of underlying affective experiences.]

[relational_patterns_and_transference_dyn] Relational Patterns and Transference Dynamics

* \[Describe any transference reactions observed in the session. Highlight how the client’s relational patterns are playing out in the therapeutic relationship. Note any repetitions of early relational experiences in the client’s narratives.]
* \[Briefly comment on the therapeutic alliance or rapport between therapist and patient observed in the session. Note any areas where rapport was strengthened or challenges arose.]

[countertransference_and_therapist_reflec] Countertransference and Therapist Reflections

* \[Document the therapist’s emotional and cognitive responses to the client. Identify any countertransference reactions that might provide insight into the client’s unconscious processes. Consider whether these reactions reflect broader relational themes. (only include if mentioned in the transcript, otherwise leave blank)]

[defenses_and_coping_mechanisms] Defenses and Coping Mechanisms

* \[Identify key defense mechanisms the client used during the session. e.g., repression, projection, intellectualisation. Consider how these defenses impact the client’s emotional experience and capacity for insight.]

[key_interventions_and_interpretations] Key Interventions and Interpretations

* \[Summarise the therapist’s interventions, including interpretations, clarifications, or confrontations. Highlight moments where unconscious material was explored. Document the client’s response to interpretations and any observable shifts in understanding.]

[progress_and_emerging_insights] Progress and Emerging Insights

* \[Note any evidence of psychological insight, increased affect tolerance, or shifts in self-perception. Highlight changes in relational dynamics or patterns over the course of therapy. Document any observed or reported progress toward therapy goals.]

[plan_for_future_sessions] Plan for Future Sessions

* \[Describe focus areas for upcoming sessions. Consider any emerging themes that warrant deeper exploration. Document any adjustments to the therapeutic approach based on session dynamics. If mentioned in session, list details of the next scheduled session, including time and date.]

[summary] Summary

* \[Provide a brief recap of key points from the session in no more than four sentences that summarise the session.]

title: ReturnToWorkSA Treating Psychologist Comprehensive Report
description: Psychologists working within the South Australian workers compensation scheme, also known as WorkCover or ReturntoWorkSA, may be requested by the
prompt:
(This template is designed to assist psychologists working within the South Australian workers compensation scheme, also known as WorkCover or ReturntoWorkSA, prepare a Treating Psychologist Comprehensive Report (ReturnToWorkSA fee schedule item PS810). This report provides a comprehensive clinical opinion, statement, or detailed response to questions relating to a worker's diagnosis, medical status, and treatment. It is used when the case is complex, involves co-morbidities or pre-existing conditions impacting recovery, or when a significant number of detailed questions have been asked. It is governed by the ReturnToWorkSA Psychology Fee Schedule and Policy and must reflect the five principles of the Clinical Framework for the Delivery of Health Services: (1) measure and demonstrate the effectiveness of management; (2) adopt a biopsychosocial approach; (3) empower the injured person to manage their injury; (4) implement goals focused on optimising function, participation, and return to work; (5) base management on best available research evidence. The report must be written in clear, plain English that can be understood by decision-makers who may not have a medical or allied health background. Avoid complex clinical jargon where possible, and explain technical terms in simple language. Only use information that is contained in the transcripts, session notes, contextual notes, added files, and psychometric test results. You must strictly adhere to the following format:)

[treating_psychologist_comprehensive_repo] Treating Psychologist Comprehensive Report

**Prepared under:** ReturnToWorkSA Psychology Fee Schedule, Item PS810

**Worker:** [Print the worker's first and last name. If unknown, print "Unknown."]

**Date of Birth:** [Print the worker's date of birth in the format DD/MM/YYYY. If unknown, print "Unknown."]

**ReturnToWorkSA Claim Number:** [Print the worker's ReturnToWorkSA claim number. If unknown, print "Unknown."]

**Employer:** [Print the name of the worker's employer at the time of injury. If unknown, print "Unknown."]

**Date of Injury:** [Print the date of the work-related injury in the format DD/MM/YYYY. If unknown, print "Unknown."]

**Date of Report:** [Print today's date in the format DD/MM/YYYY.]

**Report Requested By:** [Print the name and role of the party who requested this report (e.g., claims manager, self-insured employer, worker, worker's representative).]

**Questions Addressed:** [If specific questions were asked by the requestor, list each question here before answering them within the relevant sections below. If no specific questions were provided, print "No specific questions were provided. This report has been prepared as a comprehensive clinical overview."]
 
[diagnosis] Diagnosis

[Print the worker's DSM-5-TR diagnosis including the diagnostic code and severity level if applicable (e.g., Major Depressive Disorder, Moderate, 296.32; Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder, 309.81). If the worker has more than one diagnosis related to their work injury, list all relevant diagnoses. If unknown, print "Unknown."]
 
[history_of_the_work_related_psychologica] History of the Work-Related Psychological Injury

[Describe the history and mechanism of the accepted work-related psychological injury. Explain what happened at work that led to the psychological injury. Include when the injury occurred or developed, and describe the specific workplace events, circumstances, or conditions that contributed to the injury. Explain how the injury has developed or progressed over time since it first occurred. Write in plain language that a non-clinician can understand. Provide your response in less than five sentences. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, added files, or transcripts, otherwise print "Unknown.")]
 
[current_psychological_status] Current Psychological Status

[Provide a detailed description of the worker's current psychological condition and symptom presentation from a biopsychosocial perspective. Describe the worker's biological symptoms (e.g., sleep, appetite, physical manifestations), psychological symptoms (e.g., mood, cognition, trauma symptoms), and social or occupational functioning (e.g., relationships, daily activities, capacity for work). Describe any significant changes in the worker's condition since treatment commenced. Write in plain language that a non-clinician can understand. Provide your response in less than five sentences. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, added files, or transcripts, otherwise print "Unknown.")]
 
[co_morbidities_and_pre_existing_conditio] Co-morbidities and Pre-Existing Conditions

[Describe any co-morbid conditions or pre-existing psychological or physical health conditions that are relevant to the worker's current recovery. Explain how these conditions interact with or impact the accepted work-related psychological injury and the worker's recovery. Describe the extent to which these conditions are related to the work injury versus pre-existing or non-work factors. Write in plain language. Provide your response in less than four sentences. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, added files, or transcripts, otherwise print "Unknown.")]
 
[other_contributing_factors] Other Contributing Factors

[Describe any other factors beyond the accepted work-related injury that are contributing to the worker's current psychological presentation. These may include non-work-related stressors (e.g., family difficulties, financial stress, physical health problems), life circumstances, or other relevant factors. Explain how these factors interact with the work-related psychological injury. Write in plain language. Provide your response in less than four sentences. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, added files, or transcripts, otherwise print "Unknown.")]
 
[summary_of_treatment_provided] Summary of Treatment Provided

[Provide a summary of all psychological treatment provided to the worker to date. Include the therapeutic approach used (e.g., cognitive behaviour therapy, trauma-focused therapy, Eye Movement Desensitisation and Reprocessing), when treatment commenced, the total number of sessions provided, and the frequency of sessions. Describe the main focus and goals of the treatment as it relates to the worker's accepted work-related psychological injury. Describe how the treatment approach is consistent with best available research evidence for the worker's diagnosis. Provide your response in less than five sentences. If unknown, print "Unknown."]
 
[progress_in_treatment] Progress in Treatment

[Describe the worker's progress in treatment to date. Explain how the worker's symptoms and functioning have changed since treatment began. Describe improvements in the worker's ability to manage their symptoms, engage in daily activities, maintain relationships, or function at home and at work. Provide your response in less than four sentences. If unknown, print "Unknown."]
 
[scales] Outcome Measures

[List up to three psychometric assessments used to measure and demonstrate treatment outcomes. For each assessment administered, provide the name of the assessment, the initial administration date and scores, the most recent administration date and scores, and a brief interpretation of the results in plain language. Use an ordered list to display this information, just like the following example:

* **Depression Anxiety Stress Scale (DASS-21)**; Initial administration date: 15/03/2025; Initial scores: Depression 10 (Mild), Anxiety 10 (Mild), Stress 16 (Moderate); Most recent administration date: 10/01/2026; Most recent scores: Depression 5 (Normal), Anxiety 4 (Normal), Stress 7 (Normal); The change in DASS-21 scores indicates that the worker's psychological functioning has improved since treatment commenced.
* **PTSD Checklist for DSM-5 (PCL-5)**; Initial administration date: 05/06/2025; Initial score: 45 (Moderate); Most recent administration date: 10/01/2026; Most recent score: 22 (Mild); The change in PCL-5 scores indicates that the worker's trauma-related symptoms have reduced since treatment commenced.
* **Trauma Recovery Measure (TRM)**; Initial administration date: 19/08/2025; Initial scores: Total 56 (75th percentile) (Middle Stage); Most recent administration date: 10/01/2026; Most recent scores: Total 65 (89th percentile) (Late Stage); The change in TRM scores indicates the worker has progressed toward later-stage trauma recovery.


If no psychometric assessments have been administered, print "No psychometric assessments have been administered to date."]
 
[functional_capacity_and_work_status] Functional Capacity and Work Status

[Describe the worker's current functional capacity for work and their current work status. State clearly whether the worker has returned to work (full duties, modified duties, or graduated return to work), is currently unable to return to work, or is working in some limited capacity. Describe the impact of the worker's current psychological condition on their functional capacity at work and at home (e.g., concentration, communication, decision-making, self-care, social engagement). Describe what functional improvements have occurred since treatment commenced. Provide your response in less than four sentences. If unknown, print "Unknown."]
 
[self_management] Self-Management

[Describe how treatment is assisting the worker to self-manage their psychological injury. Explain what strategies, techniques, or skills the worker is now using independently to manage their symptoms (e.g., relaxation strategies, thought challenging, behavioural activation, mindfulness mediation). Describe the worker's current level of understanding of their condition and their engagement with self-management strategies. Describe a timeframe for when the clinician anticipates the worker will be able to self-manage their condition without ongoing professional support. Provide your response in less than four sentences. If unknown, print "Unknown."]
 
[future_treatment] Future Treatment

**Planned Interventions:** [Describe the planned psychological interventions for future treatment. Specify the therapeutic approach that will be used and explain what this will involve in plain language. Describe the focus of future treatment and how it will address the worker's ongoing needs related to their work-related psychological injury. Provide your response in less than three sentences. If unknown, print "Unknown."]

**Treatment Goals:** [Outline the functional goals for future treatment using the SMART framework (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Timed). Goals should focus on what the worker will be able to do, and should relate to functioning in daily life, capacity for work, and return to work outcomes. If unknown, print "Unknown."]

**Number and Frequency of Sessions:** [Specify the recommended number of remaining treatment sessions and the frequency of sessions (e.g., 8 sessions, fortnightly; 12 sessions, weekly). If unknown, print "Unknown."]

**Expected Duration:** [Provide the expected timeframe for the remainder of the rehabilitation plan (e.g., 3 months, 6 months). Include an anticipated end date for treatment if discussed. If unknown, print "Unknown."]

**Return to Work Alignment:** [Describe how the planned treatment and functional goals will support the worker's return to work. Explain how achieving these goals will assist the worker in returning to work, managing workplace demands, or successfully reintegrating into the workplace. Provide a prognosis for return to work, including a timeframe where possible. Provide your response in less than three sentences. If unknown, print "Unknown."]

**Anticipated Self-Management:** [Describe when the clinician anticipates the worker will be able to self-manage their condition without ongoing professional support. Provide a specific timeframe if discussed (e.g., "within 4–6 months", "by mid-2026"). If unknown, print "Unknown."]
 
[barriers_to_recovery_and_return_to_work] Barriers to Recovery and Return to Work

[Identify any barriers to the worker's recovery and/or return to work. These barriers may include psychological factors (e.g., ongoing symptoms, fear of returning to work), practical factors (e.g., lack of suitable duties, unresolved workplace issues), psychosocial risk factors, workplace relationship difficulties, or other relevant factors. Provide your response in less than four sentences. If unknown, print "Unknown."]
 
[nextsteps] RECOMMENDATIONS

[ongoing_psychological_treatment] Ongoing Psychological Treatment

[State clearly whether ongoing psychological treatment is recommended. If yes, specify the type of treatment, recommended frequency (e.g., fortnightly), and the recommended number of sessions or timeframe. Provide a clear clinical rationale for ongoing treatment in plain language, including how it will support the worker's recovery and return to work in accordance with the Clinical Framework for the Delivery of Health Services. If no further treatment is recommended, state this clearly and explain why. If anything recommended here is addressing any barrier listed above, state this explicitly, and explain how it’s addressing the barrier. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, added files, or transcripts, otherwise print "Unknown.")]

[return_to_work] Return to Work

[Provide specific, practical recommendations to support the worker's return to work. Include any recommended workplace modifications, graduated return to work plans, suitable duties, or other strategies to facilitate a safe and durable return to work. Provide timeframes where applicable. If anything recommended here is addressing any barrier listed above, state this explicitly, and explain how it’s addressing the barrier. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, added files, or transcripts, otherwise print "Unknown.")]

[other_recommendations] Other Recommendations

[If applicable, provide any other recommendations not already mentioned that would support the worker's recovery and return to work. This may include referrals to other treating practitioners, liaison with the claims manager or employer's return to work coordinator, case conferencing, or other supports. If anything recommended here is addressing any barrier listed above, state this explicitly, and explain how it’s addressing the barrier. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, added files, or transcripts, otherwise leave this section blank.)]
 
[responses_to_specific_questions] Responses to Specific Questions

[If the requestor provided specific questions at the outset of this report, provide a clear, direct response to each question here. Number each response to correspond with the questions listed at the beginning of this report. Write responses in plain language. If all questions have already been fully addressed within the body of the report above, print "All questions raised by the requestor have been addressed within the body of this report above." If no specific questions were provided, leave this section blank and do not print the sub-heading.]
 
If you require any further information, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Yours sincerely,

[Psychologist first and last name]

[AHPRA Registration Number]

[Practice Name]

Barriers to Recovery and Return to Work

title: ReturnToWorkSA Treating Psychologist Summary Report
description: Psychologists working within the South Australian workers compensation scheme, also known as WorkCover or ReturntoWorkSA, may be requested by the
prompt:
(This template is designed to assist psychologists working within the South Australian workers compensation scheme, also known as WorkCover or ReturntoWorkSA, prepare a Treating Psychologist Summary Report (ReturnToWorkSA fee schedule item PS820). This report provides a brief clinical opinion, statement, or response to a limited number of questions relating to a worker's diagnosis, medical status, and treatment. It is governed by the ReturnToWorkSA Psychology Fee Schedule and Policy and must reflect the five principles of the Clinical Framework for the Delivery of Health Services: (1) measure and demonstrate the effectiveness of management; (2) adopt a biopsychosocial approach; (3) empower the injured person to manage their injury; (4) implement goals focused on optimising function, participation, and return to work; (5) base management on best available research evidence. The report must be written in clear, plain English that can be understood by decision-makers who may not have a medical or allied health background. Avoid complex clinical jargon where possible, and explain technical terms in simple language. Only use information that is contained in the transcripts, session notes, contextual notes, added files, and psychometric test results. You must strictly adhere to the following format:) [treating_psychologist_summary_report] Treating Psychologist Summary Report **Prepared under:** ReturnToWorkSA Psychology Fee Schedule, Item PS820 **Worker:** [Print the worker's first and last name. If unknown, print "Unknown."] **Date of Birth:** [Print the worker's date of birth in the format DD/MM/YYYY. If unknown, print "Unknown."] **ReturnToWorkSA Claim Number:** [Print the worker's ReturnToWorkSA claim number. If unknown, print "Unknown."] **Employer:** [Print the name of the worker's employer at the time of injury. If unknown, print "Unknown."] **Date of Report:** [Print today's date in the format DD/MM/YYYY.] **Report Requested By:** [Print the name of the party who requested this report (e.g., claims manager, self-insured employer, worker, worker's representative). If self-initiated by the treating psychologist, print "Self-initiated by treating psychologist following the 6th consultation, in accordance with ReturnToWorkSA Psychology Fee Schedule Item PS820."] [diagnosis] Diagnosis [Print the worker's DSM-5-TR diagnosis including the diagnostic code and severity level if applicable (e.g., Major Depressive Disorder, Moderate, 296.32). If the worker has more than one diagnosis related to their work injury, list all relevant diagnoses. If unknown, print "Unknown."]   [current_medical_and_psychological_status] Current Medical and Psychological Status [Describe the worker's current psychological condition and symptom presentation. Explain how the worker is currently functioning in daily life and at work. Describe any significant changes in the worker's condition since treatment commenced. Write in plain language that a non-clinician can understand. Provide your response in less than three sentences. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, added files, or transcripts, otherwise print "Unknown.")]   [treatment_provided] Treatment Provided [Briefly describe the psychological treatment being delivered. Name the therapeutic approach in plain language (e.g., cognitive behaviour therapy, trauma-focused therapy) and explain in simple terms what this involves. Include the total number of sessions provided to date, the frequency of sessions, and the date treatment commenced. Describe the main focus of treatment as it relates to the worker's accepted work-related psychological injury. Provide your response in less than three sentences. If unknown, print "Unknown."]   [scales] Outcome Measures [List up to three psychometric assessments used to measure and demonstrate treatment outcomes. For each assessment administered, provide the name of the assessment, the initial administration date and scores, the most recent administration date and scores, and a brief interpretation of the results in plain language. Use an ordered list to display this information, just like the following example:
* **Depression Anxiety Stress Scale (DASS-21)**; Initial administration date: 15/03/2025; Initial scores: Depression 10 (Mild), Anxiety 10 (Mild), Stress 16 (Moderate); Most recent administration date: 10/01/2026; Most recent scores: Depression 5 (Normal), Anxiety 4 (Normal), Stress 7 (Normal); The change in DASS-21 scores indicates that the worker's psychological functioning has improved since treatment commenced. * **PTSD Checklist for DSM-5 (PCL-5)**; Initial administration date: 05/06/2025; Initial score: 45 (Moderate); Most recent administration date: 10/01/2026; Most recent score: 22 (Mild); The change in PCL-5 scores indicates that the worker's trauma-related symptoms have reduced since treatment commenced. If no psychometric assessments have been administered, print "No psychometric assessments have been administered to date."] [functional_capacity_and_return_to_work] Functional Capacity and Return to Work [Describe the worker's current functional capacity for work. State clearly whether the worker has capacity to return to work (full duties, modified duties, or graduated return to work), is currently unable to return to work, or is already working in some capacity. If the worker has return to work capacity, describe what type of work they can perform and any restrictions or modifications required. If the worker does not currently have return to work capacity, briefly explain what changes are needed before this would be possible. Focus on what the worker CAN do. Provide your response in less than three sentences. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, added files, or transcripts, otherwise print "Unknown.")]   [barriers_to_recovery_and_return_to_work] Barriers to Recovery and Return to Work [Outline the key barriers — psychological, practical, or workplace-related — that are currently impacting the worker's recovery and/or return to work. Provide brief, solution-focused recommendations for addressing these barriers, describing actions that could be taken by the worker, the treating team, the employer, or the claims manager. Provide your response in less than three sentences. If unknown, print "Unknown."]   [nextsteps] RECOMMENDATIONS [ongoing_psychological_treatment] Ongoing Psychological Treatment [State clearly whether ongoing psychological treatment is recommended. If yes, specify the type of treatment, recommended frequency (e.g., fortnightly), and approximate number of remaining sessions or anticipated timeframe. Briefly explain the rationale for ongoing treatment in plain language. If no further treatment is recommended, state this clearly and explain why. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, added files, or transcripts, otherwise print "Unknown.")] [return_to_work] Return to Work [Provide specific, practical recommendations to support the worker's return to work. Include any recommended workplace modifications, graduated return to work plans, or suitable duties. Provide timeframes where applicable. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, added files, or transcripts, otherwise print "Unknown.")] [other_recommendations] Other Recommendations [If applicable, provide any other recommendations not already mentioned that would support the worker's recovery and return to work. This may include referrals to other treating practitioners, liaison with the claims manager, or other supports. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the session notes, contextual notes, added files, or transcripts, otherwise leave this section blank and do not print the sub-heading.)]   [responses_to_specific_questions] Responses to Specific Questions [If the requestor of this report provided specific questions for the psychologist, provide a clear, direct response to each question here after first printing the question. Number each each question. Write responses in plain language. Keep responses brief and focused, consistent with the nature of a summary report. If any questions have already been addressed within the body of the report, indicate the relevant sections in which the answers can be found. If no specific questions were provided, leave this section blank and do not print the sub-heading.]   If you require any further information, please do not hesitate to contact me. Yours sincerely, [Psychologist first and last name] [AHPRA Registration Number] [Practice Name]

title: Risk Assessment
description: For use by a wide variety of clinicians, this template provides a risk assessment summary statement based on information from a session with a client
prompt:
(The template below is designed to be used by clinicians in a mental health setting. After analysing the transcript, you will generate a risk assessment summary on a client. Use language that is consistent with accepted standards of evidence-based psychiatry and medicine. The template will use the following format:)



[risk] Risk Assessment

\[Make a detailed risk assessment in a paragraph of no more than six sentences (e.g., suicidal or homicidal ideation, self-harm or harm to others, and risk of relapse, if any). Include any information related to past suicide attempts or past self-harming behaviour. Include any information about psychosocial stressors contributing to the level of risk. Describe any risk for social isolation. Describe any risk for being the victim or perpetrator of bullying, or of domestic violence, or of any other criminal or harmful behaviour. Describe any protective factors that mitigate the risk.]

title: Schema Therapy
description: Structured around the schema therapy framework, this session summary includes key schema therapy concepts such as maladaptive schemas, modes and
prompt:
(Use the template below to generate a client session summary written in the voice of a Schema Therapist. Ensure the language and concepts are consistent with Schema Therapy principles. Use Schema Therapy terminology throughout, drawing on concepts of modes, schemas, coping styles, unmet emotional needs, and the therapeutic relationship. Avoid vague summaries; instead, aim for clinical depth: refer to the session content to describe what specific experiences were discussed, and how they specifically link to and impact on the client’s current functioning and schema-driven patterns. The summary should be concise, focusing on the most clinically relevant information. Structure the summary using the headings provided below.)

[presentation] Presenting Concerns

* [Summarise the client's main concerns or difficulties discussed in the session in schema therapy terms. Highlight any relevant updates to symptoms, behaviours, emotional states or situations since the previous session. Detail any relevant life events or current circumstances contributing to the client’s presentation. Detail the client’s current emotional state.]

[schema_conceptualisation] Schema Conceptualisation

* [Describe the key Early Maladaptive Schemas and modes that were most prominent or actively worked with in the session. Discuss how they manifested together and influenced the client's experience. Include specific examples of how these patterns showed up in the client's current life or in the therapeutic relationship.]
* Schema patterns explored: [Describe the main schema-driven patterns that emerged and were addressed in the session] (only include if relevant to the session, otherwise leave blank)
* Mode dynamics: [Detail which modes were activated, what triggered them, and how the client experienced and responded while in these modes. Include any mode dialogues or transitions between modes observed] (only include if relevant to the session, otherwise leave blank)
* Early experiences connected: [Describe any childhood experiences or memories discussed and how they relate to current difficulties, using the client's language where appropriate] (only include if explored in the session, otherwise leave blank)
* Unmet emotional needs: [Identify specific core emotional needs that were discussed or became apparent through the session work] (only include if identified during the session, otherwise leave blank)

[therapeutic_interventions] Therapeutic Interventions

* [Detail each therapy technique used, including schema therapy interventions like imagery rescripting, floatback, chair work, limited reparenting, empathic confrontation, mode dialogues and interviews, cognitive or behavioural interventions, schema feedback from the YSQ, or psychoeducation. For each intervention, specify: 1) the rationale for using it, 2) what specific schema/mode/pattern it targeted, 3) how it was implemented, and 4) the client's response. Include specific details about what was explored, the client's emotional response, and any breakthroughs or resistance encountered.] (use as many bullet points as needed to capture all therapeutic interventions)
* [Client response and insights: Summarise key client responses, any insights gained from experiential techniques, and shifts in awareness or understanding that occurred during the interventions]

[key_insights_themes_and_progress] Key Insights, Themes and Progress

* [Identify specific themes, insights, or realisations expressed by the client during the session. Include any shifts in schema-driven beliefs, or increased awareness of schema-driven behaviours. Include any shifts in the client’s understanding of their difficulties or progress toward therapy goals. Document any strengthening of Healthy Adult mode, positive changes in coping strategies, emotional regulation improvements, or progress towards therapy goals.]

[therapeutic_relationship] Therapeutic Relationship

* [Briefly comment on the therapeutic alliance or dynamics observed in the session. Note any areas where rapport was strengthened or challenges arose.]

[homework_or_between_session_task] Homework or Between-Session Task

* [List any assigned homework, such as schema therapy homework like schema journaling, schema flashcards, imagery practice, pattern breaking or behavioural experiments. Note any barriers or motivation expressed by the client regarding homework.] (Only include if mentioned in the transcript otherwise exclude section.)

[risk] Risk Assessment

* [Summarise any risk factors, such as self-harm or suicidal ideation. List protective factors and any action plans if risk is present.] (Only include if mentioned in the transcript.)

[nextsteps] Next Steps

* [Outline the preliminary agenda or goals for the next session, based on today's discussion. Provide the time, day and date of any future session agreed upon between the therapist and the client.]

[summary] Summary

* [Provide a brief recap of key points from the session in no more than four sentences that summarise the session.]

title: School Recommendations Report
description: Designed for school counsellors and psychologists working with students experiencing behavioural, academic, or social-emotional challenges at school.
prompt:
(Generate a comprehensive school recommendations report by a school counsellor or school / education psychologist. All content must be derived exclusively from information explicitly mentioned in transcripts, contextual notes, clinical notes, or other documents provided. Do not generate recommendations, observations, or content beyond what has been stated by the school counsellor / psychologist or contained in supporting materials. Structure the report according to the section headings below. If the school counsellor / psychologist has specified a particular purpose for this report, ensure the entire report is oriented toward that stated purpose.) **CONFIDENTIAL** [Start on new line:] #### School Recommendations Report [Start on new line:] **Student Name:** [Print the student's first and last name.] [Start on new line:] **Date of Birth:** [Print the student's date of birth in Day Month, Year format e.g., 15 March, 2014. If unknown, print: "Unknown."] [Start on new line:] **School:** [Print the name of the school the student currently attends. If unknown, print: "Unknown."] [Start on new line:] **Year / Grade Level:** [Print the student's current year level e.g., Year 5. If unknown, print: "Unknown."] [Start on new line:] **Report Prepared By:** [Print the first and last name of the school counsellor / psychologist preparing this report, and their professional title e.g., Ms Sarah Chen, School Counsellor.] [Start on new line:] **Report Date:** [Print the date of this report in Day Month, Year format e.g., 05 December, 2025.] [Start on new line:] **Documents Reviewed:** * [Provide a list of documents reviewed and referenced in this report, including any added files that are medical reports, psychological reports, other health-related reports, previous school reports, Individual Education Plans, behaviour support plans, or other relevant documentation. Include the date of each document and the author/author's title where known. List in chronological order from oldest to most recent. Do not include session notes here. Do not include psychometric assessments here. (Psychometric assessments should be listed in the "Previous Assessments and Interventions" section below.) (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the added files, transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)] **Reason for Report** [In a single paragraph, describe why this report has been requested and what it aims to achieve. If known, explain who requested the report and the specific concerns or needs that prompted its preparation. Include relevant background about any decisions, applications, or processes this report will support (e.g., funding applications, behaviour intervention planning, educational adjustments). If no specific purpose has been stated, print: "To provide recommendations for supporting [student's first name]'s educational needs."] #### Background Information **Family Context** [In a single paragraph, provide relevant information about the student's family circumstances, including family composition, parental separation or relationship changes, siblings, primary caregivers, living arrangements, or significant family events that may impact the student's functioning at school. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the added files, transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] **Developmental and Medical History** [In a single paragraph, summarise relevant developmental milestones, early childhood development, pregnancy or birth complications, medical conditions, diagnoses (including when diagnosed and by whom), current and previous medications, and ongoing medical care or interventions. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the added files, transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] **Mental Health and Psychological History** [In a single paragraph, describe any mental health diagnoses, psychological assessments, counselling or therapeutic interventions (past and current), psychiatric care, or mental health concerns. Include information about when diagnoses were made and by whom, effectiveness of previous interventions, and current mental health support services. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the added files, transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] **Educational History** [In a single paragraph, outline the student's educational journey, including previous schools attended, year levels repeated or accelerated, school transitions, attendance patterns, suspension or exclusion history, previous Individual Education Plans or behaviour support plans, and any significant educational disruptions. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the added files, transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] **Strengths and Hobbies** [In a single paragraph of less than three sentences, outline the student's strengths, interests, and hobbies. Include any mentioned desirable personal qualities. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the added files, transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.) #### Previous Assessments and Interventions **Assessment Results** [For each assessment that has been completed, create a subsection with an appropriate subheading identifying the assessment name and date. For cognitive and academic assessments, provide the full scale IQ score if available, as this information is often required for funding applications in Australian schools. For other psychometric, educational, speech-language, occupational therapy, or medical assessments, provide a concise summary of key findings relevant to the student's school functioning and the purpose of this report. Focus on results that directly inform the recommendations section. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the added files, transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] **Previous Interventions** [In a single paragraph, describe previous interventions, support programs, behaviour plans, educational adjustments, therapeutic services, or other strategies that have been implemented to support the student. Include information about the duration of interventions, implementation fidelity, effectiveness or outcomes, and reasons for discontinuation if applicable. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the added files, transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.] #### Current Functioning and Support Needs **Academic Functioning and Concerns** [In a single paragraph, describe the student's current academic performance across subject areas, learning strengths and difficulties, literacy and numeracy levels, progress toward year level expectations, and engagement with learning tasks. Include any curriculum modifications or adjustments currently in place. Identify specific academic concerns that are impacting the student's ability to access the curriculum or make expected progress, including the severity and frequency of these concerns. Explain how these academic challenges affect the student's educational outcomes and ability to participate in learning on the same basis as their peers. Include information from recent school reports, teacher observations, or assessment data. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the added files, transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.)] **Social Functioning and Concerns** [In a single paragraph, describe the student's peer relationships, friendship patterns, social skills, ability to work cooperatively with others, and participation in group activities. Include observations about social isolation or rejection, conflict with peers, and interactions during unstructured times such as recess and lunch. Identify specific social concerns that are impacting the student's wellbeing or ability to engage successfully in the school environment. Explain how these social challenges affect the student's educational experience, peer learning opportunities, and overall school participation. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the added files, transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print sub-heading.)] **Behavioural Functioning and Concerns** [In a single paragraph, describe the student's behavioural patterns at school, including frequency and nature of behavioural incidents, triggers for challenging behaviours, responses to behavioural interventions, classroom behaviour, behaviour during transitions, compliance with school rules and teacher instructions, and self-regulation abilities. Identify the primary behavioural concerns that are impacting the student's learning, safety, or ability to function successfully in the school environment. Describe any patterns or contexts in which behaviours occur and explain how these behavioural challenges affect the student's educational access, relationships with peers and teachers, and overall school participation. Include perspectives from teachers, parents/caregivers, the student (where appropriate), and other relevant stakeholders. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the added files, transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank.)] **Attendance, Engagement and Concerns** [In a single paragraph, describe the student's attendance record, patterns of absence or lateness, school refusal or avoidance, engagement with school activities and curriculum, participation in extracurricular activities, and overall connection to the school community. Identify any concerns related to attendance or engagement that are impacting the student's learning progress or school participation. Explain how attendance or engagement issues affect the student's ability to benefit from education and maintain continuity in learning. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the added files, transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print the sub-heading.] **Current Supports in Place** [In a single paragraph, describe all supports, adjustments, interventions, or accommodations currently being provided to the student, including educational adjustments, behaviour support strategies, modified curriculum, teacher aide support, environmental modifications, assistive technology, or external support services. (Only include if explicitly mentioned in the added files, transcript, contextual notes, or clinical note, otherwise leave blank and do not print sub-heading.)] **Identified Gaps in Current Support** [In a single paragraph, describe what additional support, resources, or interventions the student requires that are not currently available or adequately addressed. Explain why current supports are insufficient and what specific needs remain unmet. Clearly link these gaps to the student's ability to access the curriculum, participate in learning activities, engage with peers, develop skills, make academic progress, and benefit from education on the same basis as their peers.
